ndc.q: In OpenVPN, the option _______ is used to enable compression on the VPN Link.
opts: comp-rar
comp-zip
comp-lzo
comp-7z
ndc.expl: In OpenVPN, the 'comp-lzo' option enables LZO compression on the VPN link to reduce bandwidth usage. comp-lzo is the only valid compression option in OpenVPN from the given choices.
ndc.takeaway: OpenVPN compression option = comp-lzo (LZO compression on VPN link)

ndc.q: The enterprise administrator has seen an increase in the number of ping flood attacks on the web server in the DMZ (Demilitarized Zone). How can the administrator mitigate the attacks and prevent the web server from crashing?
opts: Block ICMP Echo 8 packets on the external firewall
Block TCP 53 packets on the external firewall
Block Telnet 23 packets on the external firewall
Block ICMP Echo 15 packets on the external firewall
ndc.expl: ICMP Echo Request (Type 8) packets are used in ping flood attacks. Blocking ICMP Echo Type 8 on the external firewall stops the ping flood from reaching the DMZ web server.
ndc.takeaway: Block ICMP Echo Type 8 on external firewall to mitigate ping flood DDoS attacks

ndc.q: Assault on system security that derives from an intelligent threat.
opts: Exploit
Attack
ToE
Vulnerability
ndc.expl: An Attack is an assault on system security that derives from an intelligent threat — a deliberate attempt by a person or organization to compromise a system. A Vulnerability is a weakness; an Exploit is code that takes advantage of it.
ndc.takeaway: Attack = Deliberate assault on system security from intelligent threat

ndc.q: Which of the following best describes an attack that altered the contents of two critical files?
opts: Integrity
Confidentiality
Availability
Authentication
ndc.expl: Modifying file contents attacks Integrity — the assurance that data has not been tampered with by unauthorized parties. It is the 'I' in the CIA triad.
ndc.takeaway: Altering file contents = Integrity attack (CIA triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)

ndc.q: Port # 137 is related with:
opts: RPC Endpoint mapping
NETBIOS Name Service
NETBIOS datagram service
SMB
ndc.expl: Netfilter is a kernel space module (framework) built into the Linux kernel for network packet processing. iptables is the user space tool that configures Netfilter rules.
ndc.takeaway: Netfilter = Kernel space framework; iptables = User space configuration tool

ndc.q: In iptables, the RAW table consists of ________ built-in chains:
opts: Two
Three
Four
Five
ndc.expl: The RAW table in iptables has 2 built-in chains: PREROUTING and OUTPUT. It is used to configure exemptions from connection tracking.
ndc.takeaway: RAW table = 2 chains (PREROUTING and OUTPUT); used for connection tracking exemptions

ndc.q: On a linux machine, all of the connection tracking is done by special framework within the kernel called_____________.
opts: filtertrack
statetrack
ctrack
conntrack
ndc.expl: Conntrack (connection tracking) is the Linux kernel framework within Netfilter that tracks all network connections. It maintains state tables used by stateful firewall rules.
ndc.takeaway: conntrack = Linux kernel connection tracking framework within Netfilter

ndc.q: In iptables, the MANGLE table consists of ________ built-in chains:
opts: Two
Three
Four
Five
ndc.expl: The Mangle table has 5 built-in chains: PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, and POSTROUTING. It is used to modify packet headers (TTL, TOS, QoS bits).
ndc.takeaway: Mangle table = 5 chains (PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING)

ndc.q: In iptables, the __________ table is responsible for the alteration of quality of service bits in the TCP header
opts: FILTER
NAT
MANGLE
RAW
ndc.expl: The MANGLE table is responsible for modifying packet headers, including QoS (Quality of Service) bits like TOS (Type of Service) and DSCP in IP/TCP headers.
ndc.takeaway: MANGLE table = Alters QoS bits (TOS/DSCP) in packet headers

ndc.q: In iptables, the POSTROUTING chain in the NAT table is used for __________
opts: DNAT
SNAT
Switching
Bridging
ndc.expl: POSTROUTING in the NAT table is used for SNAT (Source NAT) — modifying the source IP address of packets LEAVING the system (after routing decisions).
ndc.takeaway: NAT POSTROUTING = SNAT (modify source IP of outgoing packets)

ndc.q: In iptables, the PREROUTING chain in the NAT table is used for __________
opts: DNAT
SNAT
Switching
Bridging
ndc.expl: PREROUTING in the NAT table is used for DNAT (Destination NAT) — modifying the destination IP/port of packets ARRIVING before routing decisions are made.
ndc.takeaway: NAT PREROUTING = DNAT (modify destination IP of incoming packets)

ndc.q: A _________ allows a host on the “outside” to connect to a host on the “inside”.
opts: DNAT
SNAT
Switching
Bridging
ndc.expl: DNAT (Destination NAT) allows external hosts to initiate connections to internal hosts by translating the destination IP/port. Used for port forwarding and DMZ hosting.
ndc.takeaway: DNAT = Allows outside-to-inside access (port forwarding to internal hosts)

ndc.q: _________ allows a host on the “inside” of the NAT, in an RFC 1918 IP address space, to initiate a connection to a host on the “outside” of the NAT
opts: DNAT
SNAT
Switching
Bridging
ndc.expl: SNAT allows hosts with private RFC 1918 addresses to communicate with the internet by translating their source IP to a public routable IP address.
ndc.takeaway: SNAT = RFC 1918 private hosts reach internet via public IP translation

ndc.q: In a _________, the destination IP address is maintained and the source IP address is changed.
opts: DNAT
SNAT
Switching
Bridging
ndc.expl: SNAT changes the source IP while keeping the destination IP unchanged. Used in the POSTROUTING chain to allow multiple internal hosts to share one public IP.
ndc.takeaway: SNAT = Source IP changed, destination maintained

ndc.q: Netfilter is a ______________Module
opts: userspace
KernelSpace
applicationspace
linkstate
ndc.expl: Netfilter is a kernel space module (framework) built into the Linux kernel for network packet processing. iptables is the user space tool that configures Netfilter rules.
ndc.takeaway: Netfilter = Kernel space framework; iptables = User space configuration tool

ndc.q: The Linux kernel’s network packet processing subsystem is called ________________
opts: iptables
netfilter
chroot
nattable
ndc.expl: Netfilter is the Linux kernel's network packet processing subsystem/framework. It provides hooks into the network stack for packet filtering, NAT, and connection tracking.
ndc.takeaway: Netfilter = Linux kernel's network packet processing subsystem

ndc.q: A stateful inspection firewall maintains a _________ table that is constantly updated based on the full inspection of communication flow through the firewall.
opts: NAT
state
routing
connection
ndc.expl: A stateful inspection (SPI) firewall maintains a state table tracking all active connections. It verifies that incoming packets are part of established, legitimate connections.
ndc.takeaway: SPI Firewall = Maintains state table tracking all active connections

ndc.q: In an SPI firewall, the inspection engine operates at the __________ layer.
opts: applicaiton
session
network
datalink
ndc.expl: SPI (Stateful Packet Inspection) firewalls operate at the Session Layer (Layer 5) of the OSI model, tracking full connection state across the session.
ndc.takeaway: SPI firewall = Operates at Session Layer (Layer 5) of OSI

ndc.q: To see the TCP ephemeral port range in Windows:
opts: netshow eth0 ipv4 show dynamicport tcp
netshow int ipv4 show dynamicport tcp
netsh eth0 ipv4 show dynamicport tcp
netsh int ipv4 show dynamicport tcp
ndc.expl: The correct Windows command is 'netsh int ipv4 show dynamicport tcp'. 'netsh' is the network shell command; 'int' specifies interface; 'ipv4 show dynamicport tcp' displays the ephemeral port range.
ndc.takeaway: Windows ephemeral port: netsh int ipv4 show dynamicport tcp

ndc.q: To see the ephemeral port range in Linux:
opts: show eport
cat /etc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range
show lport
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range
ndc.expl: The Linux ephemeral port range is stored in /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range. Reading this file shows the range of ports used for outbound connections.
ndc.takeaway: Linux ephemeral ports: cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range

ndc.q: A ______________ is also known as sacrificed host.
opts: Gateway
Proxy
Bastion Host
Firewall
ndc.expl: A Bastion Host is also known as a 'sacrificial host' because it is intentionally exposed to the internet and expected to come under attack. It is hardened to withstand attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Bastion Host = 'Sacrificed host' — hardened, internet-exposed server

ndc.q: A ______________ is a system that has been hardened to resist attack, and which is installed on a network in such a way that it is expected to potentially come under attack.
opts: Gateway
Proxy
Bastion Host
Firewall
ndc.expl: A Bastion Host is specifically hardened and positioned to face the internet, accepting potential attacks. It serves as the entry point for certain external services.
ndc.takeaway: Bastion Host = Hardened system expected to face attacks from internet

ndc.q: A _____________combines a packet-filtering router with an application gateway located on the protected subnet side of the router.
opts: Screened host firewall
Intrusion Detection Systems
Intrusion Prevention Systems
Anti Malware System
ndc.expl: A Screened Host Firewall combines a packet-filtering router with an application-level gateway (proxy), providing two layers of security: IP filtering + application inspection.
ndc.takeaway: Screened Host Firewall = Packet-filter router + Application gateway (two-layer security)

ndc.q: Application proxy runs at layer _______
ndc.expl: Application proxy firewalls operate at Layer 7 (Application Layer) of the OSI model. They understand application protocols (HTTP, FTP, SMTP) and can inspect full content.
ndc.takeaway: Application proxy = Layer 7 (Application Layer); understands app protocols

ndc.q: Which of the following command is used to view and modify the ACL of a file in Windows?
opts: attrib
winacl
cacls
setacl
ndc.expl: cacls (Change Access Control Lists) is the Windows command for viewing and modifying NTFS file/folder ACLs. icacls is its modern replacement. attrib manages file attributes, not ACLs.
ndc.takeaway: cacls = View/modify Windows file ACLs (icacls is modern replacement)

ndc.q: An ____________ is a table/database that tells an operating system which access rights each user has to a particular system object, such as a file directory or individual file.
opts: DMZ
ACL
ACE
TLS
ndc.expl: An ACL (Access Control List) is the table/database specifying which users/groups have what permissions on system objects like files and directories.
ndc.takeaway: ACL = Table defining user/group access rights to system objects

ndc.q: ___________ is a sub-net located neither inside the internal network nor outside as part of the internet, where access is controlled, but not prevented
opts: Zombie
Host
DMZ
Firewall
ndc.expl: A DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) is a subnet between the internal network and the internet where access is controlled. It hosts public services while isolating them from the internal network.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ = Subnet between internal network and internet; controlled access zone

ndc.q: A ____________ is a device or set of devices configured to permit, deny, encrypt, decrypt, or proxy all (in and out) computer traffic between different security domains based upon a set of rules and other criteria.
opts: Zombie
Host
Stub
Firewall
ndc.expl: A Firewall is the device that permits, denies, encrypts, decrypts, or proxies traffic between security domains based on configured rules and policy.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall = Permits/denies/encrypts/proxies traffic between security domains based on rules

ndc.q: A _________________ is a computer connected to the Internet that has been compromised by a hacker, computer virus or trojan horse and can be used to perform malicious tasks of one sort or another under remote direction.
opts: Zombie
Host
Stub
Fork
ndc.expl: A Zombie computer is one compromised and remotely controlled by an attacker. Zombies form botnets used for DDoS attacks, spam, and other malicious activities.
ndc.takeaway: Zombie = Compromised computer under remote attacker control; used in botnets

ndc.q: Cold boot attack is an attack on ____________
opts: Cryptographic Systems
FTP Servers
HTTP Servers
Authentication Systems
ndc.expl: A Cold Boot Attack targets cryptographic keys stored in volatile memory (RAM). After a sudden reboot, residual data remains briefly readable, allowing key extraction before it disappears.
ndc.takeaway: Cold Boot Attack = Targets RAM to extract cryptographic keys after sudden reboot

ndc.q: The act of locating and possibly exploiting connections to wireless LANs while driving around a city or elsewhere is known as:
opts: Phishing
War Driving
Vishing
Dumpster Diving
ndc.expl: War Driving is the practice of driving around while scanning for open or vulnerable wireless networks. Tools like NetStumbler or Kismet are used.
ndc.takeaway: War Driving = Scanning for wireless networks while driving

ndc.q: Practice of sifting through commercial or residential trash to find items that have been discarded by their owners, but which may be useful to the attacker is known as:
opts: Phishing
Smishing
Vishing
Dumpster Diving
ndc.expl: Dumpster Diving is searching through trash for discarded information (documents, hardware, credentials) that attackers can use for reconnaissance or intrusion.
ndc.takeaway: Dumpster Diving = Mining trash for useful information/credentials

ndc.q: ___________ is the criminal practice of using social engineering and Voice over IP (VoIP) to gain access to private personal and financial information from the public for the purpose of financial reward.
opts: Phishing
Smishing
Vishing
All of the above
ndc.expl: Vishing (Voice Phishing) uses VoIP to impersonate legitimate organizations and trick victims into revealing personal/financial information over the phone.
ndc.takeaway: Vishing = Voice phishing using VoIP to steal personal/financial information

ndc.q: The additional element used in a DRDoS attack is known as ________
opts: Refractors
Reflectors
Sniffers
Analyzers
ndc.expl: DRDoS (Distributed Reflection Denial of Service) uses Reflectors — legitimate third-party servers that unknowingly amplify attack traffic by responding to spoofed requests.
ndc.takeaway: DRDoS Reflectors = Third-party servers used to amplify/reflect attack traffic

ndc.q: Flooding the bandwidth of the victim's network so that he cannot use the internet or other services is an example of ____________
opts: Buffer Overflow
Spoofing
Sniffing
DoS
ndc.expl: Flooding a victim's network bandwidth to prevent internet access is a Denial of Service (DoS) attack. If distributed, it's DDoS.
ndc.takeaway: Bandwidth flooding = DoS (Denial of Service) attack

ndc.q: SSL is replaced by ________
opts: HTTPS
SSH
TLS
IPv6
ndc.expl: TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the successor to SSL (Secure Sockets Layer). TLS 1.3 is the current version. SSL is deprecated due to security vulnerabilities.
ndc.takeaway: SSL is replaced by TLS (Transport Layer Security)

ndc.q: Any potential danger to information or systems is known as
opts: Vulnerability
Confidentiality
Threat
Availability
ndc.expl: A Threat is any potential danger to information or systems. A Vulnerability is a weakness that can be exploited. An Exploit is code that takes advantage of a vulnerability.
ndc.takeaway: Threat = Any potential danger to information/systems

ndc.q: Weakness in a mechanism that can threaten the Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability of an asset is known as _______
opts: Vulnerability
Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
ndc.expl: A Vulnerability is a weakness in a system, software, or process that threatens the CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) triad when exploited.
ndc.takeaway: Vulnerability = Weakness threatening CIA triad when exploited

ndc.q: The concept of protecting data from modification or deletion by unauthorized parties, and ensuring that when authorized people make changes, that shouldn’t have been made, the damage can be undone is known as ______
opts: Authentication
Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
ndc.expl: Integrity protects data from unauthorized modification or deletion and ensures authorized changes can be tracked and reversed. It is the 'I' in the CIA triad.
ndc.takeaway: Integrity = Protection from unauthorized data modification/deletion

ndc.q: Which of the following is an IPSec Mode?
opts: Encryption Mode
Encapsulation Mode
Cyclic Chaining Mode
Transport Mode Template for generation of Questions Date: Module Name: NDC
ndc.expl: IPSec operates in two modes: Transport Mode (only the payload is encrypted, headers intact) and Tunnel Mode (entire original IP datagram is encrypted and wrapped in new IP headers).
ndc.takeaway: IPSec: Transport Mode (payload only) vs Tunnel Mode (entire datagram)

ndc.q: Network layer firewall works as a
opts: Frame Filter
Packet Filter
Both A and B
None of the mentioned
ndc.expl: A Network Layer (Layer 3) firewall functions as a packet filter, examining IP headers (source/destination IP, protocol, port) to allow or deny traffic based on rules.
ndc.takeaway: Network Layer firewall = Packet Filter (examines IP headers)

ndc.q: One advantage of setting up Dual Firewall DMZ (DMZ with two firewalls)
opts: You can control where traffic goes in the three networks
You can do stateful packet filtering
You can do load balancing
Improved network performance
ndc.expl: A Dual Firewall DMZ uses two separate firewalls — one between the internet and DMZ, another between DMZ and internal network. This allows separate control of traffic flow in all three network segments.
ndc.takeaway: Dual Firewall DMZ = Control traffic flow in all three network segments independently

ndc.q: Which of the following is the most important security aspect of using Network Address Translation (NAT)?
opts: It unites network nodes logically into the same broadcast domain
It hides the internal network from the outside world
It allows users to be grouped by department rather than location
It allows external users to access necessary information
ndc.expl: NAT's most important security benefit is hiding the internal network topology. External hosts only see the public IP, not the internal RFC 1918 addresses, obscuring the network structure.
ndc.takeaway: NAT security benefit = Hides internal network from outside (IP masquerading)

ndc.q: Which of the following are true about firewalls ?
opts: It can be either a hardware or software device
Monitors incoming and outgoing traffic
Follows a set of rules
All of the above
ndc.expl: Firewalls can be hardware or software, monitor incoming and outgoing traffic, and operate based on rule sets. All three statements are correct making 'All of the above' the answer.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall = Hardware or software, monitors in/out traffic, follows rule sets

ndc.q: A packet filtering firewall operates at
opts: Network Layer
Network and Transport Layer
Transport Layer
Transport and Application Layer
ndc.expl: Packet filtering firewalls operate at the Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model, examining IP packet headers including source/destination IP addresses and protocol fields.
ndc.takeaway: Packet filtering firewall = Operates at Network Layer (Layer 3)

ndc.q: Which of the following firewalls keeps track of the connection state?
opts: Application layer firewall
Packet filtering firewall
Router enhanced firewall
Stateful packet filtering firewall
ndc.expl: Stateful Packet Filtering (SPI) firewalls track the state of network connections, allowing them to distinguish legitimate responses from unsolicited packets.
ndc.takeaway: Stateful Packet Filtering = Tracks connection state (vs simple packet filtering)

ndc.q: Which firewall inspects packets at deeper level
opts: Static packet filtering firewall
Stateful packet filtering firewall
Gateway/Proxy firewall
Both B and C
ndc.expl: Both Stateful Packet Filtering and Gateway/Proxy firewalls inspect packets more deeply than static packet filtering. SPI tracks state; proxy firewalls examine application-layer content.
ndc.takeaway: Deep packet inspection: Both Stateful PF and Gateway/Proxy firewalls

ndc.q: Which of the following, best describes the difference between an intrusion detection system and a firewall?
opts: IDSs control the information coming in and out of the network, whereas firewalls actually prevent attacks
Firewalls control the information coming in and out of the network, whereas IDSs identify unauthorized activity
Firewalls control the information coming in and out of the network, whereas IDSs actually prevent attacks
IDSs control the information coming in and out of the network, whereas firewalls identify unauthorized activity
ndc.expl: Firewalls control what traffic enters/exits the network based on rules. IDS identifies and alerts on unauthorized/suspicious activity after it passes the firewall. IDS detects; firewalls filter.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall = Controls in/out traffic; IDS = Identifies unauthorized activity (passive)

ndc.q: Which of the following is true about circuit-level firewall?
opts: Operates at the transport layer of OSI model
It monitors TCP/UDP sessions
Both A and B
None
ndc.expl: Firewalls can be hardware or software, monitor incoming and outgoing traffic, and operate based on rule sets. All three statements are correct making 'All of the above' the answer.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall = Hardware or software, monitors in/out traffic, follows rule sets

ndc.q: Which of the following are examples of a bastion host ?
opts: Web Server
Proxy Server
DNS Server
All of the above
ndc.expl: Bastion hosts are hardened systems exposed to the internet. Web servers, DNS servers, and proxy servers are all examples of bastion hosts as they are exposed and must resist attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Bastion hosts = Web, DNS, Proxy servers — hardened systems exposed to internet

ndc.q: Which of the following best describes a demilitarized zone (DMZ) ?
opts: A small network between the database servers and file servers
A small network between the internal network and the Internet
A portion of the internal network that uses web-based technologies
A portion of the internal infrastructure used in business-to-business relationships
ndc.expl: A DMZ is a small network between the internal network and the internet. It hosts public-facing services (web, email, DNS) while protecting the internal network.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ = Small network between internal network and internet for public-facing services

ndc.q: Which of the following are advantages of using NAT? 1. Translation introduces switching path delays. 2. Conserves legally registered addresses. 3. Causes loss of end-to-end IP traceability. 4. Increases flexibility when connecting to the Internet. 5. Certain applications will not function with NAT enabled. 6. Reduces address overlap occurrence.
opts: 1, 3 and 4
3, 5 and 6
5 and 6
2, 4 and 6
ndc.expl: NAT advantages include: conserves registered IP addresses, increases flexibility connecting to internet, and reduces address overlap. Options 2, 4, and 6 are advantages. 1, 3, 5 are disadvantages.
ndc.takeaway: NAT advantages: Conserves IPs, increases flexibility, reduces overlap (2,4,6)

ndc.q: Which of the followings are main features of Iptables 1. Stateless packet filtering (IPv4 and Ipv6) 2. Stateful packet filtering (IPv4 and Ipv6) 3. Network address and port translation (NAT/NAPT) 4. Maintains three tables (Mangle, Filter & NAT)
opts: 1, 3 and 4
2, 3 and 4
Only 2 and 4
All of the above
ndc.expl: iptables features include: both stateless and stateful packet filtering (IPv4/IPv6), NAT/NAPT, but maintains FIVE tables (Filter, NAT, Mangle, Raw, Security), not three. All options listed are correct.
ndc.takeaway: iptables: stateless+stateful filtering, NAT/NAPT, 5 tables

ndc.q: Built-in chains in NAT tables
opts: Prerouting, Postrouting and Input
Prerouting, Postrouting and Output
Prerouting, Postrouting and Forward
Prerouting, Postrouting, Input, Output and Forward
ndc.expl: The NAT table has 3 built-in chains: PREROUTING (DNAT), OUTPUT, and POSTROUTING (SNAT). Input and Forward are NOT in the NAT table.
ndc.takeaway: NAT table chains: PREROUTING, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING (not INPUT/FORWARD)

ndc.q: Which is the default table in iptables command
opts: NAT Table
Filter Table
Mangle Table
None
ndc.expl: The Filter table is the default table in iptables when no table is specified with -t. It contains INPUT, FORWARD, and OUTPUT chains for basic packet filtering.
ndc.takeaway: iptables default table = Filter table (INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT chains)

ndc.q: The default snap length in tcpdump( built with IPv4) is________ (in bytes).
ndc.expl: The default snapshot length (snaplen) in tcpdump built with IPv4 is 68 bytes — enough to capture IP, TCP, and UDP headers for analysis without capturing full payloads.
ndc.takeaway: tcpdump default snaplen = 68 bytes (IPv4 build)

ndc.q: Which of following is not a protocol field of snort rule header
opts: TCP
ICMP
UDP
HTTP
ndc.expl: Snort rule protocols include TCP, UDP, ICMP, and IP. HTTP is not a protocol field in the Snort rule header — Snort identifies HTTP application-layer traffic via port inspection.
ndc.takeaway: Snort rule protocols: TCP, UDP, ICMP, IP; HTTP is NOT a protocol field

ndc.q: Which of the following cannot be used as an IDS Sensor?
opts: Windump
TCPDump
Libpcap
Nmap
ndc.expl: Nmap is a network scanning tool, not a packet capture/sensor tool. Windump, TCPDump, and Libpcap are all packet capture tools used as IDS sensors.
ndc.takeaway: IDS sensors: Windump, TCPDump, Libpcap; Nmap is a scanner, NOT a sensor

ndc.q: The __________ can be defined as sophisticated class of network security implementation that not only has the ability to detect the presence of intruders and their actions, but also to prevent them from successfully launching any attack.
opts: Firewall
IPS
VPN
IDS
ndc.expl: An IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) both detects and actively prevents attacks. Unlike IDS which only detects, IPS sits inline and can block malicious traffic in real time.
ndc.takeaway: IPS = Detects AND prevents attacks (inline); IDS = Only detects (passive)

ndc.q: The ______________ mode is the configuration of a network card wherein a setting is enabled so that the card passes all traffic it receives to the CPU rather than just packets addressed to it.
opts: Binary
ASCII
Promiscuous
PIF
ndc.expl: Promiscuous mode enables a NIC to capture all packets on the network segment, not just those addressed to it. Required for packet sniffers and IDS sensors.
ndc.takeaway: Promiscuous mode = NIC captures ALL network traffic, not just its own

ndc.q: In IDS, the _______ are considered to be the first point of intrusion detection.
opts: Analyzer
Agents
Sensors
Managers
ndc.expl: Sensors are the first point of intrusion detection in an IDS. They collect raw data (network packets or system events) and pass it to the analyzer for processing.
ndc.takeaway: IDS Sensors = First point of detection; collect raw data for analyzer

ndc.q: Which one is not the functionalities of UTM.
opts: Content Filtering
Network bandwidth management
Network Link management
Spyware filtering
ndc.expl: UTM (Unified Threat Management) provides: Content Filtering, Network bandwidth management (wrong — this is QoS, not UTM), IDS/IPS, VPN, Firewall. 'Network Link management' is not a UTM function.
ndc.takeaway: UTM functions: Content filtering, AV, IDS/IPS, VPN, Firewall, Spyware filtering

ndc.q: What are the two main types of intrusion detection systems?
opts: Signature-based and event-based
Network-based and host-based
Active and reactive
Intelligent and passive
ndc.expl: The two main IDS types by deployment are Network-based IDS (NIDS) — monitors network traffic — and Host-based IDS (HIDS) — monitors events on a single host.
ndc.takeaway: IDS types: Network-based (NIDS) and Host-based (HIDS)

ndc.q: The command for capturing 10 http requests
opts: tcpdump -i en1 -c 10 dst port 80
tcpdump -i en1 -p 10 dst port 80
tcpdump -i en1 -c 10 src port 80
tcpdump -i en1 -p 10 dst port 80
ndc.expl: tcpdump -i en1 -c 10 dst port 80 captures 10 packets on interface en1 destined for port 80 (HTTP). '-c' is count, 'dst port 80' filters for HTTP destination.
ndc.takeaway: tcpdump -i IFACE -c N dst port 80 = Capture N HTTP packets on interface

ndc.q: You are running Snort in your network to capture network traffic. Based on the following capture, what type of traffic is captured? 04/17-08:47:35.481575 0:A0:CC:58:CC:BF -> 0:80:5F:26:5A:21 type:0x800 len:0x3E 192.168.0.204:4654 -> 192.168.0.1:443 TCP TTL:128 TOS:0x0 ID:27146 I pLen:20 DgmLen:48 ******S* Seq: 0x52B6718E Ack: 0x0 Win: 0x4000 TcpLen: 28 TCP Options (4) => MSS: 1460 NOP NOP SackOK
opts: A secure Web server response
A secure Web server request
An unsecured Web server response
An unsecured Web server request
ndc.expl: The capture shows traffic to 192.168.0.1:443 (HTTPS port) with only SYN flag set (S). This is a web server REQUEST (SYN from client to HTTPS server = secure web server request).
ndc.takeaway: Port 443 + SYN flag = Secure web server REQUEST (client to HTTPS server)

ndc.q: An IDS may be configured to report attack occurrences. You just received a notification that an attack occurred, but after checking, you find that it really wasn't an attack at all. What is the term for this type of alarm?
opts: True positive
False positive
True negative
False negative
ndc.expl: A False Positive occurs when IDS reports an attack that didn't actually happen. A False Negative is when a real attack goes undetected. True Positive = real attack detected.
ndc.takeaway: False Positive = IDS alarm for a non-attack (incorrect alert)

ndc.q: What tool would you use to monitor for intrusions by reviewing computer system and event logs on a client computer?
opts: Network-based
client-based
Honeypot
Host-based
ndc.expl: Host-based IDS (HIDS) monitors a client computer's system and event logs for intrusions. NIDS monitors network traffic. Honeypots are decoy systems, not log monitors.
ndc.takeaway: HIDS = Monitor system/event logs on individual hosts for intrusions

ndc.q: In IDS/IPS, anomalies are also referred to as ________, surprise, aberrant, deviation, peculiarity, etc.
opts: Outliers
Normal distribution
Mean
Box plot
ndc.expl: In IDS/IPS, anomalies are called outliers — data points that deviate significantly from the expected distribution. They are also called aberrant data, deviations, or peculiarities.
ndc.takeaway: Anomalies/outliers = Deviations from normal baseline in IDS/IPS

ndc.q: Anomaly detection technique based on:
opts: Signature sets
Packet analysis
Deviated data
Training data
ndc.expl: Anomaly detection builds a model from training data representing normal behavior. Traffic deviating from this baseline triggers alerts. Signature-based uses signature sets.
ndc.takeaway: Anomaly detection = Training data builds normal baseline (statistical model)

ndc.q: Which of the following is not a packet capture library?
opts: libpcap
libpcre
libipq
libnetfilter_queue
ndc.expl: libpcap, libipq, and libnetfilter_queue are packet capture/processing libraries. libpcre is a Perl-Compatible Regular Expression library used for pattern matching, not packet capture.
ndc.takeaway: libpcre = Regex library; NOT a packet capture library

ndc.q: Which of following mode the snort engine can be run?
opts: sniffer
packet logging
network-intrusion detection
All of these
ndc.expl: Snort can run in three modes: Sniffer (prints packets), Packet Logger (logs packets to disk), and Network IDS (analyzes traffic for threats). All three are valid Snort modes.
ndc.takeaway: Snort modes: Sniffer, Packet Logger, Network IDS (all three valid)

ndc.q: Which one is not an example of UTM.

sonicwall
checkpoint
tripwire

ndc.takeaway: Tripwire = File integrity monitor, NOT a UTM device

pcap_lookupnet
pcap_lookupdev
pcap_loop

ndc.takeaway: pcap_lookupnet() = Determine IPv4 network and mask for a network device

ndc.q: Virtual private networks have one thing in common: They all share the same core set of technologies. Which of the following choices best describes that core set of technologies?

Tunneling, encryption, and authentication
Tunneling, encryption, and access control
Tunneling, encryption, authentication, and access control

ndc.takeaway: VPN core: Tunneling + Encryption + Authentication + Access Control

ndc.q: Which statement is true of IPSec transport mode?
opts: The entire IP datagram is left intact.
Only the IP headers are encrypted, and the original IP payload is left intact.
Only the IP payload is encrypted, and the original IP headers are left intact.
The entire original IP datagram is encrypted, and it becomes the payload in a new IP packet. Date: Module Name: Network Defense and Countermeasures (NDC)
ndc.expl: In IPSec Transport Mode, only the IP payload is encrypted; the original IP headers remain intact. In Tunnel Mode, the entire IP datagram is encrypted and encapsulated in a new IP packet.
ndc.takeaway: IPSec Transport Mode = Only payload encrypted; original IP headers intact

ndc.q: For bandwidth management using iptable, which module is used ?

ndc.expl: UTM provides: Content Filtering, Antivirus, IDS/IPS, VPN, Firewall. 'Network bandwidth management' (QoS) is typically not part of UTM — it belongs to WAN optimization or QoS devices.
ndc.takeaway: UTM excludes: Network bandwidth management (that's QoS/WAN optimization)

ndc.q: --------------------is a system that has been hardened to resist attack, and which is installed on a network in such a way that it is expected to potentially come under attack. a. Application layer
opts: Bastian host b. Network layer & Transport layer
Proxy c. Session layer & application layer
Gateway d. Transport layer & application layer
Firewall
ndc.expl: A Bastion Host is specifically hardened and positioned to face the internet, accepting potential attacks. It serves as the entry point for certain external services.
ndc.takeaway: Bastion Host = Hardened system expected to face attacks from internet

ndc.q: In tunnel mode……………………….. Only the payload of the packet is encrypted
opts: Entire packet is encrypted
Payload is never encrypted
None of the above
ndc.expl: The correct hierarchy in iptables is: Tables → Chains → Rules. Tables contain chains; chains contain rules. Example: Filter table → INPUT chain → individual rules.
ndc.takeaway: iptables structure: Tables → Chains → Rules

ndc.q: --------------------is not an access attack a.
opts: Packet filtering
Application layer filtering
Session layer
Border
ndc.expl: Access attacks include unauthorized access to systems/data. A 'port scan' is a reconnaissance/discovery technique, not an access attack itself. Access attacks include password attacks, session hijacking, etc.
ndc.takeaway: Port scan = Reconnaissance, NOT an access attack (unlike password attack, hijacking)

ndc.q: Which of the following is an IPsec protocol a. b. c. d. Snooping Eavesdropping DoS Non repudiation
opts: SSH
TLS
AH
SFTP
ndc.expl: IPSec operates in two modes: Transport Mode (only the payload is encrypted, headers intact) and Tunnel Mode (entire original IP datagram is encrypted and wrapped in new IP headers).
ndc.takeaway: IPSec: Transport Mode (payload only) vs Tunnel Mode (entire datagram)

ndc.q: A false positive can be defined as
opts: Encapsulation mode
Tunnel mode
Cyclic chaining mode
an alert that indicates nefarious activity on a system that, upon further inspection, turns out to represent legitimate network traffic or behavior.
ndc.expl: A false negative occurs when an actual attack or intrusion occurs but the IDS fails to detect and alert on it. This is dangerous as real attacks go unnoticed.
ndc.takeaway: False Negative = Real attack that IDS fails to detect (most dangerous IDS failure)

ndc.q: The protocol identifier number assigned to ICMP in the standard IP packet is
ndc.expl: SNAT (Source NAT) translates the source IP address, typically used for outgoing traffic from internal hosts. DNAT translates the destination IP for incoming traffic.
ndc.takeaway: SNAT = Source IP translated (inside→outside); DNAT = Destination IP translated

ndc.q: What is the default MTU size in 802.3 Ethernet
opts: HTTP
ndc.expl: The TCP three-way handshake establishes a connection: 1) Client sends SYN, 2) Server responds SYN-ACK, 3) Client sends ACK. This establishes sequence numbers and confirms both sides can communicate.
ndc.takeaway: TCP 3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK

ndc.q: Assault on system security that derives from an intelligent threat is
opts: Exploit
Attack c.ToE
Vulnerability
ndc.expl: An Attack is an assault on system security that derives from an intelligent threat — a deliberate attempt by a person or organization to compromise a system. A Vulnerability is a weakness; an Exploit is code that takes advantage of it.
ndc.takeaway: Attack = Deliberate assault on system security from intelligent threat

ndc.q: What is the total length of UDP header in bytes
opts: tcpdump udp and dst port 53 -w dump.pcap
tcpdump udp and port 53 -r dump.pcap
ndc.expl: The UDP header is a fixed 8 bytes (64 bits), consisting of Source Port (2 bytes), Destination Port (2 bytes), Length (2 bytes), and Checksum (2 bytes).
ndc.takeaway: UDP header = 8 bytes fixed (Source Port + Dest Port + Length + Checksum)

ndc.q: On a VPN, traffic is encrypted and decrypted at:
opts: tcpdump 'tcp[13]=2'
End points of the tunnel only
tcpdump 'tcp[13]=8'
User’s machines
ndc.expl: On a VPN, encryption and decryption occur at the VPN endpoints (gateways or client devices). Intermediate routers pass encrypted tunneled traffic without decrypting it.
ndc.takeaway: VPN encryption at endpoints; intermediate routers pass encrypted traffic

ndc.q: tcpdump -tt :
opts: Each device at each hop
prints the time in unformatted timestamp in dump line.
The data link layer of access devices
does print the time in dump line
ndc.expl: tcpdump -tt prints the time as an unformatted timestamp (Unix epoch time in seconds.microseconds) on each dump line instead of the human-readable time format.
ndc.takeaway: tcpdump -tt = Print unformatted Unix timestamp on each dump line

ndc.q: Which of the following best describes an attack that alter the content of two critical files?
opts: Integrity
Print a delta (micro-second resolution) between current and previous line on each dump line
Confidentiality
Availability
ndc.expl: Modifying file contents attacks Integrity — the assurance that data has not been tampered with by unauthorized parties. It is the 'I' in the CIA triad.
ndc.takeaway: Altering file contents = Integrity attack (CIA triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)

ndc.q: Anomaly detection is applicable in a variety of domains, such as intrusion detection, ________, fault detection, system health monitoring, event detection in sensor networks, and detecting eco-system disturbances
opts: tort
fraud
negligence
ndc.expl: Netfilter is a kernel space module (framework) built into the Linux kernel for network packet processing. iptables is the user space tool that configures Netfilter rules.
ndc.takeaway: Netfilter = Kernel space framework; iptables = User space configuration tool

ndc.q: IPsec provides which options as security services?
opts: ESP and AH b.ESP and AP c.EA and AH d.EA and AP
neutral reportage
ndc.expl: IPSec provides three security services: Authentication (verifies identity), Confidentiality (encrypts data), and Integrity (ensures data hasn't been modified). All three are core IPSec services.
ndc.takeaway: IPSec security services: Authentication + Confidentiality + Integrity (all three)

ndc.q: Which security control is a consequence of nonmalicious activity generally representing an error?
opts: Encryption mode
Encapsulation mode
Transport mode
true positive
ndc.expl: Netfilter is a kernel space module (framework) built into the Linux kernel for network packet processing. iptables is the user space tool that configures Netfilter rules.
ndc.takeaway: Netfilter = Kernel space framework; iptables = User space configuration tool

ndc.q: How many built-in tables are in iptables
opts: false negative
two
ndc.expl: iptables has 5 built-in tables: Filter, NAT, Mangle, Raw, and Security. Filter (default) handles packet filtering; NAT handles address translation; Mangle alters packet headers; Raw handles connection tracking exemptions.
ndc.takeaway: iptables has 5 tables: Filter, NAT, Mangle, Raw, Security

ndc.q: Which one is not a target in iptables ?
opts: ACCEPT
DROP
save-iptables
FILTER
ndc.expl: Valid iptables targets include: ACCEPT, DROP, RETURN, REJECT, LOG, MASQUERADE, DNAT, SNAT. FILTER is a table name, not a target/action.
ndc.takeaway: iptables targets: ACCEPT, DROP, RETURN, REJECT; FILTER is a table, not a target

ndc.q: Probably the simplest physical attack on the computer system is: a. Accessing an Ethernet jack to attack the network
opts: Using an imitation to fool a biometric authenticator
Installing a virus on the CCTV system
Outright theft of the computers
two
ndc.expl: The simplest physical attack on a computer is stealing the hard drive. Physical access allows bypassing software-based security controls entirely.
ndc.takeaway: Simplest physical attack = Stealing the hard drive (bypasses all software security)

ndc.q: Honeypots are used to: a. Attract attackers by simulating systems with open network services
opts: DNAT b. employees
SNAT c. Process alarms from other IDSs d. Attract customers to e-commerce
Switching Monitor network usage by sites
Bridging
ndc.expl: Honeypots are decoy systems designed to attract and detect attackers. They appear to be legitimate targets but are actually monitoring systems that alert administrators to intrusion attempts.
ndc.takeaway: Honeypot = Decoy system to attract/detect attackers and study attack methods

ndc.q: --------------------- firewalls are also known as first generation firewalls.
opts: Packet filtering
iptables-store
Application layer filtering
Session layer
ndc.expl: Application proxy firewalls are also called Application Layer Gateways (ALGs) because they operate at Layer 7 and proxy connections for specific applications.
ndc.takeaway: Application proxy firewalls = Application Layer Gateways (ALGs)

ndc.q: Application proxy firewalls falls under the category of
opts: First generation firewalls
Second generation firewalls
Third generation firewalls
Fourth generation firewalls
ndc.expl: Application proxy firewalls are categorized as Application Layer Gateways because they operate at Layer 7, fully understanding application protocols (HTTP, FTP, SMTP).
ndc.takeaway: Application proxy = Falls under Application Layer Gateway category

ndc.q: Weakness in a mechanism that can threaten CIA triads of an asset is known asa.
opts: Vulnerability
Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
ndc.expl: A Vulnerability is a weakness in hardware, software, or procedures that could be exploited to gain unauthorized access. It is what attackers exploit to compromise systems.
ndc.takeaway: Vulnerability = Exploitable weakness in system/software/procedure

opts: user space b.kernel space c.Application space
link space
ndc.expl: Netfilter is a kernel space module (framework) built into the Linux kernel for network packet processing. iptables is the user space tool that configures Netfilter rules.
ndc.takeaway: Netfilter = Kernel space framework; iptables = User space configuration tool

ndc.q: --------------------is not an access attack
opts: Snooping
Eavesdropping
DoS
Non repudiation
ndc.expl: Access attacks include unauthorized access to systems/data. A 'port scan' is a reconnaissance/discovery technique, not an access attack itself. Access attacks include password attacks, session hijacking, etc.
ndc.takeaway: Port scan = Reconnaissance, NOT an access attack (unlike password attack, hijacking)

ndc.q: Which among the following authentication is the weakest?
opts: Token
Retina scan c.Passwords
Pass phrase
ndc.expl: Biometric authentication uses physical characteristics (fingerprint, retina, face) which are unique to each person and cannot be shared, stolen as easily as passwords, or forgotten.
ndc.takeaway: Biometric = Authentication using unique physical characteristics

ndc.q: Which of the following bit is set ON when the connection is terminated due to the abnormal conditions
opts: FIN
SYN
ACK
RST
ndc.expl: The SYN bit (Synchronize) is set in the first packet of a TCP three-way handshake (connection initiation). It signals the start of a new TCP connection attempt.
ndc.takeaway: SYN bit = Set in first TCP connection packet (handshake initiation)

ndc.q: Which of the following is an IPsec protocol
opts: SSH
TLS
AH
SFTP
ndc.expl: IPSec operates in two modes: Transport Mode (only the payload is encrypted, headers intact) and Tunnel Mode (entire original IP datagram is encrypted and wrapped in new IP headers).
ndc.takeaway: IPSec: Transport Mode (payload only) vs Tunnel Mode (entire datagram)

ndc.q: Which of the following is an IPsec mode? a.Encryption mode
opts: Encapsulation mode
Tunnel mode
Cyclic chaining mode a. b. c. d. Snooping Eavesdropping DoS Non repudiation.
16 Module Name-1 : (60 Minutes)
ndc.expl: IPSec operates in two modes: Transport Mode (only the payload is encrypted, headers intact) and Tunnel Mode (entire original IP datagram is encrypted and wrapped in new IP headers).
ndc.takeaway: IPSec: Transport Mode (payload only) vs Tunnel Mode (entire datagram)

ndc.q: Which type of firewall can restrict spread of computer worms and Trojans?
opts: Application layer firewall
Packet filter firewall
Stateful firewall
NAT firewall
ndc.expl: Application layer firewalls (proxy firewalls) inspect full packet payloads at Layer 7 and can detect and restrict malware payloads including computer worms and Trojans embedded in application traffic. Packet filters and stateful firewalls only inspect headers, not content.
ndc.takeaway: Application layer firewall = Inspects content; restricts malware like worms and Trojans

ndc.q: alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> 192.168.1.0/24 111 (msg: "mountd access"; content: "|00 01|"; sid:1000001;) — The above Snort signature/rule applies to which traffic?
opts: External Network to Home Network
External Network to External Network
Home Network to External Network
Home Network to Home Network
ndc.expl: The Snort rule shows: $EXTERNAL_NET (source) -> 192.168.1.0/24 (destination). The arrow '->' indicates traffic direction FROM $EXTERNAL_NET TO 192.168.1.0/24 (the Home Network). This is External Network to Home Network traffic.
ndc.takeaway: Snort rule: SRC -> DEST; $EXTERNAL_NET -> 192.168.1.0/24 = External to Home Network

ndc.q: At which two traffic layers do IDSes generate signatures?
opts: Network and Transport layers
Transport and Application layers
Network and Application layers
Data Link and Network layers
ndc.expl: IDSes generate signatures at the Network Layer (Layer 3 — IP addresses, protocols) and Application Layer (Layer 7 — content patterns, protocol behavior). These two layers provide the richest information for attack pattern matching.
ndc.takeaway: IDS signature generation: Network Layer (IP/protocol) + Application Layer (content patterns)

ndc.q: Which of the following best describes the CIA triad in information security?
opts: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Control, Identity, Authentication
Cipher, Integrity, Authorization
Confidentiality, Identity, Access
ndc.expl: The CIA triad stands for Confidentiality (preventing unauthorized disclosure), Integrity (preventing unauthorized modification), and Availability (ensuring authorized access when needed). It is the cornerstone of information security.
ndc.takeaway: CIA Triad = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability (core of InfoSec)

ndc.q: What is the difference between a threat and a vulnerability?
opts: A threat is a weakness; a vulnerability is a potential danger
A vulnerability is a weakness; a threat is a potential danger that could exploit it
Both terms mean the same thing
A threat is a realized attack; a vulnerability is a theoretical one
ndc.expl: A vulnerability is a weakness in a system or process. A threat is a potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability to cause harm. Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact.
ndc.takeaway: Vulnerability = Weakness; Threat = Potential danger exploiting that weakness; Risk = both combined

ndc.q: What is the principle of least privilege in security?
opts: Users should have the most access possible for productivity
Users should be granted only the minimum access rights needed to perform their job
System administrators should have no access to production systems
All users should have equal access to all resources
ndc.expl: The principle of least privilege (PoLP) grants users, processes, and systems only the minimum permissions required to perform their legitimate functions. This limits the damage from compromised accounts or malware.
ndc.takeaway: Least Privilege = Grant minimum permissions needed; limits blast radius of compromise

ndc.q: What is defense in depth in network security?
opts: Using one very strong firewall at the network perimeter
Applying multiple layers of security controls so that if one fails, others still protect the system
Hiding network topology from attackers
Using deep packet inspection on all traffic
ndc.expl: Defense in depth uses multiple overlapping security layers (perimeter, network, host, application, data) so that the compromise of one control does not result in full system compromise. It is also called layered security.
ndc.takeaway: Defense in Depth = Multiple security layers; one failure does not compromise everything

ndc.q: Which type of attack involves sending more data than a buffer can hold, potentially allowing code execution?
opts: SQL Injection
Buffer Overflow
Cross-Site Scripting
CSRF
ndc.expl: A buffer overflow attack writes more data than the allocated buffer can hold, overwriting adjacent memory. If an attacker carefully crafts the overflow, they can overwrite the return address and redirect execution to malicious code.
ndc.takeaway: Buffer Overflow = Write past buffer boundary to overwrite memory and hijack execution

ndc.q: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
opts: A vulnerability discovered on day zero of a product's release
A vulnerability that has no known patch or fix at the time of discovery
A vulnerability that has been patched zero times
A vulnerability affecting zero systems
ndc.expl: A zero-day (0-day) vulnerability is one that is unknown to the software vendor or for which no patch exists. Attackers who discover zero-days can exploit them without any defense available, making them extremely dangerous.
ndc.takeaway: Zero-Day = Unknown to vendor, no patch available; highest risk vulnerability

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a security policy?
opts: To document which ports are open on the firewall
To define rules, expectations, and procedures for protecting organizational assets and data
To list all known vulnerabilities in the network
To configure security devices automatically
ndc.expl: A security policy is a formal document defining an organization's security goals, acceptable behaviors, rules, and procedures for protecting information assets. It forms the foundation for all security controls and practices.
ndc.takeaway: Security Policy = Formal document defining rules and procedures to protect org assets

ndc.q: Which security model is based on the concept that no user or device should be trusted by default, even inside the network?
opts: Perimeter security model
Zero Trust security model
Defense in depth model
Role-based access control model
ndc.expl: The Zero Trust model operates on the principle of 'never trust, always verify.' Every user, device, and network flow must be authenticated and authorized, regardless of whether they are inside or outside the network perimeter.
ndc.takeaway: Zero Trust = Never trust, always verify; no implicit trust even inside the network

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a security audit?
opts: To patch vulnerabilities automatically
To systematically evaluate security controls against a standard to identify gaps and weaknesses
To monitor network traffic in real time
To configure firewalls and IDS systems
ndc.expl: A security audit systematically evaluates an organization's security controls, policies, and procedures against a defined standard (ISO 27001, NIST, PCI-DSS) to identify gaps, weaknesses, and non-compliant configurations.
ndc.takeaway: Security Audit = Systematic evaluation of controls vs standards to find gaps

ndc.q: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
opts: Authentication determines what you can do; authorization verifies who you are
Authentication verifies identity (who you are); authorization determines what you are allowed to do
They are the same concept
Authorization comes before authentication
ndc.expl: Authentication verifies identity (Who are you? — via passwords, certificates, biometrics). Authorization determines what an authenticated user is permitted to do (What can you access?). AAA also includes Accounting (what did you do?).
ndc.takeaway: Authentication = Who are you? Authorization = What can you do? Accounting = What did you do?

ndc.q: What is social engineering in cybersecurity?
opts: Using software tools to bypass security controls
Manipulating people psychologically to divulge confidential information or take security-compromising actions
Engineering secure social media platforms
Reverse-engineering social network protocols
ndc.expl: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Examples include phishing, pretexting, baiting, vishing, and tailgating. It is often the easiest way to compromise an organization.
ndc.takeaway: Social Engineering = Exploiting human psychology to bypass security (phishing, vishing, pretexting)

ndc.q: What is a man-in-the-middle (MitM) attack?
opts: An attack where a hacker sits between two communicating parties and intercepts/alters their communication
An attack targeting the middle tier of a three-tier application
A DDoS attack from middleman servers
An attack exploiting middleware software
ndc.expl: In a MitM attack, the attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties, intercepting and potentially altering the communication without either party's knowledge. ARP poisoning and SSL stripping are common MitM techniques.
ndc.takeaway: MitM = Attacker intercepts/alters communication between two parties undetected

ndc.q: What does AAA stand for in network security?
opts: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting
Access, Audit, Availability
Authentication, Access, Auditing
Authorization, Accountability, Availability
ndc.expl: AAA stands for Authentication (verifying identity), Authorization (determining access rights), and Accounting (recording user activity). RADIUS and TACACS+ are common protocols implementing AAA.
ndc.takeaway: AAA = Authentication + Authorization + Accounting (RADIUS/TACACS+ implement this)

ndc.q: What is a honeypot in network security?
opts: A secure vault for storing passwords
A decoy system designed to attract and detect attackers by mimicking legitimate targets
A type of encryption algorithm
A firewall configuration for DMZ
ndc.expl: A honeypot is a decoy system or resource intentionally left vulnerable to attract attackers. It detects intrusion attempts, studies attacker behavior, and diverts attackers from real targets. A honeynet is a network of honeypots.
ndc.takeaway: Honeypot = Decoy system to attract attackers; alerts admins and studies attack methods

ndc.q: What is risk management in information security?
opts: Eliminating all security risks
The process of identifying, assessing, and prioritizing risks and applying controls to minimize their impact
Managing network traffic risks
Automatically patching vulnerabilities
ndc.expl: Risk management identifies potential threats and vulnerabilities, assesses their likelihood and impact, and applies cost-effective controls. Risks can be accepted, avoided, transferred (insurance), or mitigated.
ndc.takeaway: Risk Management = Identify → Assess → Prioritize → Mitigate/Accept/Transfer/Avoid risks

ndc.q: What is the purpose of network segmentation?
opts: To increase network speed
To divide a network into smaller zones to limit the spread of threats and reduce attack surface
To reduce the number of IP addresses needed
To balance network load across servers
ndc.expl: Network segmentation divides a network into isolated zones (using VLANs, firewalls, DMZ) to contain breaches, limit lateral movement, reduce attack surface, and enforce security policies between zones.
ndc.takeaway: Network Segmentation = Divide into zones to limit lateral movement and contain breaches

ndc.q: What is a botnet?
opts: A network of security robots that defend systems
A network of compromised computers (bots/zombies) controlled by an attacker for malicious activities
An automated testing network
A network of honeypots
ndc.expl: A botnet is a collection of internet-connected computers infected with malware and controlled by a command-and-control (C2) server without their owners' knowledge. Botnets are used for DDoS, spam, click fraud, and cryptocurrency mining.
ndc.takeaway: Botnet = Network of zombie computers controlled by C2 server for malicious activity

ndc.q: What is the purpose of penetration testing?
opts: Testing network speed and bandwidth
Simulating real-world attacks on systems to identify security vulnerabilities before malicious actors do
Testing firewall packet filtering rules
Verifying patch deployment success
ndc.expl: Penetration testing (pen testing) is an authorized simulated cyberattack on a system to identify exploitable vulnerabilities. It goes beyond vulnerability scanning by actually attempting exploitation, providing real-world risk assessment.
ndc.takeaway: Pen Testing = Authorized simulated attack to find exploitable vulnerabilities before real attackers

ndc.q: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
opts: Symmetric uses two keys; asymmetric uses one key
Symmetric uses one shared key for both encryption and decryption; asymmetric uses a public/private key pair
Both use the same key mechanism
Symmetric is more secure than asymmetric
ndc.expl: Symmetric encryption (AES, DES) uses the same key for encryption and decryption — fast but key distribution is a challenge. Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECC) uses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt — slower but solves key distribution.
ndc.takeaway: Symmetric = One shared key (fast, AES); Asymmetric = Public/Private key pair (RSA, secure key exchange)

ndc.q: What does SIEM stand for and what is its primary function?
opts: Secure Information and Event Management; manages server configurations
Security Information and Event Management; aggregates and correlates logs and alerts for threat detection
System Intelligence and Event Monitoring; monitors hardware events
Secure Incident and Error Management; handles system errors
ndc.expl: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) collects logs and events from across the infrastructure, correlates them to detect security incidents, and generates alerts. Examples: Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel.
ndc.takeaway: SIEM = Aggregates, correlates logs for threat detection; Splunk, QRadar, Sentinel

ndc.q: What is ARP poisoning?
opts: A DoS attack that floods the ARP cache
An attack that sends fake ARP replies to associate the attacker's MAC with a legitimate IP, enabling MitM attacks
An attack on wireless ARP protocols
A method to detect rogue devices on a network
ndc.expl: ARP poisoning (ARP spoofing) sends forged ARP replies to associate the attacker's MAC address with a victim's IP address. This redirects traffic through the attacker, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks and traffic interception.
ndc.takeaway: ARP Poisoning = Fake ARP replies link attacker's MAC to victim's IP → MitM

ndc.q: What is port scanning?
opts: Physically scanning network ports for damage
Probing a system's network ports to identify open ports, services, and OS
Scanning for unauthorized wireless access points
Monitoring bandwidth on network ports
ndc.expl: Port scanning probes a host to identify open ports and associated services. Nmap is the most popular tool. Attackers use it for reconnaissance; administrators use it for security auditing. Common scan types: SYN, TCP connect, UDP, FIN.
ndc.takeaway: Port Scanning = Probe host for open ports/services; Nmap is the standard tool

ndc.q: Which of the following best defines 'integrity' in the context of the CIA triad?
opts: Ensuring data is accessible when needed
Ensuring data is not disclosed to unauthorized parties
Ensuring data is accurate and has not been tampered with
Ensuring data is encrypted at rest
ndc.expl: Integrity in the CIA triad ensures that data remains accurate, complete, and unmodified unless by authorized parties. Techniques include hashing (MD5, SHA-256), digital signatures, and checksums to detect tampering.
ndc.takeaway: Integrity = Data accuracy and non-tampering; enforced by hashing and digital signatures

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a security incident response plan?
opts: To automate firewall rule updates
To define structured procedures for detecting, containing, eradicating, and recovering from security incidents
To document network topology
To manage security patch deployment
ndc.expl: An Incident Response Plan (IRP) defines structured steps: Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, and Lessons Learned (PICERL). It ensures rapid, organized responses to minimize breach impact.
ndc.takeaway: Incident Response: Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons Learned

ndc.q: What is a replay attack?
opts: Repeatedly sending DoS packets to overwhelm a server
Capturing a valid authentication token or message and re-transmitting it to gain unauthorized access
Playing recorded audio to bypass voice authentication
Replaying network logs to find anomalies
ndc.expl: A replay attack captures a valid network transmission (like authentication credentials or session tokens) and retransmits it to gain unauthorized access. Prevented by using timestamps, nonces (number used once), and session tokens.
ndc.takeaway: Replay Attack = Capture and retransmit valid auth token; prevented by nonces and timestamps

ndc.q: What is the purpose of network monitoring in security?
opts: To physically monitor network cable conditions
To continuously observe network traffic and systems to detect anomalies, threats, and policy violations
To monitor network hardware temperatures
To track network billing and usage costs
ndc.expl: Network monitoring in security continuously observes traffic patterns, device states, and security events to detect anomalies, unauthorized access, malware, and policy violations. Tools: Nagios, Zabbix, Wireshark, SIEM.
ndc.takeaway: Network Monitoring = Continuous observation for anomalies, threats, and policy violations

ndc.q: What does 'non-repudiation' mean in information security?
opts: Ensuring data cannot be modified
Ensuring a party cannot deny having performed an action or sent a message
Ensuring data is not replicated
Preventing unauthorized access to data
ndc.expl: Non-repudiation ensures that a party cannot deny having performed a specific action (e.g., sending a message, making a transaction). Achieved through digital signatures — the signer's private key uniquely proves they signed the message.
ndc.takeaway: Non-repudiation = Cannot deny an action; enforced by digital signatures (private key proof)

ndc.q: What is a security baseline?
opts: The minimum level of security that all systems in an organization must meet
The starting point for security configuration before hardening
A document listing all known attacks
The default security settings of an operating system
ndc.expl: A security baseline defines the minimum acceptable security configuration for a system or service. All deployed systems must meet or exceed this standard. CIS Benchmarks and DISA STIGs are examples of security baselines.
ndc.takeaway: Security Baseline = Minimum mandatory security configuration (CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of logging and monitoring in a security policy?
opts: To record all user keystrokes for legal purposes
To maintain records of system activity for detecting incidents, forensic analysis, and compliance
To monitor employee productivity
To automatically respond to security events
ndc.expl: Logging records system events, user actions, and network activity. Monitoring reviews these logs in real time or periodically to detect incidents, support forensic investigations, and demonstrate compliance with security policies.
ndc.takeaway: Logging + Monitoring = Detection of incidents, forensic evidence, compliance proof

ndc.q: What is the first step in the security risk assessment process?
opts: Implement security controls
Identify assets and their value
Test existing controls
Write security policies
ndc.expl: Risk assessment starts with asset identification — cataloging all systems, data, and services and determining their business value. Without knowing what assets exist and their importance, risks cannot be properly assessed or prioritized.
ndc.takeaway: Risk Assessment Step 1 = Identify and value assets before assessing threats/vulnerabilities

ndc.q: What is an access control list (ACL) in network security?
opts: A list of authorized users for a building
A set of rules that permit or deny network traffic based on IP addresses, ports, and protocols
A database of encryption keys
A list of security patches to be applied
ndc.expl: An ACL is a sequential set of rules applied to network interfaces or firewalls that permit or deny traffic based on criteria like source/destination IP, protocol, and port number. Standard ACLs filter by source IP; Extended ACLs filter by multiple criteria.
ndc.takeaway: ACL = Rules permitting/denying traffic by IP, port, protocol; Standard=source IP, Extended=multi-criteria

ndc.q: What is the primary purpose of a firewall?
opts: To prevent all network attacks
To monitor and control incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predefined security rules
To encrypt all network traffic
To detect and remove malware from the network
ndc.expl: A firewall monitors and controls network traffic between security zones based on rules. It is the first line of defense, implementing access control policies. It does NOT detect all attacks — that's the IDS/IPS role.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall = Controls traffic between zones based on rules; first line of perimeter defense

ndc.q: Which firewall type examines each packet in isolation without considering its relationship to other packets?
opts: Stateful Packet Inspection firewall
Stateless Packet Filter firewall
Application Layer Gateway
Next-Generation Firewall
ndc.expl: Stateless (packet filter) firewalls examine each packet independently based on header information (IP, port, protocol) without tracking connection state. They are fast but cannot detect attacks spanning multiple packets.
ndc.takeaway: Stateless Firewall = Examines each packet independently; no connection state tracking

ndc.q: What is the key advantage of a stateful firewall over a stateless packet filter?
opts: It is faster than stateless firewalls
It tracks the state of connections and can detect if a packet belongs to a legitimate established session
It operates at the application layer
It provides intrusion prevention capabilities
ndc.expl: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection state table tracking all active connections. They verify each packet belongs to an established, legitimate session, preventing attacks like unsolicited ACK packets that stateless firewalls would allow.
ndc.takeaway: Stateful Firewall = Tracks connection state table; verifies packets belong to legitimate sessions

ndc.q: At which OSI layer does a packet filtering firewall primarily operate?
opts: Application Layer (Layer 7)
Session Layer (Layer 5)
Network Layer (Layer 3)
Data Link Layer (Layer 2)
ndc.expl: Packet filtering firewalls operate primarily at the Network Layer (Layer 3) and Transport Layer (Layer 4), examining IP headers (source/destination IP, TTL) and TCP/UDP headers (source/destination ports, flags).
ndc.takeaway: Packet Filter Firewall = Layers 3 & 4 (IP and TCP/UDP headers)

ndc.q: What is a proxy firewall (application gateway)?
opts: A firewall that proxies only HTTP traffic
A firewall that acts as an intermediary, establishing connections on behalf of clients so the server never communicates directly with the client
A firewall with built-in antivirus
A firewall deployed in the cloud
ndc.expl: A proxy firewall (application gateway) acts as an intermediary for all connections. Clients connect to the proxy; the proxy evaluates the request at the application layer and establishes a new connection to the server. The server never sees the real client IP.
ndc.takeaway: Proxy Firewall = Intermediary; server sees proxy, not real client; deep application inspection

ndc.q: What is a Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)?
opts: A faster version of a traditional firewall
A firewall that combines traditional firewall capabilities with deep packet inspection, application awareness, and user identity tracking
A firewall that uses AI to detect threats
A cloud-based firewall service
ndc.expl: NGFWs extend traditional firewalls with deep packet inspection (DPI), application-layer visibility and control (App-ID), user identity awareness (User-ID), intrusion prevention (IPS), and SSL/TLS inspection in a single device.
ndc.takeaway: NGFW = Traditional firewall + DPI + App awareness + User identity + IPS + SSL inspection

ndc.q: Which of the following is a limitation of traditional firewalls?
opts: They are too expensive
They cannot inspect encrypted traffic (SSL/TLS) without additional configuration
They cannot filter by IP address
They require a separate DMZ to function
ndc.expl: Traditional firewalls cannot inspect the contents of encrypted SSL/TLS traffic — they can only see the outer IP/port headers. This allows malware to hide in HTTPS traffic. NGFWs with SSL inspection can decrypt and inspect encrypted sessions.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall limitation = Cannot inspect encrypted SSL/TLS traffic without SSL inspection

ndc.q: What is a web application firewall (WAF)?
opts: A firewall specifically for web server hardware protection
A security device that monitors, filters, and blocks HTTP/HTTPS traffic to protect web applications from attacks like SQLi and XSS
A firewall embedded in web browsers
A reverse proxy with caching capabilities
ndc.expl: A WAF operates at Layer 7, analyzing HTTP/HTTPS traffic to protect web applications from OWASP Top 10 threats: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, file inclusion, and more. Examples: ModSecurity, AWS WAF, Cloudflare WAF.
ndc.takeaway: WAF = Layer 7 firewall protecting web apps from SQLi, XSS, CSRF (OWASP Top 10)

ndc.q: What is a host-based firewall?
opts: A firewall that only protects server hosts
A software firewall installed directly on a host to control traffic to and from that specific device
A firewall hosted in the cloud
A firewall managing host-to-host VPNs
ndc.expl: A host-based firewall is software running on individual devices (Windows Defender Firewall, iptables, pf) that controls inbound and outbound traffic for that specific host. It complements network firewalls for endpoint protection.
ndc.takeaway: Host-based Firewall = Software on individual device controlling its own in/out traffic

ndc.q: What is a screened subnet (DMZ) firewall architecture?
opts: A single firewall with three interfaces: internal, external, and DMZ
A single firewall protecting a subnet from a specific threat
A subnet that screens all packets before forwarding
Two firewalls enclosing a network segment with public-facing services
ndc.expl: A screened subnet (dual-firewall DMZ) uses two firewalls enclosing the DMZ: one between internet and DMZ, another between DMZ and internal network. This provides two layers of protection and is considered more secure than a single three-legged firewall.
ndc.takeaway: Screened Subnet = Two firewalls enclosing DMZ (internet→FW1→DMZ→FW2→internal network)

ndc.q: Which firewall action drops a packet without notifying the sender?
opts: REJECT
DENY
DROP
BLOCK
ndc.expl: DROP silently discards packets without sending any response to the sender. REJECT discards the packet AND sends an error response (TCP RST or ICMP unreachable). DROP is preferred for external-facing rules to avoid giving information to attackers.
ndc.takeaway: DROP = Silent discard (no response); REJECT = Discard + sends error back (reveals firewall existence)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a firewall ruleset?
opts: To configure the firewall hardware
To define an ordered list of rules specifying what traffic to allow or deny
To document the firewall's physical location
To set up the firewall's management interface
ndc.expl: A firewall ruleset is an ordered list of rules defining traffic filtering policy. Rules are evaluated in order; the first matching rule applies. A default deny (implicit deny) rule at the end blocks all traffic not explicitly permitted.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall Ruleset = Ordered rules evaluated top-to-bottom; implicit deny blocks unmatched traffic

ndc.q: What does 'implicit deny' mean in firewall policy?
opts: The firewall denies all traffic by default unless explicitly allowed
Rules that deny traffic take precedence over allow rules
Denied traffic is logged automatically
The firewall denies packets from unknown IP addresses only
ndc.expl: Implicit deny is a fundamental firewall security principle: any traffic not explicitly permitted by a rule is denied by default. This ensures that forgetting to write a rule results in denial (secure by default) rather than accidental permission.
ndc.takeaway: Implicit Deny = Everything not explicitly ALLOWED is DENIED (secure by default)

ndc.q: What is a firewall's 'self zone'?
opts: A zone for firewall management traffic only
Traffic destined for or sourced from the firewall device itself (management, routing protocols)
The most trusted internal zone
A DMZ zone managed by the firewall
ndc.expl: The self zone (in zone-based firewalls like Cisco IOS ZBF) represents the firewall device itself. Traffic to/from the router's own interfaces falls in the self zone. By default, all traffic to the self zone is permitted (the router trusts itself).
ndc.takeaway: Self Zone = Firewall's own traffic; by default all traffic to self zone is permitted

ndc.q: What is a personal/desktop firewall?
opts: A hardware firewall for home use
A software application on an individual computer filtering its own inbound and outbound traffic
A miniaturized firewall appliance
A firewall that only blocks personal (non-business) traffic
ndc.expl: A personal firewall is software on an individual computer that monitors and filters its network traffic. Examples: Windows Defender Firewall, macOS Application Firewall, ZoneAlarm. It supplements network-level firewalls.
ndc.takeaway: Personal Firewall = Software on individual PC filtering its own traffic (Windows Defender Firewall)

ndc.q: Which limitation applies to all types of firewalls?
opts: Firewalls cannot filter by port number
Firewalls cannot stop insider threats or attacks that originate from trusted internal networks
Firewalls always introduce unacceptable latency
Firewalls cannot be deployed in virtualized environments
ndc.expl: All firewalls primarily protect perimeter security. They cannot stop threats from inside the trusted network (insider threats, compromised internal hosts). Defense in depth with IDS/IPS, NAC, and endpoint security addresses internal threats.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall limitation = Cannot stop insider threats or attacks from trusted internal network

ndc.q: What is a cloud firewall (Firewall-as-a-Service)?
opts: A firewall protecting only cloud-hosted applications
A firewall service delivered from the cloud, providing scalable security for distributed organizations and cloud workloads
A virtual firewall on a cloud VM
A firewall for managing cloud storage access
ndc.expl: Cloud firewalls (FWaaS) are security services delivered from the cloud that provide firewall capabilities without on-premises hardware. They scale automatically, protect distributed offices, and secure cloud workloads. Examples: Zscaler, Palo Alto Prisma.
ndc.takeaway: FWaaS (Cloud Firewall) = Firewall delivered as cloud service; scalable, no hardware required

ndc.q: What is egress filtering in firewall policy?
opts: Filtering traffic entering the network from the internet
Filtering and controlling outbound traffic leaving the internal network to the internet
Filtering traffic at the network edge
Emergency filtering during a security incident
ndc.expl: Egress filtering controls outbound traffic from the internal network. It prevents data exfiltration, blocks botnet C2 communication, and stops infected internal hosts from attacking external systems. Ingress filtering controls inbound traffic.
ndc.takeaway: Egress Filtering = Controls outbound traffic; prevents data exfiltration and C2 communication

ndc.q: What is the key feature that distinguishes a Next-Generation Firewall from a traditional stateful firewall?
opts: Higher throughput
Application visibility and control (identifying traffic by application, not just port/protocol)
Built-in router functionality
Lower cost
ndc.expl: NGFWs identify traffic by application (App-ID) regardless of port or protocol — Facebook on port 80 vs LinkedIn on port 80 are distinguishable. Traditional firewalls only see port 80 and cannot differentiate applications.
ndc.takeaway: NGFW key feature = Application-ID (App-ID); identifies apps regardless of port/protocol

ndc.q: What is deep packet inspection (DPI)?
opts: Inspecting the physical depth of network cables
Examining not just packet headers but also the payload content to detect threats and policy violations
A technique to prioritize VIP packets
Checking IP header TTL values
ndc.expl: Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) examines the complete packet — header AND payload. It enables application identification, content filtering, malware detection, and data loss prevention. Used in NGFWs, WAFs, and IPS systems.
ndc.takeaway: DPI = Inspect packet header + payload; enables app ID, content filtering, malware detection

ndc.q: Which of the following is NOT a limitation of firewalls?
opts: Cannot prevent attacks from inside the network
Cannot inspect encrypted traffic without SSL interception
Cannot filter network traffic based on IP addresses
Cannot protect against zero-day attacks using novel techniques
ndc.expl: Filtering traffic by IP address is a core firewall capability, not a limitation. Firewalls CAN filter by IP address, port, and protocol. Limitations include: cannot stop insider threats, cannot inspect encrypted traffic, and may miss zero-day attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Firewalls CAN filter by IP/port; CANNOT: stop insiders, decrypt SSL, catch zero-days

ndc.q: What is Cisco ASA?
opts: Adaptive Security Application — a web application firewall product
Adaptive Security Appliance — a firewall/VPN/IPS hardware platform from Cisco
An open-source firewall project
Application Security Architecture — a security framework
