ndc.expl: A TCP RST (reset) flag abruptly terminates a connection. RST in response to SYN means the port is closed (no service). Unexpected RSTs during established sessions indicate forced termination (by firewall/IPS reset action, application crash, or RST injection attack).
ndc.takeaway: TCP RST = Port closed (RST to SYN) or forced connection termination; IPS may inject RST to kill attacks

ndc.q: What can an unusual TTL value in a packet indicate?
opts: The packet has high priority
Possible OS fingerprinting, traceroute activity, TTL manipulation (evasion), or that the packet traversed unexpected hops
The packet is encrypted
The packet is on a high-speed link
ndc.expl: Different OS set different initial TTL values (Windows=128, Linux=64, Cisco=255). Unexpected TTL values may indicate OS fingerprinting attempts, TTL-based evasion (sending packets that expire before IDS but reach target), or unusual routing.
ndc.takeaway: TTL values: Windows=128, Linux=64, Cisco=255; unexpected TTL → OS fingerprint or evasion attempt

ndc.q: What is Wireshark display filter 'tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0' used to identify?
opts: All TCP traffic
Only SYN packets (initial connection requests) — useful for detecting SYN scans or SYN floods
SYN-ACK packets from servers
All TCP handshake packets
ndc.expl: This Wireshark filter captures packets with SYN=1 and ACK=0, which are the initial TCP SYN packets (connection initiation). A large number of SYN packets to many destination ports indicates a port scan; to one destination indicates a SYN flood.
ndc.takeaway: tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0 = Filter SYN-only packets; detect port scans or SYN floods

ndc.q: What is NetFlow analysis?
opts: Analyzing network cable flows
Analyzing metadata (IP pairs, ports, bytes, packets) of network flows exported by routers without capturing full packets
Full packet capture analysis
Analyzing network water cooling flow rates
ndc.expl: NetFlow exports flow metadata (source/destination IP, ports, protocol, bytes, packets, duration) from network devices. Analysis reveals traffic patterns, top talkers, large data transfers, unusual connections, and bandwidth usage without storing full packets.
ndc.takeaway: NetFlow = Traffic metadata analysis (no full packets); reveals patterns, anomalies, top talkers

ndc.q: What is the purpose of protocol decoding in packet analysis?
opts: Decoding encryption protocols
Interpreting raw packet bytes according to protocol specifications to reveal field values and meanings
Decoding network device protocols
Converting packet captures to text format
ndc.expl: Protocol decoding (dissection) interprets raw packet bytes according to protocol RFCs/specifications, identifying each field (source port, sequence number, flags, etc.) and their values. Wireshark's 600+ protocol dissectors provide human-readable analysis.
ndc.takeaway: Protocol Decoding = Parse raw bytes per RFC; reveal all field values in human-readable format

ndc.q: What is packet fragmentation and how can it be used for evasion?
opts: Physically fragmenting network cables
Splitting packets into smaller fragments that individually don't trigger IDS signatures but reassemble into malicious payload at destination
Fragmenting encryption keys across packets
A legitimate network optimization technique only
ndc.expl: IP fragmentation splits large packets. Attackers fragment malicious payloads so no single fragment matches IDS signatures. Only when reassembled at the destination does the attack payload appear. IDS must reassemble fragments to detect such evasion.
ndc.takeaway: Packet Fragmentation Evasion = Split attack payload across fragments; IDS must reassemble to detect

ndc.q: What is a Wireshark capture filter (BPF)?
opts: A filter applied after capture to display specific packets
A Berkeley Packet Filter expression applied during capture to collect only specific traffic, reducing capture file size
A filter for blocking malicious packets
A quality filter for removing corrupted packets
ndc.expl: Wireshark capture filters use BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) syntax applied at the capture stage. Only packets matching the filter are captured, reducing file size and processing overhead. Example: 'host 192.168.1.1 and port 80'.
ndc.takeaway: Wireshark Capture Filter = BPF syntax applied DURING capture; reduces file size (vs display filter after)

ndc.q: What information can port analysis of packet captures reveal?
opts: Physical port condition on network switches
Open ports, running services, potential backdoors, unusual communication patterns, and unauthorized applications
The physical location of network devices
Cable types connected to ports
ndc.expl: Port analysis in packet captures reveals which ports are communicating, identifying running services, detecting unauthorized applications using unusual ports, finding backdoors (shells on high ports), and identifying C2 beaconing to remote servers.
ndc.takeaway: Port Analysis in packets = Find services, unauthorized apps, backdoors, C2 beaconing

ndc.q: What is a PCAP file?
opts: A Palo Alto Certified Application Proxy file
A packet capture file format storing raw network traffic for offline analysis
A PCI Compliance Audit Protocol file
A PHP Content Aggregation Protocol file
ndc.expl: PCAP (Packet CAPture) is the standard file format for storing captured network traffic. Created by tcpdump, readable by Wireshark and most packet analysis tools. Used for offline analysis, forensics, and sharing traffic samples.
ndc.takeaway: PCAP = Packet capture file format; created by tcpdump; analyzed in Wireshark/other tools

ndc.q: What does it mean when you see many ICMP 'Destination Unreachable' messages in a packet capture?
opts: The network has high traffic
Could indicate port scanning (services not running), network misconfigurations, or responses to a UDP flood attack
Normal routing traffic
DHCP configuration errors
ndc.expl: Excessive ICMP Destination Unreachable messages indicate: UDP port scanning (closed ports respond with ICMP), network routing issues, misconfigured hosts, or server responses to UDP flood attacks (server sends ICMP unreachable for each unknown UDP packet).
ndc.takeaway: Many ICMP Dest Unreachable = UDP port scan, routing issues, or server responding to UDP flood

ndc.q: What is a TCP RST injection attack?
opts: Injecting malware via TCP reset packets
Sending forged TCP RST packets to both sides of an established connection to terminate it, performing a session hijacking or DoS
A reset attack on TCP sequence numbers
Injecting exploit code via TCP reset flags
ndc.expl: TCP RST injection sends forged RST packets to both ends of an established TCP session, causing both sides to believe the connection was terminated. Used for: DoS (killing connections), censorship (great firewall), and session disruption.
ndc.takeaway: TCP RST Injection = Forge RST to both parties to kill established sessions; DoS or censorship tool

ndc.q: What is a Virtual Private Network (VPN)?
opts: A private network physically separated from the internet
A secure encrypted tunnel over a public network providing confidentiality and integrity for transmitted data
A network using virtualization technology only
A private MPLS network between corporate sites
ndc.expl: A VPN creates a secure, encrypted tunnel over a public network (internet), allowing remote users and sites to communicate privately. It provides confidentiality (encryption), integrity (hashing), and often authentication.
ndc.takeaway: VPN = Encrypted tunnel over public network; provides confidentiality + integrity + authentication

ndc.q: What is the difference between site-to-site VPN and remote access VPN?
opts: Site-to-site is encrypted; remote access is not
Site-to-site permanently connects two networks; remote access VPN connects individual users to the corporate network
Remote access VPN is for branch offices; site-to-site is for mobile workers
They are the same type of VPN
ndc.expl: Site-to-site VPN creates a permanent encrypted connection between two networks (headquarters and branch office), typically using VPN gateways. Remote access VPN connects individual mobile users or teleworkers to the corporate network on demand.
ndc.takeaway: Site-to-site = Two networks permanently connected; Remote access = Individual users dial-in

ndc.q: What is split tunneling in VPN?
opts: Splitting the VPN tunnel into two parallel connections
A VPN configuration where only specific traffic (corporate network) goes through the VPN tunnel while internet traffic goes directly out
Dividing bandwidth between VPN and non-VPN traffic equally
Splitting encryption between two algorithms
ndc.expl: Split tunneling routes corporate-bound traffic through the VPN tunnel while internet traffic goes directly to the internet (bypassing the VPN). It improves performance but reduces visibility and control over users' internet activity.
ndc.takeaway: Split Tunneling = Corporate traffic → VPN; Internet traffic → direct; saves bandwidth but reduces control

ndc.q: What protocols does IPsec use for security services?
opts: SSL and TLS
AH (Authentication Header) and ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)
GRE and L2TP
SSH and SFTP
ndc.expl: IPsec uses two protocols: AH (Authentication Header) provides authentication and integrity but not encryption. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) provides encryption, authentication, and integrity. ESP is more commonly used as it provides confidentiality.
ndc.takeaway: IPsec: AH = Auth + Integrity (no encryption); ESP = Auth + Integrity + Encryption (commonly used)

ndc.q: What is IKE (Internet Key Exchange) in IPsec?
opts: A key management interface for encryption devices
The protocol that establishes and manages Security Associations (SAs) and negotiates encryption keys for IPsec
An internet encryption key algorithm
A hardware key storage interface
ndc.expl: IKE (Internet Key Exchange) is the protocol used to establish IPsec Security Associations (SAs). IKE Phase 1 authenticates peers and creates a secure channel; IKE Phase 2 negotiates IPsec SA parameters (encryption, hash, lifetime).
ndc.takeaway: IKE = IPsec key exchange protocol; Phase 1 = secure channel; Phase 2 = negotiate IPsec SA

ndc.q: What is a Security Association (SA) in IPsec?
opts: A formal agreement between security vendors
A one-way relationship between VPN peers defining parameters (algorithm, key, SPI) for securing a specific traffic flow
An association of security protocols
A security zone association in a firewall
ndc.expl: An IPsec SA is a unidirectional agreement between peers specifying the security protocol (ESP/AH), algorithm, key, and SPI (Security Parameter Index). Bidirectional communication requires two SAs. Multiple SAs can exist for different traffic flows.
ndc.takeaway: IPsec SA = Unidirectional; defines protocol, algorithm, key, SPI; bidirectional needs 2 SAs

ndc.q: What is the Diffie-Hellman (DH) algorithm used for in VPN?
opts: Encrypting VPN tunnel traffic
Securely exchanging cryptographic keys over an insecure channel without prior shared secrets
Authenticating VPN peers
Hashing VPN packets for integrity
ndc.expl: Diffie-Hellman allows two parties to establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel without transmitting the secret itself. The derived key is used to encrypt the IPsec session. DH groups (DH5, DH14, DH19) define key strength.
ndc.takeaway: Diffie-Hellman = Securely exchange encryption keys over insecure channel; DH14+ recommended

ndc.q: What is a GRE tunnel and what is its limitation?
opts: A secure encrypted tunnel; limitation is only supporting IPv4
A generic encapsulation protocol supporting multi-protocol tunneling; limitation is no built-in encryption or authentication
A ground routing encapsulation for wireless networks
A Gigabit Routing Extension for high-speed networks
ndc.expl: GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation) encapsulates various Layer 3 protocols for transport over IP networks. It is often combined with IPsec (GRE over IPsec) as GRE provides no encryption or authentication on its own.
ndc.takeaway: GRE = Multi-protocol encapsulation tunnel; NO encryption — combine with IPsec for security

ndc.q: What is L2TP/IPsec?
opts: A Layer 2 tunneling protocol paired with IPsec for security
L2TP (Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol) provides the tunnel; IPsec provides encryption and authentication for the L2TP traffic
A VPN for Layer 2 switches only
An MPLS VPN solution
ndc.expl: L2TP alone provides no encryption or authentication. L2TP/IPsec combines L2TP (which tunnels PPP frames) with IPsec (which provides encryption and authentication). The combination creates a secure Layer 2 VPN commonly used for Windows remote access.
ndc.takeaway: L2TP = Tunneling only; L2TP/IPsec = L2TP + IPsec encryption; common Windows remote access VPN

ndc.q: What are the advantages of SSL VPN over IPsec VPN?
opts: SSL VPN is more secure than IPsec VPN
SSL VPN requires only a web browser (no client software), traverses firewalls on port 443, and is easier for non-technical users
IPsec VPN is deprecated in favor of SSL VPN
SSL VPN provides better performance than IPsec VPN
ndc.expl: SSL VPN advantages: clientless mode works via web browser, uses HTTPS (port 443) which traverses most firewalls, easier deployment for remote users, and granular access control per application. IPsec requires client software but provides full network access.
ndc.takeaway: SSL VPN = Browser-based, port 443, no client needed; IPsec = client software, full network access

ndc.q: What is DMVPN (Dynamic Multipoint VPN)?
opts: A Cisco VPN supporting dynamic dial-up connections
A Cisco technology enabling scalable, dynamic hub-and-spoke VPN using NHRP, IPsec, and mGRE for spoke-to-spoke tunnels
A dynamic multi-vendor VPN protocol
A VPN with multiple encryption algorithms
ndc.expl: DMVPN uses three protocols: mGRE (allows multiple tunnels from one interface), NHRP (hub maintains a mapping database for spoke addresses), and IPsec (encrypts traffic). Spokes can dynamically communicate directly with each other after initial hub resolution.
ndc.takeaway: DMVPN = Hub-spoke VPN with dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels using NHRP + mGRE + IPsec

ndc.q: What is VPN performance tuning?
opts: Upgrading VPN hardware for better speed
Optimizing VPN configurations (algorithms, MTU, split tunneling, load balancing, hardware acceleration) to improve throughput and reduce latency
Testing VPN performance with speed tests only
Tuning TCP parameters for VPN connections
ndc.expl: VPN performance tuning involves: selecting efficient cipher suites (AES-128 vs AES-256), optimizing MTU size (avoiding fragmentation), enabling hardware crypto acceleration, implementing split tunneling, load balancing across multiple gateways, and monitoring tunnel stability.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Tuning = Efficient ciphers + optimal MTU + hardware acceleration + split tunneling + load balancing

ndc.q: What is MTU and why is it important for VPN?
opts: Maximum Tunnel Utility — a VPN performance metric
Maximum Transmission Unit — the largest packet size supported; VPN adds overhead headers, requiring MTU adjustment to prevent fragmentation
Minimum Transfer Unit — the smallest VPN packet
Multi-Tunnel Utilization — a VPN load balancing parameter
ndc.expl: MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) is the largest packet size a network link can carry. IPsec/VPN adds protocol headers (ESP, AH, GRE), reducing effective payload size. If packets exceed MTU, fragmentation occurs, degrading performance.
ndc.takeaway: MTU for VPN = Reduce by VPN overhead (IPsec ESP adds ~50 bytes); typical IPsec MTU = 1438 bytes

ndc.q: What is a VPN concentrator?
opts: A device that compresses VPN traffic
A dedicated device handling many concurrent VPN connections, providing scalable VPN termination for remote access
A hub that concentrates multiple VPN tunnels into one
A device that monitors VPN performance
ndc.expl: A VPN concentrator is a dedicated device (or function on a firewall) optimized for handling large numbers of simultaneous VPN connections. It handles encryption, decryption, authentication, and tunnel management for remote access VPNs at scale.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Concentrator = Dedicated device for many simultaneous VPN connections (Cisco ASA, VPN appliances)

ndc.q: What VPN error is indicated by 'Phase 1 negotiation failure'?
opts: The VPN tunnel data transmission failed
IPsec IKE Phase 1 failed — peers cannot agree on authentication method, encryption, or hash algorithm parameters
The VPN client software crashed
A network routing failure prevented VPN establishment
ndc.expl: IKE Phase 1 failure occurs when peers cannot agree on: authentication method (pre-shared key vs certificates), encryption algorithm (AES/3DES), hash algorithm (SHA/MD5), DH group, or lifetime settings. Also caused by mismatched pre-shared keys or certificate issues.
ndc.takeaway: Phase 1 failure = Mismatched auth method, encryption, hash, or DH group; check IKE policy match

ndc.q: What is Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) in VPN?
opts: A VPN that never loses packets (perfect forwarding)
A property ensuring that session keys cannot be derived from long-term private keys, so past session keys cannot be compromised even if the private key is later exposed
A forward error correction mechanism for VPN packets
A security feature preventing VPN session replay
ndc.expl: PFS ensures each VPN session generates independent keys using ephemeral Diffie-Hellman. Even if an attacker later compromises the long-term private key, past sessions cannot be decrypted because each session used a unique, non-persistent key.
ndc.takeaway: PFS = Each session generates independent key; compromise of long-term key doesn't expose past sessions

ndc.q: What does 'hairpinning' mean in VPN context?
opts: Bending VPN cables for compact routing
VPN traffic from remote users being routed through the corporate VPN gateway to reach internet resources, providing centralized security inspection
A VPN error where traffic loops back
A technique for improving VPN latency
ndc.expl: VPN hairpinning (also called full tunnel or hub-and-spoke routing) routes all remote user internet traffic through the corporate VPN gateway before going to the internet. This provides centralized inspection and security but increases latency and bandwidth requirements.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Hairpinning = All traffic (including internet) flows through corporate VPN for centralized inspection

ndc.q: What is a VPN kill switch?
opts: An emergency shutdown for VPN hardware
A security feature that blocks all internet traffic if the VPN connection drops, preventing data leakage outside the VPN tunnel
A button to reset VPN configuration
A timer that kills idle VPN sessions
ndc.expl: A VPN kill switch (network lock) monitors the VPN connection and immediately blocks all internet traffic if the VPN disconnects. This prevents IP address leakage or unencrypted data transmission if the VPN connection unexpectedly fails.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Kill Switch = Blocks ALL internet traffic if VPN drops; prevents IP/data leakage

ndc.q: What is the purpose of VPN authentication methods?
opts: To authenticate the VPN device hardware
To verify the identity of VPN peers/users before establishing a tunnel, preventing unauthorized access
To authenticate encrypted packets in transit
To verify software license compliance
ndc.expl: VPN authentication verifies that connecting parties are who they claim to be. Methods include: pre-shared keys (PSK — simplest), digital certificates (X.509 — most secure for site-to-site), and username/password with MFA (for remote access VPN).
ndc.takeaway: VPN Auth methods: PSK (pre-shared key, simple), Certificates (X.509, most secure), Username+MFA

ndc.q: What is the purpose of VPN tunneling?
opts: Creating physical underground network paths
Encapsulating one network protocol within another to carry it transparently across a network that might not natively support it
Tunneling through corporate firewalls using HTTPS
Creating network shortcuts for faster routing
ndc.expl: VPN tunneling encapsulates data packets within another protocol (GRE, ESP, L2TP) for transport across networks. The inner packet is treated as data by the outer protocol, allowing non-routable protocols or encrypted traffic to traverse the internet.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Tunneling = Encapsulate inner packet in outer protocol; transport across incompatible networks

ndc.q: What does 'crypto map' configure in Cisco IOS IPsec VPN?
opts: A diagram of all VPN connections
A set of parameters defining IPsec transform sets, access lists (interesting traffic), and peer addresses applied to an interface
The cryptographic algorithm mapping for routing protocols
A map of all encryption keys in the organization
ndc.expl: A Cisco crypto map binds together: the ACL defining interesting traffic, the IPsec transform set (algorithms), the peer IP address, and optionally PFS settings. Applied to an interface, it triggers IPsec processing when matching traffic is detected.
ndc.takeaway: Crypto map = Binds ACL (interesting traffic) + Transform set + Peer IP; applied to interface

ndc.q: What is Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) and how does it differ from traditional VPN?
opts: A VPN using zero-overhead encryption
ZTNA grants access per-application based on identity/context verification; traditional VPN grants full network access after one authentication
A VPN requiring zero passwords
A network with no trusted zones
ndc.expl: ZTNA grants access to specific applications based on continuous identity, device posture, and context verification — never granting broad network access. Traditional VPN authenticates once and grants full network access, creating lateral movement risk if compromised.
ndc.takeaway: ZTNA = Per-app access based on identity+context; VPN = Full network access after one auth (less secure)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of NAT-T (NAT Traversal) in IPsec?
opts: Translating IP addresses for IPsec packets
Encapsulating IPsec ESP packets in UDP to traverse NAT devices that would otherwise break IPsec AH/ESP
A traversal protocol for firewall rules
Translating NAT tables for VPN gateways
ndc.expl: NAT breaks IPsec AH (modifies IP headers) and ESP integrity checks. NAT-T encapsulates IPsec ESP packets inside UDP port 4500, allowing them to traverse NAT devices without breaking IPsec integrity checks. IKE detects NAT and enables NAT-T automatically.
ndc.takeaway: NAT-T = Wrap ESP in UDP port 4500 to traverse NAT; IKE detects NAT and enables automatically

ndc.q: What does 'show crypto ipsec sa' command show on a Cisco router?
opts: IPsec security association memory usage
Status of IPsec SAs including packets encrypted/decrypted, current state, and transform set details
All configured crypto maps
IKE Phase 1 SA status
ndc.expl: 'show crypto ipsec sa' displays active IPsec Security Associations including: packets encrypted/decrypted, bytes processed, SA lifetime remaining, transform set in use, and local/remote proxy identities. Used for VPN troubleshooting and verification.
ndc.takeaway: show crypto ipsec sa = Active IPsec SA details: packets, bytes, state, transform, lifetime remaining

ndc.q: What is OpenVPN and what protocol does it use?
opts: An open firewall policy management platform
An open-source VPN solution that uses SSL/TLS for key exchange and can run over TCP or UDP (commonly UDP 1194)
An open-source version of IPsec
A protocol for open network peering
ndc.expl: OpenVPN is an open-source SSL/TLS-based VPN solution. It uses SSL/TLS for key exchange and authentication, with data encryption using OpenSSL. It runs over UDP (default port 1194) or TCP and is cross-platform (Windows, Linux, macOS, mobile).
ndc.takeaway: OpenVPN = Open-source SSL/TLS VPN; UDP 1194 (default) or TCP; cross-platform

ndc.q: What is WireGuard VPN?
opts: A wire-based secure physical connection standard
A modern, lean VPN protocol using state-of-the-art cryptography (ChaCha20, Curve25519) designed for simplicity and performance
A wireless security guard protocol
An enterprise VPN product by a vendor called Wire
ndc.expl: WireGuard is a modern VPN protocol with only ~4,000 lines of code (vs OpenVPN's ~400,000). It uses ChaCha20 for encryption, Curve25519 for key exchange, Poly1305 for authentication, and BLAKE2 for hashing. It is faster and simpler than IPsec/OpenVPN.
ndc.takeaway: WireGuard = Modern fast VPN; ChaCha20 + Curve25519; minimal code (~4K lines); simpler than IPsec

ndc.q: What is a VPN split DNS?
opts: Using different DNS servers for different VPN tunnels
A configuration where DNS queries for internal domains resolve via internal DNS over VPN while public domains use local ISP DNS
Splitting the DNS database across multiple servers
A security feature encrypting VPN DNS queries
ndc.expl: Split DNS (with split tunneling) uses internal DNS servers via VPN for corporate domain queries (resolving internal hostnames/IPs) while routing public domain DNS queries to the local ISP's DNS without going through the VPN tunnel.
ndc.takeaway: Split DNS = Internal domains → VPN DNS server; public domains → local ISP DNS (with split tunneling)

ndc.q: What is a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security?
opts: A zone with no security controls
A network segment between the internal network and the internet that hosts public-facing services with controlled access from both sides
A backup network for disaster recovery
A network segment with maximum security restrictions
ndc.expl: A DMZ is a network segment that hosts public-facing services (web, email, DNS, FTP servers) positioned between the internet and the internal network. It allows external users to access services while protecting the internal network from direct exposure.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ = Network segment hosting public services between internet and internal network

ndc.q: What services are typically placed in a DMZ?
opts: Internal databases and file servers
Web servers, email servers, DNS servers, FTP servers, and reverse proxies serving external users
Domain controllers and authentication servers
Internal application servers and development systems
ndc.expl: DMZ hosts services needing internet accessibility: web servers (HTTP/HTTPS), email servers (SMTP), DNS servers (for external zones), FTP servers, VPN gateways, and reverse proxies. Internal servers (databases, AD) should never be in the DMZ.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ services: Web, Email (SMTP), External DNS, FTP, VPN gateway, Reverse proxy

ndc.q: What is the traffic flow policy from internal network to DMZ?
opts: All traffic from internal to DMZ is blocked
Traffic from internal to DMZ is selectively permitted based on service requirements and inspected
All internal traffic is freely allowed to DMZ
Internal traffic to DMZ is encrypted only
ndc.expl: Internal-to-DMZ traffic is selectively permitted and inspected. Internal users may need to access management interfaces or pull data from DMZ servers. Return traffic from DMZ to internal is normally blocked unless tracking established sessions.
ndc.takeaway: Internal → DMZ: Selectively permitted and inspected; DMZ → Internal: Normally blocked

ndc.q: What is the traffic flow policy from internet (outside) to DMZ?
opts: All internet traffic is blocked at the DMZ
Traffic from internet to DMZ is selectively permitted for specific services (HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, DNS)
Internet traffic is freely allowed into the DMZ
Only encrypted internet traffic reaches the DMZ
ndc.expl: Internet-to-DMZ traffic is selectively permitted for services hosted in the DMZ (HTTP/HTTPS to web server, SMTP to email server, DNS queries). Only necessary service ports are opened; all other inbound internet traffic is denied.
ndc.takeaway: Internet → DMZ: Selectively permitted for hosted services (HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, DNS only)

ndc.q: What is the traffic flow policy from DMZ to internal network?
opts: DMZ traffic to internal is freely allowed
DMZ-to-internal traffic is normally blocked or very restrictively controlled
DMZ to internal is allowed for all established connections
Only administrative traffic from DMZ reaches internal
ndc.expl: Traffic from DMZ to the internal network is normally blocked. If compromised, DMZ servers must not be able to reach internal systems. Exceptions may be specific database connections (HTTPS from web to internal DB) with strict firewall rules.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ → Internal: BLOCKED (most restrictive); if compromised DMZ cannot reach internal network

ndc.q: What is a virtual host?
opts: A host using virtual reality interfaces
A web server configuration hosting multiple websites/domains on a single physical server with a single IP address
A virtualized network host in a VM
A host name for VPN connections
ndc.expl: Virtual hosting allows multiple domains (e.g., site1.com, site2.com) to be served from a single physical server and IP address. HTTP/1.1's Host header lets the web server determine which virtual host to serve. Reduces hardware costs.
ndc.takeaway: Virtual Host = Multiple domains on one physical server; uses Host header to select correct site

ndc.q: What is a name-based virtual host?
opts: A virtual host identified by its hostname only
A web server that hosts multiple domains on a single IP, using the HTTP Host header to route to the correct virtual host
A virtual host requiring a dedicated IP per domain
A host with a virtual domain name only
ndc.expl: Name-based virtual hosting uses the HTTP/1.1 Host header to route requests to the correct website on a single IP address. All domains share one IP; the web server distinguishes them by the Host header value in each HTTP request.
ndc.takeaway: Name-based Virtual Host = One IP, multiple domains; HTTP Host header determines which site to serve

ndc.q: What security risk does a DMZ not fully mitigate?
opts: External attackers accessing web servers
Attackers compromising a DMZ server and using it as a pivot point to attack internal systems if DMZ-to-internal rules are not strict enough
Denial of service attacks on DMZ services
Port scanning of DMZ services
ndc.expl: If a DMZ server is compromised, inadequate DMZ-to-internal firewall rules can allow attackers to use the compromised server as a launch pad for attacking internal systems (lateral movement). Strict DMZ-to-internal rules and internal segmentation mitigate this.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ risk = Compromised DMZ server used as pivot to attack internal; strict DMZ→internal rules essential

ndc.q: What is the purpose of placing a reverse proxy in the DMZ?
opts: To proxy requests from DMZ servers to the internet
To receive external requests, inspect/filter them, and forward only legitimate requests to internal application servers
To replace the need for a firewall
To provide load balancing only
ndc.expl: A reverse proxy in the DMZ acts as an intermediary for inbound requests. It terminates client connections, inspects HTTP traffic, strips dangerous headers, and forwards clean requests to internal application servers. Internal servers are never directly exposed.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy in DMZ = Terminates client connections; internal servers never directly internet-exposed

ndc.q: What is a bastion host in DMZ architecture?
opts: The most secure internal server
A highly hardened, internet-exposed host in the DMZ providing specific services while minimizing attack surface
A backup host for DMZ services
The firewall separating DMZ from the internet
ndc.expl: A bastion host is a hardened system purposely exposed to the internet providing specific services (SSH, web, DNS). All unnecessary services are disabled, it's minimally configured (only required software), and it's intensively monitored.
ndc.takeaway: Bastion Host = Hardened internet-exposed host in DMZ; minimal services, maximum monitoring

ndc.q: What is a multi-homed host in DMZ?
opts: A host with multiple home directories
A host with multiple network interfaces connected to different network segments (e.g., internet, DMZ, internal)
A host owned by multiple organizations
A server with multiple home pages
ndc.expl: A multi-homed host has network interfaces connected to multiple networks (internet, DMZ, internal). It can act as a router/firewall between them. Security risk: if IP forwarding is enabled without proper controls, it bypasses firewall protection.
ndc.takeaway: Multi-homed Host = Multiple NICs on different networks; must disable IP forwarding or control carefully

ndc.q: What is the security implication of having a database server in the DMZ?
opts: Database servers should always be in the DMZ for performance
Database servers should never be in the DMZ — they contain sensitive data and should be in a protected internal segment inaccessible from the internet
Database servers in DMZ require special encryption only
No security implication as long as the database is encrypted
ndc.expl: Database servers should be placed in an internal protected segment, not the DMZ. DMZ servers can be compromised from the internet. If the database is in the DMZ, sensitive data is at direct risk. Web servers in DMZ should only access databases via tightly controlled internal rules.
ndc.takeaway: DB servers = NEVER in DMZ; place in protected internal segment; web DMZ → DB internal via strict rules

ndc.q: What are virtual machines used for in DMZ architecture?
opts: Replacing physical firewalls in the DMZ
Hosting multiple DMZ services on a single physical server using virtualization, with each VM isolated in its own network segment
Providing virtual users for DMZ services
Creating virtual IP addresses for DMZ services
ndc.expl: VMs in DMZ architecture allow multiple services (web, email, DNS) to run on one physical server, each isolated in its own VM. Hypervisor-level network separation prevents one compromised VM from affecting others. Reduces hardware costs while maintaining isolation.
ndc.takeaway: VMs in DMZ = Multiple isolated services on one server; hypervisor prevents cross-VM compromise

ndc.q: What is a screened host firewall?
opts: A single firewall with a screening router
An architecture combining a packet-filtering router (screening) with an application gateway behind it, providing two inspection layers
A firewall that screens all inbound host traffic
A host-based firewall with packet screening
ndc.expl: A screened host firewall uses a packet-filtering router to screen initial traffic, followed by an application gateway (bastion host) providing deeper inspection. Two layers: the router filters coarsely by IP/port; the proxy inspects application-layer content.
ndc.takeaway: Screened Host Firewall = Packet-filter router + Application gateway (two security inspection layers)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of placing a WAF in the DMZ?
opts: To provide web acceleration in the DMZ
To inspect and filter HTTP/HTTPS traffic to web servers in the DMZ, blocking web application attacks before they reach the web server
To terminate VPN connections in the DMZ
To provide DNS services in the DMZ
ndc.expl: Placing a WAF in the DMZ (or in front of DMZ web servers) provides application-layer inspection of all incoming web traffic. The WAF blocks SQLi, XSS, and other web attacks before they reach the web server, adding a dedicated application security layer.
ndc.takeaway: WAF in DMZ = Application-layer web traffic inspection; blocks SQLi/XSS before reaching web server

ndc.q: What is a reverse proxy?
opts: A proxy that hides client identity from servers
A server sitting in front of web servers that receives client requests on their behalf, providing security, load balancing, and caching
A proxy that reverses traffic direction
A backward-compatible proxy server
ndc.expl: A reverse proxy sits between internet clients and internal web servers. Clients connect to the reverse proxy (thinking it's the actual server); the proxy forwards requests to appropriate backend servers. It provides load balancing, SSL termination, caching, and WAF functionality.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy = Sits in front of servers; clients connect to it; provides LB, SSL, caching, security

ndc.q: What is the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
opts: Forward proxy = for servers; reverse proxy = for clients
Forward proxy = serves clients (hides client from internet); reverse proxy = serves servers (hides servers from internet)
They are identical in function
Forward proxy is hardware; reverse proxy is software
ndc.expl: Forward proxy acts on behalf of clients (hides client IP from internet, enables web filtering/caching). Reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers (hides server IPs from clients, provides load balancing, SSL termination, WAF).
ndc.takeaway: Forward Proxy = Acts for clients (hides them); Reverse Proxy = Acts for servers (hides them)

ndc.q: What security benefits does a reverse proxy provide?
opts: It encrypts all traffic between proxy and clients
It hides internal server IPs, provides SSL termination, WAF filtering, DDoS protection, and enforces access controls
It authenticates all clients with certificates
It prevents all web attacks automatically
ndc.expl: Reverse proxy security benefits: hides internal server IPs (attackers can't directly reach servers), SSL/TLS termination (centralized certificate management), WAF integration (filter attacks), DDoS protection (rate limiting, challenge pages), and centralized authentication.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy security: Hides server IPs + SSL termination + WAF + DDoS protection + centralized auth

ndc.q: What is SSL/TLS termination at a reverse proxy?
opts: Terminating all SSL connections from the reverse proxy
The reverse proxy decrypts HTTPS traffic from clients, inspects it, then re-encrypts (or sends plaintext) to backend servers
Blocking all SSL traffic at the proxy
Terminating SSL certificates at the proxy for archiving
ndc.expl: SSL termination at the reverse proxy decrypts client HTTPS traffic at the proxy level, relieving backend servers of encryption overhead. The proxy can inspect plaintext, apply WAF rules, then either re-encrypt (SSL re-encryption) or send plaintext to backends on the trusted internal network.
ndc.takeaway: SSL Termination = Proxy decrypts HTTPS; backend receives plaintext (or re-encrypted); reduces server load

ndc.q: What is load balancing in reverse proxy?
opts: Balancing the weight of proxy hardware
Distributing client requests across multiple backend servers to improve availability and performance
Balancing security rules between multiple proxies
Load testing backend servers via the proxy
ndc.expl: Load balancing distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers using algorithms (round robin, least connections, IP hash). This improves performance, prevents single servers from overloading, and provides high availability if one server fails.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Load Balancing = Distribute requests across backends; round robin, least connections

ndc.q: What is caching in reverse proxy?
opts: Storing proxy configuration files
Storing copies of frequently requested content to serve future identical requests without contacting backend servers, improving performance
Caching SSL certificates for faster HTTPS
Temporarily storing blocked traffic for analysis
ndc.expl: Reverse proxy caching stores copies of web content (images, HTML, CSS, API responses) to serve repeated requests without contacting the backend. This reduces backend load, decreases latency for users, and saves bandwidth.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Caching = Store frequent content locally; reduce backend load and improve response time

ndc.q: What is Nginx and what role does it serve?
opts: A network security scanner
A high-performance web server and reverse proxy known for efficient handling of concurrent connections
A network monitoring tool
A next-generation intrusion detection system
ndc.expl: Nginx (Engine X) is a high-performance, event-driven web server, reverse proxy, and load balancer. It efficiently handles thousands of concurrent connections with low memory usage and is widely used as a reverse proxy in front of application servers.
ndc.takeaway: Nginx = High-performance web server + reverse proxy + load balancer; event-driven architecture

ndc.q: What is HAProxy?
opts: A high-availability security appliance
A highly reliable, open-source TCP/HTTP load balancer and reverse proxy for high availability
A hardware proxy appliance for enterprise use
A security proxy for analyzing HTTP attacks
ndc.expl: HAProxy (High Availability Proxy) is an open-source, high-performance TCP/HTTP load balancer and reverse proxy. It provides advanced load balancing algorithms, health checking, SSL termination, and sticky sessions for enterprise deployments.
ndc.takeaway: HAProxy = Open-source, high-performance load balancer + reverse proxy; advanced HA features

ndc.q: What is a Content Delivery Network (CDN) and how does it relate to reverse proxies?
opts: A network for delivering content securely
A globally distributed reverse proxy network caching and delivering content from edge servers close to users for performance and DDoS protection
A private network for delivering internal content
A content management system for networks
ndc.expl: CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS CloudFront) are globally distributed reverse proxy networks. They cache static content at edge servers worldwide, delivering it from the location closest to the user. They also provide DDoS mitigation and WAF capabilities.
ndc.takeaway: CDN = Global distributed reverse proxy; serves content from nearest edge; DDoS protection + caching

ndc.q: What is reverse proxy caching policy?
opts: Rules defining which administrators can modify proxy configuration
Rules defining which content is cached, for how long (TTL), and cache invalidation conditions
A security policy for content inspection
A policy defining proxy failover behavior
ndc.expl: Reverse proxy caching policies define: what content to cache (static vs dynamic), TTL (Time-To-Live) per content type, cache invalidation rules (when stale content is refreshed), and cache control header handling (Cache-Control, Expires, ETag).
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Cache Policy = Define what to cache, TTL, invalidation rules, cache-control headers

ndc.q: What is sticky session (session persistence) in reverse proxy load balancing?
opts: Sessions that never expire
Routing subsequent requests from a specific client to the same backend server to maintain session state
Sticky cookie authentication sessions
Persistent encrypted sessions between proxy and backend
ndc.expl: Sticky sessions (session affinity) ensure all requests from a specific client go to the same backend server. This is important for stateful applications storing session data locally. Implemented via cookie-based or IP-based affinity.
ndc.takeaway: Sticky Session = Route same client to same backend; required for stateful apps without shared session storage

ndc.q: What is health check in reverse proxy?
opts: Checking the physical health of proxy hardware
Periodic testing of backend servers to detect failures and remove unhealthy servers from the load balancing pool
A security health audit of the proxy
Checking for security vulnerabilities in backend servers
ndc.expl: Health checks are periodic tests (HTTP GET, TCP connection, custom checks) the reverse proxy sends to backend servers. Servers failing health checks are removed from the active pool until they recover, ensuring traffic only goes to healthy servers.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Health Check = Periodic backend tests; failed servers removed from pool automatically

ndc.q: What is reverse proxy authentication?
opts: Proxy authenticating itself to backend servers
The reverse proxy authenticating clients before forwarding requests to backend servers, centralizing authentication
Backend servers authenticating to the proxy
Certificate-based proxy authentication only
ndc.expl: Reverse proxy authentication centralizes user authentication at the proxy level. All clients authenticate to the proxy (via OAuth, SAML, basic auth, certificate) before requests reach backend applications, simplifying application security.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Auth = Centralize authentication at proxy level; backends trust proxy; simplifies app security

ndc.q: What is a reverse proxy policy?
opts: The proxy vendor's usage policy
A set of rules governing how the reverse proxy handles traffic: routing rules, access control, caching, rate limiting, and security filtering
A firewall policy for the proxy server
A network routing policy for proxy servers
ndc.expl: Reverse proxy policies define: request routing rules (which paths go to which backends), access control (IP allowlists, geo-blocking), caching behavior, rate limiting, security filtering (WAF rules), and SSL/TLS configuration.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Policy = Routing + Access control + Caching + Rate limiting + WAF + SSL config

ndc.q: What is X-Forwarded-For header in reverse proxy?
opts: A header forwarding requests to multiple backends
An HTTP header added by the reverse proxy containing the original client's IP address so backend servers can identify the real source
A forwarding security certificate header
A header for load balancer health checks
ndc.expl: When a reverse proxy forwards requests, the backend sees the proxy's IP as the source. X-Forwarded-For (XFF) header contains the original client IP so backends can: log real client IPs, apply geo-restrictions, or perform user-based rate limiting.
ndc.takeaway: X-Forwarded-For = HTTP header carrying original client IP through reverse proxy to backend server

ndc.q: What is rate limiting in reverse proxy?
opts: Limiting the proxy's throughput rate
Restricting requests from a single client/IP to a maximum rate, preventing abuse, scraping, and brute force attacks
Limiting proxy configuration change frequency
Rate controlling backend server connections
ndc.expl: Rate limiting at the reverse proxy caps requests per IP, user, or endpoint per time window. This prevents API abuse, web scraping, brute force password attacks, and helps mitigate DDoS attacks before they reach backend servers.
ndc.takeaway: Reverse Proxy Rate Limiting = Cap requests per IP/user/endpoint; prevents brute force and API abuse

ndc.q: What is IP allowlisting (whitelisting) in reverse proxy?
opts: Allowing the proxy to use only whitelisted IP ranges
Restricting access to the application through the proxy to only specific trusted IP addresses or ranges
A list of allowed proxy IP addresses for backend access
Whitelisting SSL certificates at the proxy
ndc.expl: IP allowlisting at the reverse proxy restricts access to the application to only specific trusted IP addresses or ranges (e.g., only the corporate IP range, specific partner IPs). All other source IPs are blocked at the proxy level.
ndc.takeaway: IP Allowlist (Whitelist) in Reverse Proxy = Only specified IPs can access; all others blocked

ndc.q: What is geo-blocking in reverse proxy?
opts: Blocking geolocation header information
Restricting or allowing traffic based on the geographic location of the client's IP address
Blocking proxied traffic to geographic regions
A physical blocking mechanism in proxy hardware
ndc.expl: Geo-blocking uses IP geolocation databases to determine the client's country/region and block requests from restricted locations. Used to enforce regulatory compliance, reduce attack surface from high-risk regions, or enforce content licensing.
ndc.takeaway: Geo-blocking = Block/allow traffic by client's geographic location using IP geolocation databases

ndc.q: What is TLS mutual authentication (mTLS) in reverse proxy?
opts: Two-way SSL for proxy management
Both the client and server authenticate each other with certificates; the reverse proxy validates client certificates before forwarding requests
TLS authentication using two different algorithms
Mutually agreed TLS cipher suites only
ndc.expl: mTLS (mutual TLS) requires both the client to present a certificate to the reverse proxy AND the proxy to present its certificate to the client. Used for B2B API security and Zero Trust environments to authenticate machine-to-machine communications.
ndc.takeaway: mTLS = Both client and server present certificates; reverse proxy validates client cert before forwarding

ndc.q: Which tool is widely used as a reverse proxy and API gateway in modern microservices architectures?
opts: Apache HTTP Server
Kong, Envoy, or Traefik — purpose-built for microservices API gateway and reverse proxy needs
Microsoft IIS
Squid Proxy
ndc.expl: Modern microservices use purpose-built API gateways like Kong (open-source, extensible), Envoy (service mesh proxy from Lyft), and Traefik (cloud-native, Docker/Kubernetes-native). They provide routing, auth, rate limiting, observability, and service mesh capabilities.
ndc.takeaway: Modern Reverse Proxy/API GW: Kong, Envoy, Traefik — designed for microservices and Kubernetes

ndc.q: What is the purpose of multi-factor authentication (MFA)?
opts: Using multiple passwords for a single account
Requiring two or more verification factors (something you know, have, or are) to reduce unauthorized access risk
Authenticating to multiple systems at once
Using multiple authentication servers simultaneously
ndc.expl: MFA requires users to provide two or more factors: something you know (password), something you have (OTP token, smartphone), or something you are (biometric). Even if a password is stolen, attackers cannot authenticate without the second factor.
ndc.takeaway: MFA = 2+ factors: Know (password) + Have (token) + Are (biometric); defeats stolen passwords

ndc.q: What is OWASP and why is it important?
opts: A government security agency
The Open Web Application Security Project — a nonprofit providing free security resources including the Top 10 web vulnerability list
An international security certification body
A security product vendor consortium
ndc.expl: OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) is a nonprofit producing free, open security resources. The OWASP Top 10 (most critical web vulnerabilities) is the de-facto standard for web application security awareness and drives WAF rules worldwide.
ndc.takeaway: OWASP = Open nonprofit; OWASP Top 10 = Most critical web vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, etc.)

ndc.q: What is a CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) identifier?
opts: A classification value for exploitation difficulty
A standardized identifier (CVE-YEAR-NUMBER) assigned to publicly known security vulnerabilities for tracking and reference
A cryptographic validation engine
A security vendor rating for vulnerabilities
ndc.expl: CVE IDs (e.g., CVE-2021-44228 for Log4Shell) are standardized identifiers for publicly disclosed vulnerabilities. Assigned by MITRE, they allow security tools, advisories, and teams to reference the same vulnerability with a common identifier.
ndc.takeaway: CVE = Standardized vulnerability ID (CVE-YEAR-NUMBER); e.g., CVE-2021-44228 = Log4Shell

ndc.q: What is CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)?
opts: A system for scoring vulnerability scan performance
A standardized framework for rating severity of security vulnerabilities on a 0-10 scale
A compliance validation scoring system
A CVE vendor scoring system
ndc.expl: CVSS provides a numerical score (0-10) reflecting the severity of a vulnerability based on metrics: Attack Vector, Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction, Scope, Confidentiality/Integrity/Availability Impact. Critical=9.0-10, High=7.0-8.9.
ndc.takeaway: CVSS = 0-10 vulnerability severity score; Critical(9-10), High(7-8.9), Medium(4-6.9), Low(0-3.9)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of vulnerability scanning?
opts: Testing systems by actively exploiting vulnerabilities
Automatically identifying known security weaknesses in systems, networks, and applications using a vulnerability database
Scanning physical network devices for damage
Monitoring network traffic for vulnerabilities
ndc.expl: Vulnerability scanners (Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys) automatically probe systems for known vulnerabilities by comparing findings against CVE/vulnerability databases. Unlike penetration testing, they identify but do not exploit vulnerabilities.
ndc.takeaway: Vulnerability Scanner = Auto-identify known weaknesses (Nessus, OpenVAS); does NOT exploit

ndc.q: What is patch management?
opts: Applying physical patches to network cables
The process of systematically acquiring, testing, and deploying software updates to fix vulnerabilities and bugs
Managing network access patches and policies
Patching security policies for new threats
ndc.expl: Patch management involves identifying, acquiring, testing, and deploying security patches and updates to software and systems. Unpatched vulnerabilities are the most common attack vector. Regular patching reduces attack surface significantly.
ndc.takeaway: Patch Management = Identify → Test → Deploy security updates; unpatched systems = major attack vector

ndc.q: What is data exfiltration in cybersecurity?
opts: Accidentally deleting critical data
The unauthorized transfer of data from an organization to an external destination by an attacker
Exfiltrating malware from infected systems
Filtering data for extraneous information
ndc.expl: Data exfiltration is the unauthorized transfer of sensitive data outside an organization's network by an attacker. Methods include: DNS tunneling, HTTPS to external servers, email, USB, and cloud storage. DLP tools and egress filtering detect/prevent exfiltration.
ndc.takeaway: Data Exfiltration = Unauthorized transfer of org data to external attacker; DLP and egress filtering prevent

ndc.q: What is a rainbow table attack?
opts: An attack using colorful visual phishing techniques
A precomputed table of hash values used to reverse-engineer passwords from their hashes
A multi-vector attack using multiple techniques simultaneously
A table-based SQL injection attack
ndc.expl: Rainbow tables contain precomputed pairs of plaintext passwords and their hash values. Attackers compare stolen password hashes against the table to find matches instantly. Mitigated by salting hashes (adding random data before hashing, making precomputation infeasible).
ndc.takeaway: Rainbow Table = Precomputed hash→password pairs; mitigated by password salting before hashing

ndc.q: What is a security token?
opts: A physical key for data centers
A hardware or software device generating time-based or event-based one-time passwords for MFA
An encryption key stored in memory
A network access token for VPN authentication
ndc.expl: Security tokens (hardware: RSA SecurID, YubiKey; software: Google Authenticator, Authy) generate one-time passwords (OTP) for MFA. TOTP (Time-based OTP) generates codes every 30 seconds; HOTP (Event-based) generates codes per authentication event.
ndc.takeaway: Security Token = Generates OTP for MFA; TOTP (30s interval) or HOTP (event-based); YubiKey, Authy

ndc.q: What is a DMZ in terms of security policy enforcement?
opts: A zone with no security policy
A zone where all three-network traffic flows are controlled by explicit security policies differentiating internal, DMZ, and external trust levels
A zone managed by the DMZ security team only
A zone where standard security policies do not apply
ndc.expl: DMZ security policy enforces different trust levels: external is least trusted (internet), DMZ has medium trust (public services), internal is most trusted. Policies control all three traffic flows: internet↔DMZ, internal↔DMZ, and internet↔internal (usually blocked).
ndc.takeaway: DMZ policy = Three trust zones: External(low) ↔ DMZ(medium) ↔ Internal(high); all flows explicitly controlled

ndc.q: What is the purpose of an Acceptable Use Policy (AUP)?
opts: A technical policy for firewall configuration
A policy defining acceptable and unacceptable uses of organizational IT resources by employees
An agreement between ISPs about acceptable bandwidth use
A policy for accepting vulnerability reports
ndc.expl: An AUP documents what employees may and may not do with organizational technology resources (internet, email, devices). It sets expectations, provides legal standing for discipline, and informs users of monitoring. Required for BYOD, remote work, and social media policies.
ndc.takeaway: AUP = Policy defining acceptable IT use by employees; legal basis for disciplinary action

ndc.q: What is insider threat?
opts: Threats from former employees only
Security risks posed by current or former employees, contractors, or partners with legitimate access who misuse it maliciously or negligently
External attackers impersonating insiders
Threats to physical security inside buildings
ndc.expl: Insider threats come from people with authorized access: malicious insiders (intentionally steal/sabotage), negligent insiders (accidentally expose data), and compromised insiders (credentials stolen by attackers). They bypass perimeter controls since they already have legitimate access.
ndc.takeaway: Insider Threat = Authorized users misusing access; malicious, negligent, or compromised

ndc.q: What is the purpose of network access control (NAC)?
opts: Controlling physical access to the network room
Enforcing endpoint compliance (patch level, antivirus, OS version) before allowing devices to access the network
Controlling bandwidth for network access
Managing network access credentials
ndc.expl: NAC enforces security policies on devices before granting network access. It checks: OS patch level, antivirus status, encryption, approved software. Non-compliant devices are quarantined or given limited access until remediated. Examples: Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass.
ndc.takeaway: NAC = Check device compliance (patches, AV, OS) before granting network access; quarantine non-compliant

ndc.q: What is a security operations center (SOC)?
opts: A physical room storing security equipment
A centralized team using technology to continuously monitor, detect, analyze, and respond to cybersecurity incidents
A software operations center for security tools
A government security command center
ndc.expl: A SOC is a team of security analysts working 24/7 using SIEM, threat intelligence, IDS/IPS, and other tools to monitor an organization's security posture. They detect, investigate, and respond to security incidents.
ndc.takeaway: SOC = 24/7 team monitoring for threats using SIEM, IDS/IPS, threat intel; detects and responds

ndc.q: What is threat hunting?
opts: Hunting for security vulnerabilities in software
Proactively searching through networks and systems for signs of attackers that evaded automated detection tools
Searching the dark web for threats targeting the organization
Identifying new threat actors on social media
ndc.expl: Threat hunting is a proactive, human-led process where security analysts search for signs of attackers or compromise that automated tools missed. It involves hypothesis-driven investigation using threat intelligence, log analysis, and behavioral analytics.
ndc.takeaway: Threat Hunting = Proactive human-led search for attackers evading automated detection

ndc.q: What is a firewall's 'allow list' (whitelist) approach?
opts: A list of approved firewall vendors
Only explicitly listed IPs, domains, or applications are permitted; everything else is denied by default
A list of IP addresses allowed through without inspection
Whitelisting firewall administrators
ndc.expl: The allowlist approach permits only explicitly specified traffic (IP addresses, domains, applications) and denies everything else. More secure than a blocklist (deny known bad) since unknown traffic is blocked by default — aligns with 'deny by default' principle.
ndc.takeaway: Allowlist = Only specified traffic permitted; everything else denied — secure by default

ndc.q: What does 'stateful firewall' track that a packet filter cannot?
opts: Physical cable connections
The state of TCP connections (SYN, ESTABLISHED, FIN) and UDP pseudo-states to allow only legitimate return traffic
User identity and application data
All packet payloads in memory
ndc.expl: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection state table tracking TCP three-way handshake, ESTABLISHED connections, and termination. This allows them to permit return traffic for established outbound sessions while blocking unsolicited inbound traffic that wasn't preceded by an outbound request.
ndc.takeaway: Stateful Firewall tracks TCP state: SYN→ESTABLISHED→FIN; allows return traffic for known sessions only

ndc.q: What type of attack attempts to bypass firewall rules by using allowed ports?
opts: Port scanning
Port evasion or covert channel attack (tunneling malicious traffic through allowed ports like 80, 443, or DNS)
SYN flood attack
Protocol spoofing
ndc.expl: Attackers tunnel malicious traffic (C2 communication, exfiltration) through ports allowed by firewalls: HTTPS (443), DNS (53), or HTTP (80). Since these ports are typically allowed, traffic passes through. DPI and application-aware firewalls detect this covert channel use.
ndc.takeaway: Port Evasion = Tunnel malicious traffic through allowed ports (443, 53, 80); DPI/NGFW detects it

ndc.q: What is firewall rule ordering and why does it matter?
opts: Organizing firewall rules alphabetically for readability
Rules are evaluated top-to-bottom and the first matching rule wins; incorrect ordering can allow or block unintended traffic
Ordering rules by traffic volume for efficiency
The sequence in which firewall hardware processes packets
ndc.expl: Firewall rules are processed sequentially from top to bottom. The first rule matching the packet is applied and processing stops. An overly broad permit rule early in the list can allow traffic that a specific deny rule below it was meant to block.
ndc.takeaway: Firewall rules: Top-to-bottom evaluation; first match wins; specific rules must come before broad rules

ndc.q: What is the key difference between network IPS and host-based IPS (HIPS)?
opts: NIPS is cheaper than HIPS
NIPS inspects traffic at network segments; HIPS monitors activities on individual hosts (processes, files, registry)
HIPS requires more bandwidth than NIPS
NIPS works for IPv6; HIPS works for IPv4
ndc.expl: NIPS monitors traffic flowing through network segments, inspecting packets for known attack patterns. HIPS runs on individual hosts and monitors local activities: system calls, process behavior, file changes, registry modifications, and local network connections.
ndc.takeaway: NIPS = Network traffic inspection; HIPS = Individual host monitoring (processes, files, system calls)

ndc.q: What is an IDS/IPS evasion technique called 'fragmentation'?
opts: Fragmenting the IDS into multiple instances
Splitting attack payloads into small fragments so no single fragment contains a complete signature match
Fragmenting IP addresses to confuse the IDS
A fragmentation disk attack on IDS storage
ndc.expl: Fragmentation evasion splits malicious payloads across multiple IP fragments. Each individual fragment is too small to match a complete IDS signature. Only when reassembled at the target does the full attack payload appear. IDS must reassemble fragments to detect this.
ndc.takeaway: Fragmentation IDS Evasion = Split payload across fragments; IDS must reassemble to detect

ndc.q: What is TTL manipulation in IDS evasion?
opts: Manipulating TTL for routing optimization
Sending packets with low TTL values that reach the IDS but expire before reaching the target, confusing the IDS about what traffic the target actually receives
Changing TTL to bypass firewall rules
A technique to extend session lifetime
ndc.expl: TTL manipulation exploits differences in how IDS and the target host process TTL. Attacker sends decoy packets with TTL that will expire at the IDS but not reach the target. IDS processes these packets as part of a sequence that the target never sees.
ndc.takeaway: TTL Manipulation IDS Evasion = Decoy packets expire at IDS but not target; confuses IDS reassembly

ndc.q: What is the purpose of an IPS action 'block attacker IP'?
opts: Physically blocking access to the IPS
Automatically adding the attacker's IP to a block list for a specified duration when an attack is detected
Blocking all traffic from the attacked subnet
Disabling the attacker's internet service
ndc.expl: The 'block attacker IP' IPS action adds the source IP of a detected attack to a temporary blocklist. All subsequent traffic from that IP is dropped for a configured duration. Risk: IP spoofing or shared IP could block legitimate traffic (DoS by spoofing).
ndc.takeaway: Block Attacker IP = Temporary IP blocklist after attack detection; risk of DoS via spoofing

ndc.q: What is a Snort rule's 'msg' field used for?
opts: Measuring message size of detected traffic
A human-readable description of the alert displayed when the rule is triggered
The SMTP message identifier for email alerts
A mandatory rule syntax checker field
ndc.expl: The 'msg' option in a Snort rule provides a human-readable description displayed in the alert when the rule matches. Example: 'msg:"SQL Injection Attempt";' — this text appears in IDS logs and alerts, helping analysts understand the detected threat.
ndc.takeaway: Snort msg: = Human-readable alert description displayed when rule triggers; essential for analyst review

ndc.q: What is the Snort 'sid' field?
opts: Source identifier for the attacker IP
A unique numeric signature identifier for each Snort rule used for tracking and referencing
A session identifier for network flows
A severity indicator for rule priority
ndc.expl: The 'sid' (Signature ID) field assigns a unique numeric identifier to each Snort rule. Community rules use SIDs 1-999999; locally created rules use SIDs 1000001+. SIDs are used for rule management, suppression, and tracking rule hits.
ndc.takeaway: Snort sid: = Unique rule ID; local rules use SID 1000001+; community rules 1-999999

ndc.q: What is network baselining in IDS context?
opts: Setting up the baseline network hardware configuration
Documenting normal network traffic patterns (bandwidth, protocols, connections) to identify deviations that indicate potential intrusions
Baseline configuration of IDS hardware
Setting IDS thresholds to baseline sensitivity levels
ndc.expl: Network baselining captures normal traffic patterns (peak/off-peak bandwidth, protocol distribution, top talkers, connection patterns) over time. Anomaly-based IDS compares current traffic against this baseline to detect significant deviations indicating attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Network Baseline = Document normal traffic patterns; anomaly IDS detects deviations from baseline

ndc.q: What does 'dwell time' mean in intrusion context?
opts: How long an IDS takes to process alerts
The time an attacker remains undetected inside a compromised network from initial intrusion to detection
How long a VPN session is active
The duration of a DDoS attack
ndc.expl: Dwell time (mean time to detect, MTTD) is the period between an attacker's initial compromise of a network and detection/discovery. Industry average is months. Reducing dwell time through better detection limits the attacker's ability to cause damage and exfiltrate data.
ndc.takeaway: Dwell Time = Days/months attacker is undetected; reducing it limits damage and data exfiltration

ndc.q: What is a security rule base in firewall management?
opts: The hardware specifications for firewall models
The complete ordered set of firewall rules defining the organization's network traffic security policy
A database of known attack signatures
The base configuration template for new firewalls
ndc.expl: The rule base is the complete ordered collection of firewall rules implementing the organization's security policy. Good rule base management includes: rule cleanup, removing unused rules, proper ordering, documentation, and regular reviews.
ndc.takeaway: Rule Base = Complete ordered firewall rule set; requires regular cleanup, documentation, and review

ndc.q: What is a network tap in IDS deployment?
opts: A water tap analogy for network flow monitoring
A passive hardware device that copies all packets passing through a network link to a monitoring port without affecting the original traffic
A wireless network access point for IDS sensors
A software-based traffic copy mechanism
ndc.expl: A network tap is a passive hardware device placed inline on a network link that copies all passing traffic to a monitoring port without adding latency to the original traffic. Unlike SPAN ports, taps provide complete passive visibility without depending on switch configurations.
ndc.takeaway: Network Tap = Passive hardware copies all traffic to monitoring port; no latency impact, 100% visibility

ndc.q: What is the purpose of IDS/IPS signature updates?
opts: Updating the IDS hardware firmware
Downloading new signatures for recently discovered attacks to maintain current threat detection capability
Updating IDS configuration settings
Refreshing network baselining data
ndc.expl: Regular signature updates ensure IDS/IPS can detect the latest known attacks and malware. Vendors release signature updates as new threats are discovered. Outdated signatures leave known vulnerabilities undetected. Critical for signature-based detection effectiveness.
ndc.takeaway: Signature Updates = Download new attack patterns regularly; outdated signatures = blind spots for new threats

ndc.q: What is application control in NGFW context?
opts: Controlling which applications run on the firewall
Identifying and controlling applications by behavior/signature regardless of port, enabling allow/block/limit per application
Managing application server access
Controlling application license compliance
ndc.expl: Application control uses deep packet inspection to identify applications (Facebook, BitTorrent, Skype, YouTube) regardless of port/protocol. Administrators can allow, block, or limit bandwidth for specific applications, providing granular policy enforcement.
ndc.takeaway: Application Control = NGFW identifies apps by DPI; allow/block/limit regardless of port used

ndc.q: What is a connection tracking table in a stateful firewall?
opts: A table listing all permitted firewall connections
A dynamic memory table tracking all active network connections including their state, source/destination IP, port, and protocol
A log of all past connections through the firewall
A routing table for established connections
ndc.expl: The connection tracking table (state table) in a stateful firewall dynamically records all active connections. Each entry includes: source/destination IP, source/destination port, protocol, connection state, and timeout. This enables automatic permission of return traffic.
ndc.takeaway: Connection Tracking Table = Dynamic record of all active connections; enables stateful traffic decisions

ndc.q: What is an IPS 'drop' action versus a 'reset' action?
opts: Drop removes the rule; reset restores default settings
Drop silently discards the packet; reset sends TCP RST to both ends to terminate the connection
Drop logs and blocks; reset ignores and continues
They are identical actions in modern IPS
ndc.expl: 'Drop' discards the malicious packet silently without notification. 'Reset' (TCP reset) sends RST packets to both the sender and receiver, explicitly terminating the TCP connection. Reset is faster at stopping ongoing attacks; drop is stealthier.
ndc.takeaway: IPS Drop = Silent packet discard; IPS Reset = TCP RST to both ends (explicitly kills connection)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of SIEM correlation rules?
opts: Correlating SIEM hardware components
Defining logic that matches multiple related events across different log sources to identify complex attack patterns
Correlating network performance metrics
Rules for correlating SIEM licenses
ndc.expl: SIEM correlation rules analyze events from multiple sources and apply logic (e.g., 'if failed login from IP X more than 5 times in 1 minute, trigger alert') to detect attack patterns that individual alerts would miss. Reduces false positives by requiring correlated evidence.
ndc.takeaway: SIEM Correlation Rules = Logic matching events across multiple sources to detect complex attack patterns

ndc.q: What is the 'chain of custody' in network security forensics?
opts: The sequence of firewall rules applied to network traffic
Documentation of who collected, handled, transferred, and analyzed digital evidence, maintaining its admissibility in legal proceedings
A chain of network devices in a security path
The sequence of incident response steps
ndc.expl: Chain of custody documents every person who handled digital evidence (network captures, log files, disk images) from collection through analysis and court presentation. Maintaining integrity and documented handling ensures evidence is legally admissible.
ndc.takeaway: Chain of Custody = Document who handled evidence when; required for legal admissibility

ndc.q: What is IKEv2 and what advantages does it have over IKEv1?
opts: IKEv2 is the second vendor release of IKE
IKEv2 is a more efficient, secure, and resilient key exchange protocol with faster negotiation, MOBIKE support, and built-in NAT-T
IKEv2 adds layer 2 VPN capabilities
IKEv2 replaces encryption in IPsec tunnels
ndc.expl: IKEv2 improvements over IKEv1: fewer messages for SA establishment (4 vs 6-9 exchanges), MOBIKE (seamless VPN mobility across network changes), built-in NAT-T, EAP authentication support, and better resistance to DoS attacks. Preferred for modern VPN deployments.
ndc.takeaway: IKEv2 vs IKEv1: Fewer messages, MOBIKE (roaming), built-in NAT-T, DoS-resistant; use IKEv2 for modern VPN

ndc.q: What is VPN redundancy?
opts: Having multiple VPN client applications installed
Deploying multiple VPN gateways or paths so if one fails, VPN connectivity is maintained through another
Redundant authentication servers for VPN
Backup encryption keys for VPN recovery
ndc.expl: VPN redundancy ensures continuous connectivity by deploying multiple VPN gateways (active/active or active/standby), multiple ISP links for diversity, and failover mechanisms. Essential for business-critical site-to-site VPNs and remote access services.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Redundancy = Multiple gateways/links; failover ensures continuity if primary VPN fails

ndc.q: What is the purpose of Dead Peer Detection (DPD) in IPsec VPN?
opts: Detecting dead (failed) network routes
A mechanism to detect when a VPN peer has become unresponsive so resources can be released and connections re-established
Detecting duplicate packets in VPN tunnels
A DDoS detection mechanism for VPN gateways
ndc.expl: DPD (Dead Peer Detection) sends periodic 'are you alive?' messages (R-U-THERE) to VPN peers. If no response after configured retries, the peer is declared dead, the SA is torn down, and the VPN can attempt re-establishment. Prevents zombie VPN connections consuming resources.
ndc.takeaway: DPD = Detect dead VPN peers via keepalives; tear down stale SAs; prevents zombie connections

ndc.q: What security threat does a DMZ NOT protect against?
opts: External attacks on web servers
Attacks from the internet on public services
Zero-day vulnerabilities in DMZ-hosted applications themselves
Unauthorized inbound access to internal servers
ndc.expl: DMZ protects against unauthorized access to internal network from internet. However, it does not protect DMZ servers themselves from zero-day vulnerabilities in their software (web server, application code). Patch management, WAF, and application security must address those risks.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ limitation = Does not protect against zero-day vulns in DMZ server software itself

ndc.q: What is a honeypot network (honeynet) in DMZ architecture?
opts: A DMZ zone with very sweet firewall rules
A network of honeypots deployed in the DMZ to attract, detect, and study attackers in an isolated environment
A network segment using honey encryption
An intrusion decoy deployed on internal networks only
ndc.expl: A honeynet is a network of interconnected honeypots (virtual systems) that simulates a realistic production environment. Deployed in or adjacent to DMZ, it attracts attackers, captures their techniques, and provides early warning of targeted attacks while being isolated from real systems.
ndc.takeaway: Honeynet = Multiple honeypots simulating real network; captures attacker techniques in isolated environment

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a proxy server in DMZ?
opts: Proxying requests from internal to external network only
Providing controlled, inspected internet access for internal users and/or controlling access to internal services from outside
Replacing the need for a firewall in DMZ
Caching internet content only
ndc.expl: A proxy in the DMZ can: (1) Forward proxy: allow internal users to access internet via the proxy (with inspection/filtering) without direct internet connectivity. (2) Reverse proxy: receive external requests and forward to internal servers, hiding internal architecture.
ndc.takeaway: Forward Proxy in DMZ = Internal users access internet via proxy; Reverse Proxy = External to internal

ndc.q: What is VLAN and how does it relate to DMZ?
opts: Very Large Area Network, used as a DMZ extension
Virtual LAN — a logical network segmentation technique often used to create DMZ segments without requiring separate physical hardware
A VPN-based LAN for DMZ services
A virtual local area networking protocol
ndc.expl: VLANs create logical network segments at Layer 2, often used to implement DMZ zones without separate physical switches. A three-legged firewall can use VLANs for outside, DMZ, and inside segments. Proper VLAN configuration prevents cross-VLAN data leakage.
ndc.takeaway: VLAN implements DMZ = Logical L2 segmentation; three-legged firewall with VLAN per zone

ndc.q: What is the risk of 'VLAN hopping' in DMZ architecture?
opts: VLANs consuming too much memory
An attacker exploiting switch misconfiguration to gain access to VLANs they shouldn't access, bypassing VLAN segmentation security
VLANs hopping between switches incorrectly
IP address conflicts between VLANs
ndc.expl: VLAN hopping exploits switch misconfigurations (auto-trunking on access ports) to send tagged frames to other VLANs. In DMZ architecture, this could allow internet-facing VLAN traffic to access internal VLANs, bypassing firewall controls. Disable DTP auto-trunking to prevent.
ndc.takeaway: VLAN Hopping = Exploit switch misconfig to cross VLAN boundaries; disable DTP/auto-trunking to prevent

ndc.q: What is Internet DMZ vs. Internal DMZ?
opts: They are different names for the same DMZ
Internet DMZ faces the public internet hosting public services; Internal DMZ provides additional segmentation between internal departments or tiers
Internet DMZ uses different protocols than Internal DMZ
Internal DMZ is more secure than Internet DMZ
ndc.expl: Internet DMZ hosts public-facing services (web, email, DNS) between internet and internal network. Internal DMZ (also called an extranet or restricted segment) provides additional segmentation within the internal network for sensitive systems (PCI scope, HR data), isolating them from general internal traffic.
ndc.takeaway: Internet DMZ = Public services facing internet; Internal DMZ = Segmentation of sensitive internal systems

ndc.q: What is the best practice for managing remote access VPN authentication?
opts: Username and password only is sufficient
Multi-factor authentication (certificate or token-based) combined with strong password policy
Using the same credentials as internal systems
Allowing auto-login for VPN convenience
ndc.expl: Best practice for remote access VPN authentication: MFA combining user certificate (or OTP token) with password/PIN. This ensures that stolen credentials alone cannot establish VPN access. Certificate-based authentication provides the strongest assurance.
ndc.takeaway: VPN Auth best practice = MFA: Certificate or OTP token + password; stolen password alone is insufficient
