Performance remains constant
Only SSL decryption affects performance
ndc.expl: Combining stateful inspection, DPI, IPS, and SSL/TLS decryption is computationally intensive. SSL decryption requires maintaining certificates, performing crypto operations (key exchange, decryption/re-encryption), which can reduce NGFW throughput by 60-80% compared to plain traffic.
ndc.takeaway: NGFW SSL inspection overhead: Can reduce throughput 60-80%; requires dedicated hardware or scaling

ndc.q: What is a transparent firewall (bridge mode)?
opts: A firewall that is invisible to users
A firewall operating at Layer 2 (bridging) without IP addresses on its interfaces, allowing it to be inserted into an existing network without IP reconfiguration
A firewall that allows all traffic transparently
A wireless firewall
ndc.expl: A transparent firewall operates at Layer 2 (Data Link) as a bridge, without IP addresses on its filtering interfaces. It can be inserted between network segments without changing IP addressing. Traffic passes through it invisibly while being filtered.
ndc.takeaway: Transparent FW = Layer 2 bridge mode; no IP on interfaces; invisible insertion into network

ndc.q: What are stateless vs stateful firewall rules for FTP and why does FTP require special handling?
opts: FTP only uses one port so no special handling needed

FTP uses separate control (port 21) and dynamically negotiated data (port 20 or random in passive mode) connections; stateful firewalls track the control session to allow the dynamic data connection automatically
FTP is always blocked by firewalls
Stateless firewalls handle FTP better than stateful
ndc.expl: FTP uses port 21 for control and dynamically negotiates data connections (active: port 20; passive: random high port). Stateless firewalls require static rules for all possible data ports. Stateful firewalls with ALG (Application Layer Gateway) track the PORT/PASV commands to automatically allow the data channel.
ndc.takeaway: FTP ALG = Stateful FW tracks control session to dynamically allow negotiated data connections

ndc.q: What is the main difference between a NIDS and a HIDS?
opts: NIDS monitors individual hosts; HIDS monitors network segments
NIDS monitors network traffic at strategic points; HIDS monitors events on a specific host (logs, system calls, file integrity)
HIDS is more accurate than NIDS
NIDS is deployed on each host; HIDS is deployed on the network
ndc.expl: NIDS (Network IDS) monitors network traffic at chokepoints (like a network tap or SPAN port). HIDS (Host IDS) monitors events on individual hosts: system logs, file integrity, registry changes, and process activity. NIDS sees network; HIDS sees host.
ndc.takeaway: NIDS = Network traffic monitoring; HIDS = Individual host monitoring (logs, file integrity)

ndc.q: What is a true positive in IDS/IPS terminology?
opts: A false alarm generated by IDS
An alert generated for traffic that is actually malicious (correctly detected attack)
An attack that went undetected
A legitimate alert that was suppressed
ndc.expl: A true positive is when the IDS correctly identifies and alerts on a genuine attack. A false positive is an alarm for legitimate traffic. A true negative is correctly allowing legitimate traffic. A false negative is missing a real attack.
ndc.takeaway: True Positive = Real attack correctly detected; False Positive = Legitimate traffic incorrectly flagged

ndc.q: What is signature-based IDS detection?
opts: Detecting attacks using machine learning
Comparing network traffic or system events against a database of known attack patterns (signatures)
Monitoring statistical deviations from baseline
Detecting attacks using behavioral analysis
ndc.expl: Signature-based IDS compares traffic or events against a database of known attack signatures (patterns). Fast and accurate for known attacks but cannot detect unknown (zero-day) attacks. Must be regularly updated with new signatures.
ndc.takeaway: Signature-based IDS = Compare against known attack patterns; cannot detect zero-days

ndc.q: What is the main limitation of signature-based IDS?
opts: Too many false positives
Cannot detect known attacks
Cannot detect zero-day attacks not in the signature database
Very slow processing speed
ndc.expl: Signature-based IDS cannot detect zero-day attacks or novel attack variants that don't match existing signatures. Attackers can evade signature-based IDS by slightly modifying attack patterns or using encryption.
ndc.takeaway: Signature IDS limitation = Cannot detect zero-days or novel attacks without matching signatures

ndc.q: What is anomaly-based IDS detection?
opts: Matching packets against attack signatures
Establishing a baseline of normal behavior and alerting when traffic deviates significantly from it
Monitoring only known malicious IP addresses
Inspecting packets at the application layer only
ndc.expl: Anomaly-based IDS establishes a statistical baseline of normal network/system behavior during a training period. Any significant deviation from this baseline triggers an alert. Can detect zero-days but generates more false positives.
ndc.takeaway: Anomaly IDS = Baseline normal behavior; alert on deviations; can detect zero-days (more false positives)

ndc.q: What is Snort?
opts: A network performance monitoring tool
An open-source network intrusion detection/prevention system using signature and protocol analysis
A commercial firewall product
A vulnerability scanner
ndc.expl: Snort is an open-source NIDS/NIPS that performs real-time traffic analysis and packet logging. It uses rules for signature detection, protocol analysis, and content matching. Can run in IDS (alert) or IPS (inline/block) mode.
ndc.takeaway: Snort = Open-source NIDS/NIPS; signature-based + protocol analysis; alerting or inline blocking

ndc.q: What is OSSEC?
opts: A network-based IDS
An open-source host-based IDS (HIDS) providing log analysis, file integrity monitoring, and rootkit detection
A firewall management tool
A packet capture library
ndc.expl: OSSEC is an open-source HIDS that performs log analysis, file integrity monitoring, Windows registry monitoring, rootkit detection, and active response. Runs agents on monitored hosts reporting to a central manager.
ndc.takeaway: OSSEC = Open-source HIDS; log analysis + file integrity + rootkit detection + active response

ndc.q: What is Suricata?
opts: A commercial IPS solution
An open-source IDS/IPS/NSM engine that can run multi-threaded for high-performance network monitoring
A SIEM platform
A firewall replacement
ndc.expl: Suricata is an open-source, high-performance IDS/IPS/NSM (Network Security Monitoring) engine maintained by OISF. It supports multi-threading, protocol detection, file extraction, TLS/HTTP logging, and can run Snort-compatible rules.
ndc.takeaway: Suricata = Open-source multi-threaded IDS/IPS; compatible with Snort rules; high performance

ndc.q: What does IPS stand for and what is its key difference from IDS?
opts: Internet Protection System; monitors only internet traffic
Intrusion Prevention System; it is inline and actively blocks malicious traffic (unlike IDS which only alerts)
Integrated Packet System; uses packet analysis
Intrusion Protection System; monitors email traffic
ndc.expl: IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is inline and actively blocks, drops, or resets malicious traffic in real time. IDS only monitors and alerts. The IPS position in the traffic path allows it to take preventive action.
ndc.takeaway: IPS = Inline + Active blocking; IDS = Passive/Out-of-band + Alert only

ndc.q: What is the ELK stack and how is it used in security?
opts: A programming framework for security tools
Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana — used for log collection, parsing, storage, and visualization for security analysis and threat hunting
A type of intrusion detection methodology
A VPN configuration stack
ndc.expl: The ELK stack (Elasticsearch for search/storage, Logstash for log collection/parsing, Kibana for visualization) is widely used in security for log management, SIEM functionality, threat hunting, and incident investigation.
ndc.takeaway: ELK = Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana; log management and security analytics platform

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) port for IDS?
opts: To increase switch performance
To copy traffic from monitored ports to the IDS sensor so it can analyze traffic without being inline
To block malicious traffic
To assign ports to VLANs
ndc.expl: A SPAN (mirror) port copies traffic from specified switch ports or VLANs to a designated port connected to the IDS sensor. This allows the IDS to analyze traffic passively without being in the traffic path.
ndc.takeaway: SPAN port = Copies switch traffic to IDS sensor; enables passive monitoring without inline placement

ndc.q: What is the difference between inline and passive IDS/IPS deployment?
opts: Inline is used for monitoring only; passive blocks traffic
Inline sits in the traffic path and can block traffic; passive receives a copy of traffic and can only alert
Inline is slower; passive is faster
Passive is more secure than inline
ndc.expl: Inline deployment places the sensor between network segments in the actual traffic path — can drop/block malicious packets (IPS mode). Passive deployment uses a SPAN port or network tap to analyze a copy of traffic — can only alert, not block (IDS mode).
ndc.takeaway: Inline = In traffic path, can block (IPS); Passive = Traffic copy via SPAN, alert only (IDS)

ndc.q: What is network traffic baselining in the context of IDS?
opts: Setting firewall rules based on traffic volume
Measuring and recording normal traffic patterns to establish a reference point for anomaly detection
A performance benchmarking tool
Capturing traffic for forensic analysis
ndc.expl: Traffic baselining involves measuring and recording normal network behavior (protocols, volumes, timing, source/destination pairs) over time. Anomaly-based IDS uses this baseline to detect deviations indicating potential attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Traffic baseline = Normal behavior reference for anomaly detection; deviations trigger alerts

ndc.q: What is Nagios used for in network security monitoring?
opts: Intrusion detection and prevention
Infrastructure and network monitoring — alerting on host/service up/down, performance thresholds, and failures
Packet capture and analysis
Firewall log analysis
ndc.expl: Nagios is an open-source infrastructure monitoring system that monitors hosts and services, alerting administrators when thresholds are exceeded or components fail (server down, CPU high, disk full). Used for availability monitoring, not deep security analysis.
ndc.takeaway: Nagios = Infrastructure/availability monitoring; alerts on host/service failures and thresholds

ndc.q: What is the difference between a network tap and a SPAN port for IDS monitoring?
opts: A tap uses software; SPAN uses hardware
A network tap is a passive hardware device providing a complete copy of all traffic; SPAN port is switch-provided and may drop packets under load
SPAN provides more reliable captures than a tap
They are functionally identical
ndc.expl: A network tap is a dedicated passive hardware device that provides a full, lossless copy of all traffic — even during high loads. A SPAN port is configured on a switch and may drop mirrored packets when the switch is under load, potentially missing traffic.
ndc.takeaway: Network tap = Lossless passive hardware capture; SPAN = Switch software mirror (may drop packets)

ndc.q: What is the concept of defense in depth as applied to IDS/IPS?
opts: Using only IPS for all security
Deploying multiple detection layers: network IDS, host IDS, endpoint protection, SIEM correlation — so that if one layer misses an attack, others catch it
Placing all IDS sensors at the network perimeter only
Using maximum sensitivity settings on all IDS sensors
ndc.expl: Defense in depth with IDS/IPS means layering: NIDS at the perimeter, NIDS inside the network, HIDS on critical servers, endpoint detection (EDR), and SIEM correlating all alerts. An attack evading one layer is likely caught by another.
ndc.takeaway: IDS Defense-in-Depth: NIDS perimeter + NIDS internal + HIDS + EDR + SIEM correlation

ndc.q: What is IDS rule tuning and why is it necessary?
opts: Upgrading IDS hardware
Adjusting IDS signatures and thresholds to reduce false positives for the specific environment while maintaining detection of real threats
Updating IDS software versions
Configuring IDS logging settings
ndc.expl: IDS rule tuning adjusts signatures, thresholds, and exclusions for the specific network environment. Without tuning, IDS generates excessive false positives (alert fatigue) that overwhelm analysts. Tuning balances sensitivity vs. false positive rate.
ndc.takeaway: IDS Tuning = Adjust rules for environment; reduce false positives while keeping true positive rate high

ndc.q: What is alert fatigue in the context of IDS/SIEM?
opts: Physical fatigue from monitoring screens
The condition where security analysts are overwhelmed by too many alerts, leading to important alerts being missed or ignored
Slow IDS processing due to too many alerts
Alert logging disk space exhaustion
ndc.expl: Alert fatigue occurs when IDS/SIEM generates so many alerts (especially false positives) that analysts cannot process them all effectively. This leads to real attacks being missed. Addressed by tuning, prioritization, and correlation rules.
ndc.takeaway: Alert Fatigue = Too many alerts overwhelm analysts; real threats missed; fixed by tuning and correlation

ndc.q: What is the function of the IDS manager component?
opts: The physical hardware running IDS
The component that receives data from sensors, correlates events, applies analysis algorithms, and provides management/reporting capabilities
A tool that configures IDS signatures
The network switch port used for monitoring
ndc.expl: The IDS manager (also called the console or management station) collects data from multiple sensors/agents, correlates events across the network, applies analysis algorithms, generates alerts, and provides a management interface and reporting dashboard.
ndc.takeaway: IDS Manager = Collects sensor data, correlates events, generates alerts, provides reporting

ndc.q: What is protocol-based IDS detection?
opts: Detecting attacks only in TCP traffic
Monitoring and analyzing the state and behavior of specific application protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMTP) to detect deviations from the protocol specification
Filtering packets by protocol number only
A signature-based approach using protocol signatures
ndc.expl: Protocol-based IDS monitors application protocol behavior for deviations from RFC specifications. For example, detecting HTTP request smuggling, malformed DNS queries, or SMTP command injection — attacks that exploit protocol implementations.
ndc.takeaway: Protocol-based IDS = Monitors for deviations from protocol specifications (RFC compliance)

ndc.q: What is a honeynet and how does it differ from a single honeypot?
opts: A honeypot with multiple decoy files
A network of honeypots that simulates a complete network environment to study complex attacks and attacker behavior
A type of IPS sensor
A network tap configuration
ndc.expl: A honeynet is a network of interconnected honeypots that simulates a realistic network environment (multiple hosts, services, network topology). It captures more complex attack behaviors including lateral movement and post-compromise activities.
ndc.takeaway: Honeynet = Network of multiple honeypots; simulates realistic environment; captures complex attacks

ndc.q: What is the purpose of Snort rules and what are the main components of a Snort rule?
opts: Snort rules configure network routing
Snort rules define what traffic to detect; components: action, protocol, source IP/port, direction, destination IP/port, rule options (content, msg, sid, rev)
Snort rules configure firewall policies
Snort rules define VPN tunnels
ndc.expl: Snort rules instruct the IDS what to detect and what action to take. Structure: 'action protocol src_ip src_port direction dst_ip dst_port (options)'. Options include: msg (message), content (pattern), sid (signature ID), rev (revision).
ndc.takeaway: Snort rule = action protocol src dir dst (msg:"text"; content:"pattern"; sid:N; rev:N;)

ndc.q: What does the IDS sensor do and where is it deployed?
opts: Sensors encrypt network traffic
Sensors collect and analyze raw network/host data (packets, logs, system events) and report to the IDS manager; deployed at strategic network points or on hosts
Sensors store IDS signatures
Sensors configure firewall rules
ndc.expl: IDS sensors collect raw data from the network (via SPAN/tap) or from host systems (agent-based). They perform initial analysis, match against signatures or baseline, and forward alerts/logs to the central IDS manager for correlation.
ndc.takeaway: IDS Sensor = Data collection + initial analysis; network (via SPAN) or host-based (agent)

ndc.q: How does an IPS prevent attacks in inline mode?
opts: By encrypting all traffic
By sitting in the traffic path and dropping, resetting, or modifying malicious packets before they reach the target
By logging attacks for later analysis
By redirecting traffic to a honeypot
ndc.expl: Inline IPS receives actual traffic, analyzes it, and for malicious traffic: drops packets (silently discards), sends TCP RST to both parties, or modifies packets. Legitimate traffic is forwarded normally. Real-time prevention with low latency is critical.
ndc.takeaway: Inline IPS actions: DROP (discard), RST (reset connection), MODIFY (clean/alter packet)

ndc.q: What is the difference between Snort and Suricata?
opts: Snort is commercial; Suricata is open-source
Both are open-source IDS/IPS; Snort is single-threaded; Suricata is multi-threaded (better performance on multi-core systems) and has native IPv6, protocol detection, and file extraction
Snort supports more protocols than Suricata
Suricata cannot use Snort rules
ndc.expl: Both Snort and Suricata are open-source IDS/IPS tools with similar rule formats. Key differences: Suricata is multi-threaded (scales better on multi-core hardware), has native multi-file analysis, protocol keyword detection, TLS logging, and file extraction features not in Snort.
ndc.takeaway: Snort = Single-threaded; Suricata = Multi-threaded + protocol detection + file extraction

ndc.q: What is the MITRE ATT&CK framework's role in IDS/IPS detection engineering?
opts: It provides firewall rules
It maps attacker TTPs to detection opportunities, enabling security teams to build IDS rules covering specific attack techniques and measure detection coverage
It replaces IDS entirely
It provides network performance metrics
ndc.expl: MITRE ATT&CK catalogs adversary tactics and techniques observed in real attacks. Security teams map their IDS/IPS detection rules to ATT&CK techniques to identify coverage gaps, prioritize new detections, and measure overall detection posture.
ndc.takeaway: MITRE ATT&CK + IDS = Map detection rules to TTPs; identify coverage gaps; measure detection posture

ndc.q: What is a false positive storm and how does it impact security operations?
opts: A physical storm disrupting network connectivity
A sudden flood of incorrect IDS/SIEM alerts caused by a rule change or network event, overwhelming analysts and masking real threats
A DDoS attack generating high traffic
A power surge affecting security equipment
ndc.expl: A false positive storm occurs when a misconfigured IDS rule, a network change, or a scan generates massive numbers of false alerts. It overwhelms security analysts, consumes resources, and creates the perfect cover for a real attack that gets buried in the noise.
ndc.takeaway: False Positive Storm = Flood of incorrect alerts; overwhelms SOC; masks real threats

ndc.q: How does behavioral analysis differ from signature-based detection in IDS?
opts: Behavioral analysis is less accurate

Behavioral analysis learns normal patterns of behavior (users, systems, network) and flags deviations; signature-based matches specific known attack patterns. Behavioral catches zero-days; signatures catch known threats accurately.
Behavioral analysis only works for network traffic
They are identical approaches
ndc.expl: Behavioral analysis (UEBA, anomaly detection) models normal behavior profiles and detects deviations — effective against zero-days, insider threats, and slow attacks. Signature-based matching is fast, accurate for known threats, but blind to novel attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Behavioral = Catches zero-days/insider threats; Signature = Fast/accurate for known threats

ndc.q: What is the purpose of the BASE (Basic Analysis and Security Engine) in Snort deployments?
opts: A command-line tool for writing Snort rules
A web-based front-end for viewing and analyzing Snort alerts stored in a database
A network packet capture tool
A Snort rule testing framework
ndc.expl: BASE (Basic Analysis and Security Engine) is a PHP web-based front-end that interfaces with Snort's database (typically MySQL) to display, search, and analyze IDS alerts. It provides a graphical interface for alert review and forensic investigation.
ndc.takeaway: BASE = Web GUI for Snort alerts; queries MySQL database; provides search and analysis of IDS events

ndc.q: What is IDS evasion and what techniques do attackers use?
opts: Disabling IDS remotely
Techniques to avoid IDS detection: packet fragmentation, protocol obfuscation, encoding (URL/Unicode), session splicing, encryption, slow-rate attacks, polymorphic shellcode
Flooding IDS with traffic
Using VPN to hide attack traffic
ndc.expl: IDS evasion bypasses detection using: IP fragmentation (IDS reassembly issues), session splicing (small TCP segments), protocol obfuscation, URL/Unicode encoding, encrypted channels, polymorphic shellcode (changes signature), and slow-rate attacks that stay below thresholds.
ndc.takeaway: IDS Evasion: Fragmentation, session splicing, encoding, encryption, polymorphic code, slow-rate

ndc.q: What is the role of machine learning in next-generation IDS/IPS?
opts: ML replaces all signature-based detection
ML enables automated learning of normal behavior, anomaly detection, classification of attack types, and reduction of false positives without manual rule creation
ML is only used for performance optimization
ML cannot be applied to network security
ndc.expl: ML-based IDS/IPS uses supervised learning (classification of known attacks), unsupervised learning (clustering for anomaly detection), and reinforcement learning to adapt. Benefits: detects zero-days, reduces false positives, classifies attacks automatically, and adapts to network changes.
ndc.takeaway: ML in IDS = Automated anomaly detection, zero-day detection, reduced false positives without manual rules

ndc.q: What is threat hunting and how does it complement IDS/IPS?
opts: Automated attack blocking based on threat intelligence
A proactive human-led investigation process that searches for threats that have evaded automated IDS/IPS; complements by finding what automated systems miss
Threat hunting replaces IDS entirely
Automated scanning of known vulnerability databases
ndc.expl: Threat hunting proactively searches for threats that bypassed automated defenses. Hunters formulate hypotheses based on threat intelligence and ATT&CK TTPs, then investigate using raw logs, packet captures, and forensic tools. IDS provides triggers; threat hunting finds what IDS missed.
ndc.takeaway: Threat Hunting = Proactive search for IDS-evading threats; hypothesis-driven investigation

ndc.q: How does an IPS handle the problem of encrypted traffic inspection?
opts: IPS cannot inspect encrypted traffic
IPS integrates with SSL/TLS decryption (via man-in-the-middle decryption proxy) to decrypt traffic for inspection, then re-encrypts before forwarding; has privacy implications
IPS blocks all encrypted traffic
IPS uses quantum cryptography to inspect
ndc.expl: For encrypted traffic, IPS requires SSL/TLS inspection: traffic is decrypted at the IPS (acting as a trusted MitM), inspected, then re-encrypted. This requires certificate management, adds latency, and has privacy/legal implications in some jurisdictions.
ndc.takeaway: IPS SSL inspection = Decrypt→Inspect→Re-encrypt (MitM); requires certificate trust; adds latency

ndc.q: What is a distributed IDS (dIDS) and why is it needed?
opts: An IDS distributed across cloud regions
An IDS architecture using multiple sensors across the network reporting to centralized analyzers; needed because no single sensor can monitor all network segments efficiently
An IDS that distributes load across CPU cores
A peer-to-peer IDS architecture
ndc.expl: A distributed IDS deploys sensors at multiple network points (internet edge, internal segments, DMZ, WAN links) all reporting to a central manager. This provides enterprise-wide visibility. No single sensor can monitor all segments; centralization enables cross-segment correlation.
ndc.takeaway: Distributed IDS = Multiple sensors → Central manager; enterprise-wide visibility + cross-segment correlation

ndc.q: What is the difference between reactive and proactive IPS response?
opts: Reactive is better than proactive
Reactive IPS responds after detecting an attack (drop packet, reset connection); proactive IPS pre-emptively blocks based on threat intelligence feeds before an attack is seen
Proactive IPS only logs threats
They are identical in function
ndc.expl: Reactive IPS acts after detecting malicious traffic in real time (drop, reset, rate-limit). Proactive IPS pre-emptively blocks known malicious IPs/domains from threat intelligence feeds before any attack traffic arrives, reducing exposure window.
ndc.takeaway: Reactive IPS = Block on detection; Proactive IPS = Pre-block based on threat intel feeds

ndc.q: What is sensor placement strategy in an IDS deployment?
opts: Place all sensors on external interfaces only
Strategic placement at multiple points: outside the outer firewall (sees all attacks), inside outer firewall (sees what passed), DMZ, internal segments, and on hosts (HIDS) for complete visibility
Place sensors only on internal network
Place sensors only in the DMZ
ndc.expl: Optimal sensor placement: outside the external firewall (full attack visibility), inside external firewall (attacks that bypassed it), in the DMZ (server-targeted attacks), inside internal firewalls (lateral movement), and HIDS on critical servers for comprehensive defense in depth.
ndc.takeaway: IDS placement: Outside FW + Inside FW + DMZ + Internal + HIDS on servers = Complete visibility

ndc.q: What is the role of SIEM log correlation in detecting multi-stage attacks?
opts: SIEM only stores logs

SIEM correlates events across multiple sources over time to detect attack patterns spanning multiple stages (e.g., port scan + exploitation + persistence) that individual tools would see as isolated events
SIEM replaces IDS and firewalls
SIEM only analyzes firewall logs
ndc.expl: Multi-stage attacks generate separate events in different systems (scan in FW, exploit attempt in IDS, registry change in endpoint). SIEM correlates these time-related events from multiple sources, recognizing the kill chain pattern that each individual tool sees as isolated noise.
ndc.takeaway: SIEM correlation = Connect events across systems/time to detect attack chains (kill chain visibility)

ndc.q: What is the concept of IDS signature versioning and why is it critical?
opts: Versioning tracks which IDS software version is running
Signatures have version numbers (rev in Snort) to track updates; critical because attackers modify attacks to evade current signatures, requiring frequent updates and rollback capability
Versioning is only needed for compliance
Signature versioning is only for documentation
ndc.expl: IDS signature versioning tracks signature updates using revision numbers. Critical because: attack techniques evolve (attacker modifications), new vulnerabilities emerge requiring new signatures, and false positive fixes require controlled rollouts. Rollback capability is needed when new signatures break things.
ndc.takeaway: Signature versioning = Tracks signature evolution; critical for staying current and rollback capability

ndc.q: What are the limitations of IPS that organizations must account for?
opts: IPS has no limitations

IPS limitations: high false positive risk causing service disruption if inline; performance bottleneck; cannot inspect all encrypted traffic; evasion by attackers; failure modes (fail-open vs fail-closed)
IPS can only be deployed in cloud environments
IPS is always slower than IDS
ndc.expl: IPS limitations: False positives may block legitimate traffic (critical if inline); performance bottleneck for high-speed networks; encrypted traffic evasion; sophisticated attackers use fragmentation/encoding to bypass; fail-open mode (bypass on IPS failure) creates vulnerability; fail-closed causes outages.
ndc.takeaway: IPS limitations: False positive disruption, performance bottleneck, encrypted traffic, evasion, fail mode risk

ndc.q: What is a VPN (Virtual Private Network)?
opts: A type of firewall
An encrypted, private communication channel over a public network (internet) that provides secure remote connectivity
A network monitoring tool
A type of network switch
ndc.expl: A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel over a public network (like the internet), allowing users and sites to communicate securely as if they were on a private network. It provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication for remote access.
ndc.takeaway: VPN = Encrypted private tunnel over public network; secure remote access

ndc.q: What are the two main types of VPN deployments?
opts: Encrypted VPN and non-encrypted VPN
Site-to-site VPN (connects entire networks) and Remote Access VPN (connects individual users to the corporate network)
Internal VPN and External VPN
IPv4 VPN and IPv6 VPN
ndc.expl: Site-to-site VPN connects entire office networks through encrypted tunnels (replaces leased lines). Remote access VPN allows individual users to securely connect to the corporate network from anywhere over the internet.
ndc.takeaway: VPN types: Site-to-site (network-to-network) vs Remote access (user-to-network)

ndc.q: What protocol does OpenVPN use for its control channel?
opts: IPSec
TLS (SSL)
PPTP
L2TP
ndc.expl: OpenVPN uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) for its control channel, providing certificate-based authentication and key exchange. The data channel uses symmetric encryption (AES). OpenVPN can run on TCP or UDP, typically port 1194.
ndc.takeaway: OpenVPN control channel = TLS; data channel = AES; port 1194 (UDP or TCP)

ndc.q: What is the difference between IPSec tunnel mode and transport mode?
opts: Tunnel mode encrypts headers; transport mode encrypts payload
Tunnel mode encrypts the entire original IP packet (adds new IP header); transport mode encrypts only the payload (original headers intact)
They encrypt the same data
Transport mode is more secure than tunnel mode
ndc.expl: IPSec Tunnel Mode encrypts the entire original IP datagram and encapsulates it in a new IP packet — used for VPN gateways (site-to-site). Transport Mode encrypts only the payload, leaving original IP headers intact — used for end-to-end host communication.
ndc.takeaway: IPSec Tunnel = Encrypt entire packet (new IP header added); Transport = Encrypt payload only

ndc.q: What is PPTP (Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol)?
opts: A modern, highly secure VPN protocol
An older VPN protocol developed by Microsoft using GRE for data and TCP port 1723 for control; considered insecure due to MS-CHAPv2 weaknesses
A firewall protocol
A routing protocol
ndc.expl: PPTP is a legacy VPN protocol using TCP port 1723 for control and GRE for data encapsulation. MS-CHAPv2 authentication has known cryptographic weaknesses. PPTP is no longer considered secure and should be replaced with L2TP/IPSec or OpenVPN.
ndc.takeaway: PPTP = Legacy VPN; TCP 1723 + GRE; MS-CHAPv2 weaknesses; NOT recommended

ndc.q: What is SSL VPN?
opts: A VPN that only works with SSL certificates
A VPN using SSL/TLS protocol for the tunnel, accessible via web browser without requiring client software installation
A type of site-to-site VPN
A VPN only for email access
ndc.expl: SSL VPN uses SSL/TLS to create a secure tunnel over HTTPS (port 443). Key advantage: accessible via standard web browsers without installing VPN client software, making it firewall-friendly as port 443 is almost always allowed.
ndc.takeaway: SSL VPN = TLS tunnel over port 443; browser-based access; no client installation needed

ndc.q: What is full tunnel VPN?
opts: A VPN spanning full geographic distance
All traffic (corporate and internet) routes through the VPN tunnel; provides maximum security but increases load on VPN server and adds latency for internet access
A VPN for large enterprise networks
A physical leased line VPN
ndc.expl: Full tunnel VPN routes ALL user traffic through the corporate VPN, including internet browsing. This allows corporate security controls (web filter, DLP) to inspect all traffic. Trade-off: higher VPN gateway load, increased internet latency for users.
ndc.takeaway: Full tunnel = ALL traffic via VPN; max security; higher VPN load vs split tunnel

ndc.q: What port does IPSec IKE use?
opts: Port 443
Port 500 (and 4500 for NAT traversal)
Port 1723
Port 1194
ndc.expl: IPSec IKE uses UDP port 500 for initial key exchange. When NAT is detected between VPN peers, it switches to UDP port 4500 (NAT-T) to allow IPSec traffic through NAT devices. ESP (protocol 50) carries the encrypted data.
ndc.takeaway: IPSec IKE = UDP 500; NAT-T = UDP 4500; ESP (protocol 50) carries encrypted data

ndc.q: What is a VPN tunnel?
opts: A physical cable for VPN connections
An encapsulated and encrypted communication channel created between two VPN endpoints over a public network
A type of network switch configuration
A VPN authentication protocol
ndc.expl: A VPN tunnel is an encrypted, encapsulated communication path between two VPN endpoints. The original packet is encrypted and wrapped in a new packet (encapsulation) so it can traverse the public internet privately.
ndc.takeaway: VPN tunnel = Encapsulated + encrypted path between endpoints; original packet hidden inside

ndc.q: What is the purpose of authentication in VPN?
opts: To speed up VPN connection
To verify that both the VPN server and client are who they claim to be before establishing the tunnel
To compress VPN traffic
To assign IP addresses to VPN clients
ndc.expl: Authentication in VPN verifies the identity of connecting parties. Methods: username/password, digital certificates (PKI), pre-shared keys (PSK), multi-factor authentication. Strong authentication prevents unauthorized VPN access.
ndc.takeaway: VPN authentication = Verify identity before tunnel; certificate/PSK/MFA are common methods

ndc.q: What is a site-to-site VPN?
opts: A VPN for a single user at a remote site
A permanent VPN connection between two or more network locations (branch offices) creating a secure interconnected network
A VPN for accessing specific websites
A VPN only between two computers
ndc.expl: Site-to-site VPN connects entire networks (branch offices, data centers) through VPN gateways. Traffic between sites is automatically encrypted without requiring individual user VPN clients. Used to replace expensive leased lines.
ndc.takeaway: Site-to-site VPN = Network-to-network; VPN gateways handle encryption automatically for all users

ndc.q: What are the key components of IPSec?
opts: Only encryption is needed
AH (Authentication Header) for integrity/auth, ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) for encryption/auth, IKE for key management, and Security Associations (SAs) defining parameters
IPSec only uses ESP
IPSec requires SSL certificates
ndc.expl: IPSec components: AH (Authentication Header, protocol 51) — provides integrity and authentication but NOT confidentiality. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload, protocol 50) — provides encryption, integrity, and authentication. IKE — manages key exchange and SA negotiation.
ndc.takeaway: IPSec: AH (auth+integrity, no encrypt) + ESP (encrypt+auth+integrity) + IKE (key mgmt)

ndc.q: What is Dead Peer Detection (DPD) in IPSec VPN?
opts: Detection of network performance issues
A mechanism to detect when an IPSec VPN peer is no longer responding and automatically clear the stale SA and re-establish if needed
A security feature that detects unauthorized VPN peers
A method for detecting VPN performance bottlenecks
ndc.expl: DPD (RFC 3706) allows IPSec peers to detect if the other peer has gone offline (network failure, crash) by sending IKE informational messages. If no response, the stale SA is cleared and the VPN can be re-established. Prevents black-hole routing through dead tunnels.
ndc.takeaway: DPD = Detects dead IPSec peers; clears stale SAs; prevents black-hole routing

ndc.q: What is a VPN split tunneling security risk?
opts: Split tunneling has no security risks
Corporate traffic may pass through an insecure home/public network; compromised endpoints can bridge corporate and internet traffic, becoming an attack pivot
Split tunneling reduces VPN speed
Split tunneling breaks all internet access
ndc.expl: Split tunneling risk: a compromised endpoint has one interface on the corporate VPN and another on the internet — potentially acting as a bridge (pivot) for attackers to reach internal resources. Also, internet traffic evades corporate security controls (web filtering, DLP).
ndc.takeaway: Split tunneling risk = Compromised endpoint bridges internet and corporate network (attack pivot)

ndc.q: How does VPN ensure data integrity?
opts: By encrypting all data
Through cryptographic hash functions (HMAC-SHA256) applied to each packet, allowing the receiver to verify the packet was not modified in transit
Through IP addressing
By requiring password for each packet
ndc.expl: VPN ensures data integrity using HMAC (Hash-Based Message Authentication Code) with algorithms like HMAC-SHA256. Each packet includes a MAC (Message Authentication Code) computed from the packet content and a shared key. Any modification changes the MAC and is detected.
ndc.takeaway: VPN integrity = HMAC (HMAC-SHA256/HMAC-SHA1) verifies packets not modified in transit

ndc.q: What is NAT traversal (NAT-T) in IPSec?
opts: A method to bypass firewall rules
A technique encapsulating IPSec ESP packets in UDP (port 4500) to allow them to pass through NAT devices that would otherwise break ESP
A NAT protocol for VPN clients
A routing protocol for VPN traffic
ndc.expl: IPSec ESP (protocol 50) packets cannot pass through NAT (NAT modifies IP headers, breaking ESP integrity). NAT-T detects NAT in the path during IKE and encapsulates ESP packets in UDP port 4500, allowing normal NAT processing while preserving IPSec security.
ndc.takeaway: NAT-T = Wrap ESP in UDP 4500 to pass through NAT; needed when IPSec crosses NAT devices

ndc.q: What is RRAS (Routing and Remote Access Service) in Windows Server?
opts: A network monitoring service
A Windows Server role providing VPN server functionality (PPTP, L2TP/IPSec, SSTP, IKEv2) and routing services
A firewall management service
A Windows file sharing service
ndc.expl: RRAS (Routing and Remote Access Service) is a Windows Server role enabling VPN server capabilities (supporting PPTP, L2TP/IPSec, SSTP, IKEv2 protocols), demand-dial routing, and NAT. Used for Windows-based VPN infrastructure.
ndc.takeaway: RRAS = Windows VPN server role; supports PPTP, L2TP/IPSec, SSTP, IKEv2

ndc.q: What is a VPN concentrator's role in enterprise SSL VPN deployments?
opts: A device that caches web content
A dedicated device terminating many SSL VPN sessions, providing user authentication, authorization, traffic encryption, and often acting as a reverse proxy for application access
A load balancer for web servers
A VPN router for site-to-site connections
ndc.expl: Enterprise SSL VPN concentrators terminate thousands of remote access sessions, integrate with LDAP/RADIUS for authentication, enforce access policies (role-based access), provide endpoint compliance checking, and serve as application proxies for web-based access.
ndc.takeaway: SSL VPN concentrator = Terminates many sessions; auth integration; role-based access; endpoint compliance

ndc.q: What is IPv6 VPN and what considerations are unique to it?
opts: IPv6 VPN is identical to IPv4 VPN
IPv6 has IPSec as part of its specification (mandatory in IPv6); VPNs over IPv6 must handle longer addresses, dual-stack environments, and IPv6-specific routing considerations
IPv6 cannot be used with VPN
IPv6 VPN only uses PPTP
ndc.expl: IPv6 includes IPSec as a mandatory component (unlike IPv4 where it's optional). IPv6 VPN considerations: dual-stack tunneling, 6to4/Teredo for IPv6-over-IPv4 tunneling, longer address space management, and ensuring IPv6 traffic is also tunneled (split tunneling leaks IPv6).
ndc.takeaway: IPv6 VPN: IPSec mandatory in IPv6; dual-stack handling; prevent IPv6 split tunnel leaks

ndc.q: What is a VPN common error 'GRE blocked' and how is it resolved?
opts: GRE is a security vulnerability
PPTP VPN uses GRE (IP protocol 47) for data; if GRE is blocked by firewall/NAT, PPTP fails; resolved by allowing GRE through firewall or switching to L2TP/IPSec or OpenVPN
GRE is a DNS issue
GRE blocking improves VPN security
ndc.expl: PPTP uses TCP 1723 for control and GRE (Generic Routing Encapsulation, IP protocol 47) for data. Firewalls or NAT devices that block GRE will break PPTP. Resolution: allow GRE or migrate to L2TP/IPSec (UDP) or OpenVPN (TCP/UDP) which traverse firewalls more easily.
ndc.takeaway: PPTP uses GRE (protocol 47); GRE blocked = PPTP fails; switch to L2TP or OpenVPN to fix

ndc.q: What is IKEv2 and how does it improve over IKEv1?
opts: IKEv2 is older and less secure than IKEv1
IKEv2 provides faster initial negotiation (fewer messages), MOBIKE for mobility, built-in EAP support, and simpler configuration; IKEv1 requires aggressive vs main mode complexity
IKEv1 and IKEv2 are identical
IKEv2 only works with IPv6
ndc.expl: IKEv2 improvements: fewer messages for SA establishment (4 vs IKEv1's 9 in main mode), MOBIKE extension for IP address changes (roaming), built-in EAP authentication, traffic selector negotiation, and better handling of NAT-T. More efficient and reliable than IKEv1.
ndc.takeaway: IKEv2 vs IKEv1: Fewer messages, MOBIKE (roaming), built-in EAP, simpler; preferred modern choice

ndc.q: What is a VPN security consideration regarding Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)?
opts: PFS stores session keys permanently for auditing
PFS generates new Diffie-Hellman keys for each session so that compromising long-term keys does not allow decryption of past recorded sessions
PFS is only needed for file transfers
PFS reduces VPN performance without security benefit
ndc.expl: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) uses ephemeral Diffie-Hellman key exchange for each session. Even if the VPN server's long-term private key is compromised, previously recorded encrypted sessions cannot be decrypted because each session used unique, discarded DH keys.
ndc.takeaway: PFS = New DH keys per session; compromised long-term key cannot decrypt past sessions

ndc.q: What is a hybrid VPN and when is it used?
opts: A VPN combining two different brands
A VPN architecture combining IPSec for site-to-site connections and SSL VPN for remote access users, leveraging each protocol's strengths
A VPN combining IPv4 and IPv6
A VPN that uses both encryption and compression
ndc.expl: Hybrid VPN combines IPSec tunnels (reliable, high-performance for permanent site-to-site) with SSL VPN (flexible, browser-friendly for remote users). Many enterprise deployments use both: IPSec between data centers and offices, SSL VPN for individual remote workers.
ndc.takeaway: Hybrid VPN = IPSec (site-to-site strength) + SSL VPN (remote access flexibility)

ndc.q: What VPN vulnerabilities should organizations be aware of?
opts: VPNs have no known vulnerabilities

Common vulnerabilities: weak authentication (default credentials), unpatched VPN software (CVEs like CVE-2019-11510 Pulse VPN), credential reuse from phishing, split tunnel misconfigurations, and outdated cipher suites
All VPN protocols are equally secure
Only PPTP has vulnerabilities
ndc.expl: VPN vulnerabilities: unpatched VPN appliances (CVE-2019-11510 Pulse Secure, CVE-2020-12812 FortiVPN), credential stuffing (reused passwords), weak/default configs (PSK exposure, weak ciphers), split tunnel pivoting, and phished VPN credentials enabling network access.
ndc.takeaway: VPN vulnerabilities: Unpatched appliances, credential reuse, weak ciphers, split tunnel pivoting

ndc.q: How does SSTP (Secure Socket Tunneling Protocol) work and what are its advantages?
opts: SSTP uses GRE tunneling over port 443
SSTP encapsulates PPP traffic over SSL/TLS using TCP port 443, allowing it to pass through most firewalls and web proxies that allow HTTPS; supported natively in Windows
SSTP is only for mobile devices
SSTP uses UDP for better performance
ndc.expl: SSTP (developed by Microsoft) encapsulates PPP over SSL/TLS (HTTPS, port 443). Since it uses port 443, it passes through firewalls and web proxies without special configuration. Native Windows support makes it easy for Windows clients, but it is proprietary to Microsoft.
ndc.takeaway: SSTP = PPP over SSL on port 443; passes through firewalls; Windows native; Microsoft proprietary

ndc.q: What is the Diffie-Hellman key exchange and its role in VPN?
opts: A symmetric key encryption algorithm
A cryptographic method allowing two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel without transmitting the secret; fundamental to VPN key exchange (IKE, TLS)
A key storage mechanism
A hash function for VPN authentication
ndc.expl: Diffie-Hellman (DH) allows two parties to compute a shared secret using public values without transmitting the secret. VPN uses DH groups (DH14 minimum, DH19-21 preferred with ECDH) during IKE phase 1. The shared secret is used to derive session encryption keys.
ndc.takeaway: DH key exchange = Compute shared secret over public channel; VPN uses DH in IKE (DH14+ recommended)

ndc.q: What is VPN split tunneling DNS leak and how is it prevented?
opts: DNS is not relevant to split tunneling

When split tunneling routes internet traffic directly, DNS queries for corporate hostnames may also go to public DNS servers, revealing internal hostnames; prevented by forcing all DNS through VPN DNS server
DNS leaks only occur with PPTP
All VPN protocols prevent DNS leaks automatically
ndc.expl: DNS leaks occur in split tunnel VPN when the system's DNS resolver sends queries to public DNS servers (ISP or 8.8.8.8) for corporate hostnames, exposing internal naming conventions. Prevention: force all DNS through VPN-assigned DNS server, use DNS-over-HTTPS, or use full tunnel.
ndc.takeaway: DNS leak = Corporate DNS queries going to public DNS in split tunnel; fix by forcing DNS via VPN

ndc.q: What is the role of PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) in enterprise VPN?
opts: PKI manages VPN bandwidth
PKI provides X.509 certificates for strong mutual authentication between VPN peers/clients, eliminating shared password weaknesses; the CA signs VPN server and user certificates
PKI is not used in VPN
PKI only manages website certificates
ndc.expl: PKI-based VPN authentication: each VPN server and client has a unique X.509 certificate signed by the enterprise CA. Authentication is mutual — both sides verify each other's certificate. Eliminates credential-stuffing attacks and provides non-repudiation. Required for large-scale deployments.
ndc.takeaway: PKI in VPN = X.509 certificates for mutual authentication; eliminates shared credential weaknesses

ndc.q: What is IPSec AH (Authentication Header) and when is it used?
opts: AH provides encryption for IPSec
AH (protocol 51) provides data integrity and authentication for entire IP packet (including IP header) but NO encryption; used when only authentication is required, not confidentiality
AH is the main IPSec encryption method
AH is used only in tunnel mode
ndc.expl: IPSec AH (protocol 51) calculates an HMAC over the entire IP packet (including headers that don't change during routing). It provides integrity and source authentication but NOT confidentiality. Used in scenarios where authentication without encryption is required. Incompatible with NAT.
ndc.takeaway: IPSec AH = Integrity + Authentication (NO encryption, protocol 51); broken by NAT

ndc.q: How does VPN help with regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS)?
opts: VPN is not related to compliance
VPN provides encryption in transit (required by GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS), access logging (audit trail), and controlled access to sensitive systems; helps demonstrate due diligence
VPN replaces all compliance requirements
Only SSL VPN satisfies compliance requirements
ndc.expl: VPNs help compliance by: encrypting data in transit (GDPR Art. 32, HIPAA 164.312(e)(2), PCI-DSS 4.1), creating access logs for audit trails, enforcing access control to sensitive resources, and providing secure remote access that can be monitored and controlled.
ndc.takeaway: VPN compliance: Encrypts data in transit + access logs + controlled access; helps GDPR/HIPAA/PCI-DSS

ndc.q: What is a SYN flood attack?
opts: Flooding a server with HTTP requests
Sending many TCP SYN packets without completing the handshake, exhausting the server's connection table (half-open connections)
Flooding a network with ICMP packets
Sending malformed UDP packets to a server
ndc.expl: A SYN flood exploits TCP three-way handshake: attacker sends many SYN packets but never sends the final ACK. Server allocates resources for each half-open connection waiting for ACK. Connection table fills up, preventing legitimate connections.
ndc.takeaway: SYN flood = Many SYN + no ACK = exhausts server's half-open connection table

ndc.q: What is an amplification attack in DDoS?
opts: Using larger packet sizes
Sending small requests to servers that respond with much larger replies, using spoofed victim IP; amplifies bandwidth toward victim (DNS, NTP, SSDP amplification)
Amplifying botnet size
Using faster attack tools
ndc.expl: Amplification attacks use protocols with asymmetric request/response ratios. Attacker sends small queries with spoofed victim's source IP to multiple open servers (DNS resolvers, NTP servers). Servers send large replies to the victim, amplifying bandwidth. DNS amplification: 50x factor; NTP: 556x.
ndc.takeaway: Amplification DDoS = Small spoofed request → Large reply to victim; DNS/NTP/SSDP used

ndc.q: What is QoS (Quality of Service)?
opts: A security certification standard
A set of techniques for managing network resources to provide different priority levels to different types of traffic, ensuring performance for critical applications
A type of firewall rule
A VPN protocol
ndc.expl: QoS manages network traffic prioritization and bandwidth allocation. It ensures critical traffic (VoIP, video conferencing, business applications) gets priority over less important traffic (file downloads, streaming). Parameters: bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss.
ndc.takeaway: QoS = Traffic prioritization; ensures critical apps (VoIP, video) get guaranteed bandwidth

ndc.q: What is traffic shaping?
opts: Monitoring network traffic patterns
Controlling the rate and timing of traffic flow to enforce bandwidth limits, reduce congestion, and ensure fair usage
Encrypting network traffic
A type of packet filtering
ndc.expl: Traffic shaping (packet shaping) controls data flow rates — delaying low-priority traffic to smooth bursts and prioritize critical traffic. Uses techniques like token bucket and leaky bucket algorithms. Can also throttle specific applications (P2P throttling).
ndc.takeaway: Traffic shaping = Control traffic rate; delay low-priority; token/leaky bucket algorithms

ndc.q: What is a ping of death attack?
opts: Sending many ICMP ping requests
Sending a malformed ICMP packet larger than the maximum IP packet size (65535 bytes), causing buffer overflows in the target OS
Pinging a target continuously
Sending ICMP packets with wrong checksums
ndc.expl: Ping of Death sends an oversized ICMP packet (>65535 bytes) which gets fragmented. When the target reassembles fragments, the buffer overflows, crashing or corrupting the system. Modern OSes are patched against this but it was a major attack in the 1990s.
ndc.takeaway: Ping of Death = Oversized ICMP packet (>65535 bytes) → buffer overflow on reassembly

ndc.q: What is rate limiting as a DoS mitigation technique?
opts: Limiting the number of network devices
Restricting the rate of traffic from individual IPs or connection types to prevent a single source from overwhelming resources
Limiting total network bandwidth
Blocking all ICMP traffic
ndc.expl: Rate limiting restricts the number of requests/packets an individual IP or connection type can send per second. Excess packets are dropped or delayed. Implemented in iptables (--limit), nginx (limit_req), and cloud WAF/DDoS protection services.
ndc.takeaway: Rate limiting = Restrict requests per IP per second; blocks single-source flooding (iptables --limit)

ndc.q: What is Anycast routing used for in DDoS mitigation?
opts: Routing traffic to the closest server for performance only
Distributing incoming traffic across multiple geographically distributed nodes so DDoS attacks are absorbed and distributed globally rather than overwhelming a single location
A routing protocol for internal networks
A load balancing algorithm for databases
ndc.expl: Anycast routing assigns the same IP address to servers in multiple locations. DNS and CDN providers use anycast to distribute DDoS traffic across their global network — no single location is overwhelmed. Used by Cloudflare, Akamai, and major DDoS mitigation services.
ndc.takeaway: Anycast = Same IP at multiple locations; DDoS traffic distributed globally, no single point overwhelmed

ndc.q: What is a blackhole route (null route) for DDoS mitigation?
opts: A routing error causing packet loss
Routing all traffic destined for a DDoS target IP to a null interface (dropping it), stopping the attack but also blocking legitimate traffic
A BGP routing protocol
A VPN routing method
ndc.expl: Blackhole routing (null routing) directs all traffic to a victim IP to /dev/null (a virtual interface). Stops the attack immediately but also blocks all legitimate traffic to that IP. Used as an emergency measure — ISP RTBH (Remotely Triggered Blackhole) is a common implementation.
ndc.takeaway: Blackhole route = Drop ALL traffic to attacked IP (stops attack + legitimate traffic); emergency measure

ndc.q: What is ARP spoofing (ARP poisoning)?
opts: Spoofing an ARP server
Sending fake ARP replies mapping the attacker's MAC address to a legitimate IP address, redirecting traffic through the attacker for MitM attacks
A type of DNS attack
Flooding a network with ARP requests
ndc.expl: ARP spoofing sends unsolicited ARP replies poisoning the ARP cache of victims with a false MAC-to-IP mapping. Traffic destined for the spoofed IP is sent to the attacker instead, enabling Man-in-the-Middle attacks. Mitigated by Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on switches.
ndc.takeaway: ARP spoofing = Fake ARP replies; redirect traffic through attacker (MitM); mitigated by DAI

ndc.q: What is the Slowloris attack?
opts: A fast DDoS attack using multiple sources
A slow HTTP DoS attack that holds many partial connections open by sending incomplete HTTP headers, exhausting server connections without much bandwidth
A ICMP flood attack
A type of SQL injection
ndc.expl: Slowloris opens many HTTP connections to a server and sends partial HTTP headers periodically, keeping connections open indefinitely. The server's connection limit is exhausted while the attack uses minimal bandwidth — hard to detect. Mitigated by connection timeouts and limits per IP.
ndc.takeaway: Slowloris = Slow HTTP attack; holds many partial connections open; exhausts server with minimal bandwidth

ndc.q: What is RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) and how does it help prevent insider threats?
opts: A routing protocol for access control
A model assigning permissions to roles rather than individuals; users get permissions by being assigned to roles; limits access to minimum needed, reducing insider threat damage
A firewall access control mechanism
A network segmentation technique
ndc.expl: RBAC assigns permissions to roles (Admin, Editor, Viewer) and users are assigned to roles. Users only access what their role permits — implementing least privilege. If an insider's account is compromised or they act maliciously, they can only damage what their role permits.
ndc.takeaway: RBAC = Permissions assigned to roles, not individuals; implements least privilege; limits insider threat

ndc.q: What are SYN cookies and how do they mitigate SYN flood attacks?
opts: Cookies are web browser features unrelated to SYN floods
SYN cookies encode connection state in the sequence number of SYN-ACK packets instead of allocating memory, eliminating half-open connection table exhaustion
SYN cookies are a cryptographic technique for VPN
SYN cookies prevent all DDoS attacks
ndc.expl: SYN cookies encode the server-side connection state (hash of IP, port, timestamp) into the TCP initial sequence number in the SYN-ACK. No state is stored until the client sends valid ACK with the correct sequence. Prevents half-open table exhaustion while maintaining legitimate connection handling.
ndc.takeaway: SYN cookies = Encode state in seq number; no memory allocated until valid ACK; prevents SYN flood

ndc.q: What is traffic policing vs traffic shaping?
opts: They are identical techniques
Traffic policing drops or re-marks traffic exceeding the rate limit immediately; traffic shaping delays excess traffic by buffering and releasing it smoothly without dropping
Policing is for inbound; shaping for outbound only
Policing uses more bandwidth than shaping
ndc.expl: Traffic policing enforces rate limits by immediately dropping or re-marking (DSCP) packets exceeding the configured rate. Traffic shaping buffers excess traffic and releases it at the configured rate without dropping — smoother but adds latency. Policing is harsher; shaping is gentler.
ndc.takeaway: Policing = Drop/remark exceeding traffic immediately; Shaping = Buffer + release smoothly (no drop)

ndc.q: What is DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) marking?
opts: A type of firewall rule marking
A 6-bit field in the IP header used to mark packets for QoS priority treatment; allows routers to classify and queue traffic by priority
A type of encryption marking
A DNS security extension
ndc.expl: DSCP (6 bits in IP ToS field) marks packets for QoS treatment. Common values: EF (Expedited Forwarding, 46) for VoIP; AF classes for different priority levels; BE (Best Effort, 0) for default. Routers use DSCP to determine queue priority and scheduling.
ndc.takeaway: DSCP = 6-bit IP header field for QoS marking; EF (46)=VoIP priority; routers use it for queue priority

ndc.q: What is DDoS scrubbing (traffic scrubbing)?
opts: Physically cleaning network hardware
Routing attack traffic through a scrubbing center where attack traffic is filtered and only clean traffic is forwarded to the destination
Removing malware from network packets
Cleaning up old firewall rules
ndc.expl: DDoS scrubbing centers receive all traffic destined for the victim via BGP re-routing, apply multi-layer filtering to separate attack traffic from legitimate traffic, and forward only clean traffic to the victim. Used by Akamai, Cloudflare, and ISP-based scrubbing services.
ndc.takeaway: Scrubbing = Route attack traffic to scrubbing center; filter attack; forward clean traffic to victim

ndc.q: What is ingress filtering (BCP38) and how does it prevent DDoS amplification?
opts: Filtering inbound VPN connections
ISPs filtering outbound traffic with source IPs not belonging to their address space, preventing customers from sending spoofed traffic used in amplification attacks
A web application firewall technique
Filtering emails for spam
ndc.expl: BCP38 (RFC 2827) recommends ISPs implement ingress filtering — dropping packets leaving their network with source IPs not from their allocated ranges. This prevents customers from spoofing other IPs used in amplification DDoS attacks. Widely recommended but not universally implemented.
ndc.takeaway: BCP38 = ISPs drop packets with spoofed source IPs; prevents amplification DDoS spoofing

ndc.q: What is jitter and how does it affect real-time applications?
opts: Jitter is network latency
Variation in packet arrival time (delay variation); real-time apps (VoIP, video) need consistent timing — high jitter causes choppy audio/video; mitigated by playout buffers
Jitter is packet loss
Jitter is network bandwidth variation
ndc.expl: Jitter is the variation in latency between consecutive packets. Real-time applications (VoIP, video conferencing) require consistent packet timing. High jitter causes audio dropouts, video freezing, and poor quality. Mitigated by QoS prioritization and playout buffers at endpoints.
ndc.takeaway: Jitter = Variation in packet arrival time; causes choppy VoIP/video; mitigated by QoS + playout buffers

ndc.q: What is BGP blackholing for DDoS mitigation?
opts: A BGP routing error
Advertising the victim's IP prefix with a specific BGP community to upstream providers, causing them to route attack traffic to null (drop it) before it reaches the victim's network
A BGP security feature
A type of BGP route filtering
ndc.expl: RTBH (Remotely Triggered Blackhole) uses BGP communities to signal upstream providers to drop traffic destined for the attacked IP. Traffic is absorbed upstream before reaching the victim's link, protecting the network but sacrificing the attacked IP service.
ndc.takeaway: RTBH = Signal ISPs via BGP to drop attack traffic upstream; protects network but kills IP service

ndc.q: What is the 'low and slow' DDoS attack technique?
opts: A DDoS from geographically distant sources
Sending traffic just below detection thresholds over a long period, gradually exhausting resources without triggering rate-limit alarms
A slow network scanning technique
DDoS using older, slower attack tools
ndc.expl: Low and slow attacks send minimal traffic (just enough to keep connections or resources occupied) over extended periods, flying under threshold-based detection. Examples: Slowloris (HTTP), RUDY (R-U-Dead-Yet), slow POST attacks. Require behavioral analysis rather than rate-based detection.
ndc.takeaway: Low and slow DDoS = Below threshold, sustained attack; evades rate limits; needs behavioral detection

ndc.q: How does DNSSEC prevent DNS spoofing?
opts: DNSSEC encrypts DNS queries
DNSSEC uses digital signatures to verify DNS record authenticity; resolvers verify the signature against the domain's public key, rejecting unsigned or tampered records
DNSSEC blocks all DNS queries
DNSSEC uses VPN for DNS traffic
ndc.expl: DNSSEC adds digital signatures (RRSIG records) to DNS records using the zone's private key. Resolvers verify signatures using the published public key (DNSKEY). Tampered or spoofed records fail signature verification and are rejected, preventing cache poisoning.
ndc.takeaway: DNSSEC = Digitally signs DNS records; resolvers verify signature; prevents cache poisoning

ndc.q: What is application layer (Layer 7) DDoS and why is it harder to mitigate than volumetric DDoS?
opts: Layer 7 DDoS uses more bandwidth

Layer 7 attacks target specific application functions (HTTP GET/POST floods, Slowloris) using seemingly legitimate requests that exhaust server resources without high bandwidth; hard to distinguish from legitimate users
Layer 7 DDoS is less damaging
Layer 7 attacks only affect mobile applications
ndc.expl: Layer 7 DDoS sends seemingly legitimate application requests (HTTP floods, search queries, login attempts). Each request consumes significant server resources (CPU, DB queries). Low bandwidth requirements make them hard to detect by volume. Mitigation requires behavioral analysis, CAPTCHAs, and rate limiting per user behavior.
ndc.takeaway: Layer 7 DDoS = Low bandwidth, legitimate-looking requests; exhausts server resources; needs behavioral detection

ndc.q: What is DNS amplification DDoS and what amplification factor does it achieve?
opts: DNS amplification uses TCP and achieves 2x amplification
Attacker sends small DNS ANY queries to open resolvers with spoofed victim IP; resolvers send large responses (DNS ANY can return 2,000+ bytes for 40-byte request); amplification factor up to 50-70x
DNS amplification only affects authoritative servers
DNS amplification achieves 1:1 ratio
ndc.expl: DNS amplification: attacker sends 40-60 byte queries for ANY records (or DNSSEC-enabled domains) to open DNS resolvers with spoofed victim IP. Resolvers send large responses (2KB+) to victim. Amplification factor: 50-70x. Mitigated by BCP38, response rate limiting (RRL), and blocking open resolvers.
ndc.takeaway: DNS amplification: 40-byte query → 2KB+ response; 50-70x amplification; mitigate by RRL + BCP38

ndc.q: What is NTP amplification DDoS and why is it particularly effective?
opts: NTP amplification uses small NTP servers
Exploiting NTP monlist command: tiny request gets response listing last 600 clients (2,000+ bytes); amplification factor up to 556x, making it one of the most effective amplification vectors
NTP attacks only affect time synchronization
NTP amplification uses TCP
ndc.expl: NTP amplification uses the 'monlist' command (ntpdc -n -c monlist) which returns the last 600 hosts that queried the NTP server. A 46-byte request generates a ~46KB response — 1,000x amplification in some cases. Mitigated by disabling monlist, upgrading NTP, or firewall blocking.
ndc.takeaway: NTP monlist amplification: up to 1000x; disable monlist; upgrade NTP to prevent

ndc.q: What is the difference between reactive and proactive DDoS mitigation?
opts: Proactive is less effective than reactive

Reactive mitigation activates after detecting an attack (blackholing, rate limiting, scrubbing); proactive mitigation uses always-on filtering, threat intel-based blocking, and pre-positioned anycast infrastructure
They use identical techniques
Proactive requires more hardware
ndc.expl: Reactive DDoS mitigation activates during an attack (manual or automatic). Proactive includes: always-on scrubbing (cloud-based), pre-configured rate limits, threat intel-based IP blocking, anycast infrastructure distribution, and DDoS protection plans pre-positioned before attacks occur.
ndc.takeaway: Reactive = Activate during attack; Proactive = Always-on protection before attack (cloud DDoS, anycast)

ndc.q: How does a reflective DDoS (DRDoS) attack work and why is it effective?
opts: DRDoS attacks use reflected light to disrupt networks
Attacker spoofs victim's IP and sends requests to legitimate third-party servers (reflectors); servers send large responses to victim; attacker hides behind legitimate servers and amplifies traffic
DRDoS is a type of phishing
Reflectors are the primary attack targets
ndc.expl: DRDoS uses innocent third-party servers (reflectors) as intermediaries. Attacker spoofs victim's IP in requests to many reflectors (DNS, NTP, SSDP servers). Reflectors send large legitimate-looking responses to victim. Advantages: hides attacker, amplifies traffic, uses legitimate servers making filtering harder.
ndc.takeaway: DRDoS = Spoof victim IP → reflectors send large replies to victim; hides attacker + amplifies

ndc.q: What is memcached DDoS amplification and what made it the largest DDoS in history?
opts: Memcached DDoS is not a real attack vector

Memcached servers (port 11211/UDP) have no authentication; attacker stores payload, spoofs victim IP in request; server responds with full payload; amplification up to 51,000x; used in 2018 GitHub attack (1.35 Tbps)
Memcached amplification only achieves 2x
Memcached DDoS only affects database servers
ndc.expl: Memcached (distributed caching) was designed for internal networks but many were exposed to the internet with no authentication. Attack: pre-store large data in memcached, send small GET request with spoofed victim IP → server sends full data to victim. Up to 51,000x amplification used in 2018 GitHub (1.35 Tbps) and later Netscout (1.7 Tbps) attacks.
ndc.takeaway: Memcached DDoS: up to 51,000x amplification; 2018 GitHub 1.35 Tbps record; fix by blocking UDP 11211

ndc.q: What is a BGP hijacking attack and how does it relate to DDoS?
opts: A routing error in BGP

Maliciously or mistakenly announcing more-specific BGP prefixes for someone else's IP space, causing traffic to be routed to the attacker; can redirect DDoS traffic away from victim or route victim's traffic through attacker for surveillance
A DDoS attack against BGP routers
BGP hijacking is not security-relevant
ndc.expl: BGP hijacking involves announcing more-specific or equal-specific prefixes for IP space belonging to others. Effects: can steal traffic (surveillance, credential theft), perform BGP blackholing of targets, redirect DDoS traffic, or cause service outages. Mitigated by RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure).
ndc.takeaway: BGP hijacking = Fake route announcement for others' IPs; traffic theft; mitigated by RPKI

ndc.q: What is RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) and how does it prevent IP hijacking?
opts: A PKI for VPN certificates

A cryptographic framework allowing IP address holders to create Route Origin Authorizations (ROAs) signed with certificates, allowing routers to validate BGP announcements and reject unauthorized ones
A routing protocol security extension
A DNS security mechanism
ndc.expl: RPKI allows IP block owners to create cryptographically signed ROAs (Route Origin Authorizations) stating which AS can originate their prefix. Routers performing origin validation can reject BGP announcements that don't have valid ROAs, preventing unauthorized prefix hijacking.
ndc.takeaway: RPKI = Cryptographically signed ROAs; validates BGP origin; prevents IP prefix hijacking

ndc.q: How do you mitigate UDP-based DDoS amplification attacks at the network level?
opts: Block all UDP traffic
Implement BCP38 ingress filtering, response rate limiting (DNS RRL), block known amplifier ports (53, 123, 161, 11211, 1900) from external access, use anycast distribution, and deploy scrubbing
Rate limiting UDP is sufficient
Only patch the application servers
ndc.expl: UDP amplification mitigation: BCP38 prevents IP spoofing at source ISPs, DNS/NTP RRL (Response Rate Limiting) limits reflection potential, block amplifier protocols from internet (firewall UDP 123/161/11211/1900), anycast distributes traffic, and scrubbing centers filter volumetric attacks.
ndc.takeaway: UDP amplification mitigation: BCP38 + RRL + block amplifier ports + anycast + scrubbing

ndc.q: What is the difference between a DDoS on-premise mitigation vs cloud-based DDoS protection?
opts: Cloud protection is always better

On-premise (CPE-based) mitigation uses local hardware (typically up to 10-40 Gbps capacity); cloud-based protection uses ISP/CDN scrubbing centers with Tbps capacity, more suitable for volumetric attacks exceeding internet link capacity
They provide identical protection levels
On-premise protection works for cloud attacks; cloud protection is for on-premise attacks
ndc.expl: On-premise DDoS hardware (Radware, F5) is limited by the internet uplink bandwidth — if attack exceeds 10 Gbps and uplink is saturated, on-premise can't help. Cloud scrubbing (Cloudflare, Akamai) absorbs attacks upstream at Tbps scale before they reach the victim's link.
ndc.takeaway: On-premise DDoS limited by uplink bandwidth; cloud scrubbing absorbs Tbps attacks before reaching you

ndc.q: What is SSL termination at a reverse proxy?
opts: Terminating SSL certificates permanently
Decrypting HTTPS connections at the reverse proxy and forwarding plain HTTP to backend servers, reducing backend CPU load and simplifying certificate management
Renewing SSL certificates
Blocking all non-SSL traffic
ndc.expl: SSL termination decrypts TLS at the reverse proxy. Backend servers receive plain HTTP (or re-encrypted internal traffic), reducing their CPU overhead for crypto operations. Certificate management is centralized at the proxy rather than on each backend server.
ndc.takeaway: SSL termination at proxy = Decrypt TLS at proxy; backend gets HTTP; simplifies cert management

ndc.q: What is a Web Application Firewall (WAF) and how does it differ from a traditional firewall?
opts: WAF is a hardware device; traditional is software
WAF specifically inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic for web attacks (SQLi, XSS, CSRF); traditional firewall inspects network headers; WAF understands web application context
They are identical devices
WAF is slower than traditional firewall
ndc.expl: A WAF inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic at Layer 7 understanding web application semantics: request parameters, cookies, headers, session tokens. Detects OWASP Top 10 attacks. A traditional firewall inspects network and transport layer headers (IP, TCP/UDP) without understanding application content.
ndc.takeaway: WAF = Layer 7 HTTP inspection; detects SQLi/XSS/CSRF; traditional FW = Layer 3-4 only

ndc.q: What is a syslog server and what is its role in security monitoring?
opts: A server that runs system processes
A centralized server collecting log messages from network devices, servers, and security appliances for storage, analysis, and alerting
A server managing system updates
A backup server for system configurations
ndc.expl: A syslog server collects log messages from routers, switches, firewalls, servers, and applications using the syslog protocol (UDP/TCP port 514). Centralizes logs for analysis, correlation, compliance storage, and forensic investigation.
ndc.takeaway: Syslog server = Centralized log collection; syslog protocol; UDP/TCP 514; enables correlation

ndc.q: What does SIEM stand for and what is its primary purpose?
opts: Security Inspection and Event Monitoring
Security Information and Event Management; centralizes log collection, correlates events to detect threats, and provides alerting and reporting
System Integrity and Event Management
Secure Infrastructure and Enterprise Management
ndc.expl: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) collects and aggregates logs from across the infrastructure, normalizes them, correlates related events to identify attack patterns, generates alerts, and provides dashboards and reports for SOC analysts. Examples: Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel.
ndc.takeaway: SIEM = Collect + Normalize + Correlate logs → Alert + Report; backbone of SOC operations

ndc.q: What is log correlation in SIEM?
opts: Sorting logs chronologically
Analyzing events from multiple sources to identify relationships and patterns indicating security incidents that individual logs would not reveal
Compressing logs for storage
Encrypting log files
ndc.expl: Log correlation analyzes events from different systems (firewall, IDS, endpoint, authentication) to identify related events forming an attack chain. For example: firewall allows connection + IDS detects exploit + endpoint shows new process = likely compromise across three log sources.
ndc.takeaway: Log correlation = Connect related events across systems/time to reveal attack chains

ndc.q: What is a DMZ virtual host?
opts: A type of VLAN
A virtual machine or server hosted in the DMZ network segment providing public-facing services while being isolated from internal network
A cloud hosting service
A virtualized firewall
ndc.expl: A DMZ virtual host is a server (physical or virtual) placed in the DMZ segment, accessible from the internet for specific services (web, email, DNS) while firewalls prevent it from directly accessing the internal network. Isolates internet-facing services from internal resources.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ virtual host = Internet-accessible server in DMZ; firewalls prevent access to internal network

ndc.q: What is nginx used for in web infrastructure?
opts: A database management system
A high-performance web server and reverse proxy that can serve static content, load balance, perform SSL termination, and act as API gateway
A network monitoring tool
A type of VPN server
ndc.expl: Nginx is a high-performance, event-driven web server and reverse proxy. Uses: serve static content (faster than Apache for concurrency), reverse proxy for backend apps, load balancer across backend servers, SSL/TLS termination, rate limiting, and caching.
ndc.takeaway: Nginx = High-performance web server + reverse proxy + load balancer + SSL termination

ndc.q: What is Squid and how is it used as a security control?
opts: A type of IDS
An open-source caching HTTP proxy that provides web access control, content filtering, user authentication, logging, and bandwidth management
A network scanning tool
A firewall management tool
ndc.expl: Squid is an open-source forward proxy/cache server. Security uses: enforcing acceptable use policies (block adult/malicious sites), logging all web requests, authenticating users before granting internet access, inspecting URLs, and caching content to reduce bandwidth.
ndc.takeaway: Squid = Caching proxy; enforces web access control, logs requests, authenticates users, caches content

ndc.q: What is server load balancing and why is it important for security?
opts: Distributing weight across physical server racks
Distributing client requests across multiple backend servers for availability and performance; also important for DDoS mitigation and ensuring no single server is overwhelmed
A type of RAID configuration
A network routing protocol
ndc.expl: Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple backend servers for performance, scalability, and high availability. Security benefits: distributes DDoS attacks across multiple servers, hides individual server IPs, enables health checking to redirect away from compromised servers.
ndc.takeaway: Load balancing = Distribute traffic across servers; high availability + DDoS distribution

ndc.q: What WAF deployment modes exist and how do they differ?
opts: WAF has only one deployment mode
Three modes: inline (traffic flows through; can block attacks), out-of-band (receives copies; alert only), and reverse proxy mode (WAF is the endpoint; most common for web applications)
WAF is always deployed out-of-band
WAF is always inline
ndc.expl: WAF deployment modes: Inline (transparent bridge — active blocking but failure = outage), Out-of-band (passive monitoring — no blocking, only alerts), Reverse proxy (WAF terminates connections — full visibility and blocking, most secure but adds hop). Reverse proxy is most common for web app protection.
ndc.takeaway: WAF modes: Inline (bridge), Out-of-band (passive), Reverse proxy (terminates connections; most common)

ndc.q: What is OWASP Top 10 and why is it relevant to WAF configuration?
opts: A list of top 10 security companies
The 10 most critical web application security risks; WAF rules should specifically target these: SQLi, XSS, broken access control, cryptographic failures, security misconfiguration, SSRF, etc.
A framework for network security
A list of the top 10 DDoS attacks
ndc.expl: OWASP Top 10 (latest: 2021) lists the 10 most critical web application risks. WAF vendors develop rule sets specifically targeting these: SQLi (A03), XSS (A03), Broken Access Control (A01), SSRF (A10), etc. WAF configuration should include OWASP Top 10 coverage as baseline.
ndc.takeaway: OWASP Top 10 = Critical web risks; WAF rules should cover: SQLi, XSS, Broken Access, SSRF

ndc.q: What is a WAF positive security model vs negative security model?
opts: Positive allows all; negative blocks all
