A policy defining what users are permitted and prohibited from doing with organizational IT systems and data (e.g., no personal use, no P2P, no sharing credentials)
A network access control list
A firewall ruleset
ndc.expl: An AUP (Acceptable Use Policy) sets expectations for how employees may use organizational technology resources: permitted activities, prohibited activities (downloading pirated content, sharing credentials, visiting inappropriate sites), monitoring notice, and consequences for violations.
ndc.takeaway: AUP = Policy defining permitted/prohibited use of organizational IT; monitoring notice and consequences

ndc.q: What is security awareness training and why is it important?
opts: Technical training for security professionals only
Education for all employees on security threats (phishing, social engineering, password hygiene, incident reporting) to reduce human-factor security incidents
A compliance checkbox exercise
Training only for IT staff
ndc.expl: Security awareness training educates all employees (not just IT) on: recognizing phishing, social engineering red flags, secure password practices, handling sensitive data, incident reporting procedures, and physical security. Humans are the most frequently exploited attack vector — training reduces this risk.
ndc.takeaway: Security awareness training = Educate all employees; phishing recognition, passwords, incident reporting

ndc.q: What is a security audit?
opts: A financial audit of security spending
A systematic examination of security controls, policies, and procedures to determine whether they meet defined requirements and are functioning effectively
A penetration test
A vulnerability scan
ndc.expl: A security audit systematically evaluates security controls against a standard (ISO 27001, NIST, PCI-DSS). Includes review of policies, configuration checks, access reviews, log review, and control effectiveness testing. Identifies gaps between security posture and required standards.
ndc.takeaway: Security audit = Systematic evaluation against standards; policy review + config check + control testing

ndc.q: What is vulnerability management and what is its lifecycle?
opts: Deleting vulnerable software
A continuous process: Discover (scan for vulnerabilities) → Prioritize (CVSS scoring + asset criticality) → Remediate (patch, compensating control) → Verify (rescan to confirm fix) → Report
A one-time security assessment
Installing antivirus on all systems
ndc.expl: Vulnerability management lifecycle: Discover (regular scanning with Nessus/OpenVAS), Prioritize (CVSS score × asset criticality), Remediate (patching, compensating controls for unpatchable systems), Verify (rescan to confirm fix), Report (trend metrics, SLA compliance). Continuous process, not one-time.
ndc.takeaway: Vuln management cycle: Discover → Prioritize (CVSS × criticality) → Remediate → Verify → Report

ndc.q: What is a security information policy and how should data classification drive security controls?
opts: A policy about press releases

A framework classifying information by sensitivity (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) and mandating corresponding controls: encryption, access restrictions, retention periods, and handling requirements
A policy for IT infrastructure security only
A password management policy
ndc.expl: Data classification drives security controls: Public (no restriction), Internal (access control), Confidential (encryption at rest+transit, need-to-know access, DLP), Restricted/Secret (strongest controls: MFA, audit logging, limited access, secure disposal). Classification must be applied at data creation.
ndc.takeaway: Data classification: Public→Internal→Confidential→Restricted; classification drives encryption+access+audit controls

ndc.q: What is network access control (NAC) and how does it enforce security policy?
opts: An ACL on network devices

A security framework that evaluates device compliance (OS patched, antivirus current, disk encryption enabled) before granting network access; non-compliant devices are quarantined or given limited access
A user authentication system
A type of firewall
ndc.expl: NAC (e.g., Cisco ISE, ForeScout) assesses endpoint compliance before and after network access: checks OS patch level, antivirus status, disk encryption, host firewall. Non-compliant devices are quarantined (limited network segment with remediation access). Enforces security policy at network access point.
ndc.takeaway: NAC = Assess endpoint compliance (patched, AV, encryption) before granting access; quarantine if non-compliant

ndc.q: What is a QoS policy and what are its main components?
opts: A firewall rule set for quality control

A set of rules prioritizing network traffic to meet performance requirements; components: classification (marking traffic by type), queuing (prioritizing queues), scheduling (dequeuing order), policing/shaping (rate control)
A compliance policy for network quality
A VPN performance configuration
ndc.expl: QoS policy components: Classification (identify traffic type using DSCP, CoS, 5-tuple), Marking (apply DSCP value), Queuing (assign to priority queue), Scheduling (LLQ for VoIP, CBWFQ for data), Policing (drop excess) or Shaping (buffer excess). Ensures VoIP/video gets consistent treatment.
ndc.takeaway: QoS policy: Classification → Marking (DSCP) → Queuing → Scheduling (LLQ/CBWFQ) → Policing/Shaping

ndc.q: What is LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) and why is it used for VoIP?
opts: A queuing algorithm for HTTP traffic

LLQ provides a strict priority queue for delay-sensitive traffic (VoIP, video) that is always served first; other traffic uses CBWFQ; ensures VoIP packets are never delayed more than configured maximum delay
LLQ is used for file downloads
LLQ reduces overall network bandwidth
ndc.expl: LLQ combines Strict Priority Queuing (PQ) for delay-sensitive traffic with Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing (CBWFQ) for other traffic. VoIP packets go to the strict priority queue and are dequeued before any other class. Without LLQ, VoIP packets wait in queue causing jitter and poor call quality.
ndc.takeaway: LLQ = Strict priority queue for VoIP; always served first; prevents queuing jitter for real-time traffic

ndc.q: What is CBWFQ (Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing)?
opts: A traffic shaping algorithm for WAN
A queuing algorithm dividing bandwidth into user-defined classes with guaranteed minimum bandwidth; each class gets its configured bandwidth allocation, preventing one class from starving others
A routing protocol for QoS
A wireless QoS standard
ndc.expl: CBWFQ allows defining traffic classes (web traffic, database, backup) each with guaranteed minimum bandwidth allocation. If a class has no traffic, other classes can use its bandwidth. If all classes are active, each gets at least its minimum guarantee. Prevents high-volume traffic from starving critical applications.
ndc.takeaway: CBWFQ = Classes with guaranteed minimum bandwidth; prevents starvation; fair sharing when idle

ndc.q: What is WRED (Weighted Random Early Detection) and how does it prevent TCP global synchronization?
opts: A network intrusion detection algorithm

A queue management algorithm that randomly drops packets from heavier traffic classes before queues fill up; prevents TCP global synchronization (where all flows back off simultaneously causing bandwidth waste)
A wireless roaming detection algorithm
A routing redundancy protocol
ndc.expl: TCP global synchronization: when a queue fills and tail-drops many packets, multiple TCP flows simultaneously detect loss and all back off, causing bandwidth collapse. WRED probabilistically drops packets from less-preferred classes before queue fills, so only some flows back off at a time, maintaining smooth throughput.
ndc.takeaway: WRED = Probabilistic early drops prevent TCP global sync; only some flows back off (not all)

ndc.q: What is CoS (Class of Service) and how does it relate to DSCP?
opts: CoS and DSCP are identical

CoS is a 3-bit field in the 802.1Q VLAN tag (Layer 2) for traffic prioritization on LAN switches; DSCP is a 6-bit field in the IP header (Layer 3) for WAN QoS; they serve similar purposes at different layers
DSCP replaces CoS completely
CoS is only for wireless networks
ndc.expl: CoS (Class of Service) uses the 3-bit PCP field in 802.1Q Ethernet frames for Layer 2 QoS on switches (values 0-7). DSCP uses 6 bits in the IP ToS byte for Layer 3 QoS on routers (values 0-63). At Layer 2/3 boundaries, mappings between CoS and DSCP are configured.
ndc.takeaway: CoS = 3-bit in 802.1Q (Layer 2, switches); DSCP = 6-bit in IP header (Layer 3, routers); mapped at boundaries

ndc.q: What are the types of Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS)?
opts: Only network-based IPS exists
Network-based IPS (NIPS, inline in network), Host-based IPS (HIPS, agent on endpoint), Wireless IPS (WIPS, monitors 802.11 traffic), and Network Behavior Analysis (NBA, analyzes flow data)
Signature-based only
Cloud-based IPS only
ndc.expl: IPS types: NIPS (Network IPS) — inline between network segments, inspects all passing traffic. HIPS (Host IPS) — agent on endpoint, monitors system calls and file access. WIPS (Wireless IPS) — detects rogue APs and wireless attacks. NBA — analyzes NetFlow/IPFIX for behavioral anomalies.
ndc.takeaway: IPS types: NIPS (network inline) + HIPS (host agent) + WIPS (wireless) + NBA (flow analysis)

ndc.q: What is a Host-based IPS (HIPS) and what events does it monitor?
opts: A network IPS deployed on servers only

Software on an endpoint monitoring: system calls, file system changes, registry modifications, network connections, and process execution; blocks suspicious activities based on policy and behavioral analysis
A host-based firewall
A SIEM agent
ndc.expl: HIPS monitors endpoint activities: system call sequences (detect exploit patterns), file system modifications (detect rootkits), registry changes (detect persistence), network connections (detect C2), and process execution (detect injection). Can block suspicious actions unlike HIDS which only alerts.
ndc.takeaway: HIPS = Endpoint agent; monitors syscalls, file changes, registry, processes; blocks suspicious activity

ndc.q: What is inline IPS failover mode and what is the security trade-off?
opts: IPS never fails

Fail-open: IPS failure allows all traffic to pass (availability preserved, security lost); Fail-closed: IPS failure blocks all traffic (security preserved, availability lost); choice depends on business criticality
IPS should always fail-open
Fail-closed is always preferred
ndc.expl: Fail-open vs fail-closed is a critical IPS design decision. Fail-open (bypass on failure): traffic continues flowing but unprotected — chosen for availability-critical paths. Fail-closed (block on failure): traffic stops — chosen for high-security environments where breach is more costly than downtime. Most production IPS use fail-open with monitoring.
ndc.takeaway: IPS fail modes: Fail-open (traffic passes, no security); Fail-closed (blocks all traffic); choose by risk tolerance

ndc.q: What is the limitation of IPS for encrypted traffic?
opts: IPS works perfectly on encrypted traffic

Without SSL/TLS inspection, IPS cannot inspect encrypted payloads — malware and exploits hidden in HTTPS traffic bypass IPS signatures; requires SSL decryption capability which adds latency and complexity
IPS can always decrypt traffic
Encryption is not a limitation for IPS
ndc.expl: Encrypted traffic hides payload from IPS signature matching. Without TLS decryption, IPS only sees encrypted blobs and cannot detect malware, exploit payloads, or C2 traffic hidden in HTTPS. TLS inspection adds complexity (certificate management) and latency (decrypt-inspect-re-encrypt pipeline).
ndc.takeaway: IPS limitation: Cannot inspect encrypted traffic without TLS decryption; adds latency and cert management

ndc.q: What is IPS false positive impact in inline deployment?
opts: False positives have no impact

A false positive incorrectly blocks legitimate traffic — in inline IPS this means disruption of business applications, user complaints, and loss of productivity; requires careful tuning before full inline deployment
False positives only affect test environments
IPS false positives only trigger alerts not blocking
ndc.expl: Inline IPS false positives block legitimate traffic, disrupting business operations. A signature incorrectly matching SAP database traffic would break ERP operations company-wide. This is why IPS tuning in monitor mode before enabling blocking is essential, and why exception lists and sensitivity tuning are critical.
ndc.takeaway: IPS false positive = Blocks legitimate traffic; disrupts business; tune in monitor mode before enabling inline blocking

ndc.q: What is the IPS performance limitation and how is it addressed?
opts: IPS has no performance impact

IPS adds latency (packet must be inspected before forwarding) and reduces throughput; complex rules, SSL decryption, and full DPI significantly degrade throughput; addressed by ASIC hardware acceleration and throughput sizing
IPS improves network performance
Only SSL decryption causes performance issues
ndc.expl: IPS latency impact: simple signature matching <1ms; complex DPI + SSL decryption adds 5-20ms per packet. Throughput: software IPS may handle 1-10 Gbps; hardware ASIC-based IPS handles 100+ Gbps. Solution: right-size IPS for expected traffic volume + use hardware acceleration for encryption.
ndc.takeaway: IPS latency: <1ms simple, 5-20ms with SSL DPI; address by ASIC hardware acceleration + right-sizing

ndc.q: What is signature update frequency for IPS and why is it critical?
opts: Signatures are updated annually

IPS signatures must be updated frequently (daily or more) as new threats emerge constantly; outdated signatures cannot detect recent exploits and malware families; most IPS vendors provide automatic update feeds
Weekly updates are sufficient
Signature updates are optional
ndc.expl: New exploits and malware emerge daily. IPS signatures (CVE-based exploit signatures, malware C2 patterns, new attack techniques) must be updated frequently to remain effective. Automatic signature updates from vendor feeds (Emerging Threats, Cisco Talos, Palo Alto Threat Vault) are essential.
ndc.takeaway: IPS signatures = Update daily or automatically; outdated signatures miss recent exploits and malware

ndc.q: What is virtual patching in the context of IPS?
opts: Applying patches virtually in a VM
Using IPS signatures to detect and block exploitation attempts against a known vulnerability before the actual software patch can be applied; protects systems during the patching window
A virtualization feature
A cloud patch management technique
ndc.expl: Virtual patching: when a vulnerability is disclosed (e.g., Log4Shell), IPS vendors rapidly publish signatures detecting exploitation attempts. Organizations deploy the IPS signature immediately, blocking attacks while the actual vendor patch is tested, approved, and deployed (which may take days/weeks).
ndc.takeaway: Virtual patching = IPS signature blocks exploits of known vuln before actual patch is applied

ndc.q: What is an incident ticket and what information should it contain?
opts: A purchase ticket for security software

A structured record of a security incident containing: incident ID, detection time, affected systems, severity/priority, description of events, responder actions, status updates, and resolution details
A firewall configuration ticket
A help desk ticket for IT issues
ndc.expl: An incident ticket documents the full lifecycle of a security incident: unique ID, timestamps (detected, confirmed, contained, resolved), severity (P1-P4), affected systems and data, description of attack, timeline of responder actions, current status, and post-resolution summary. Maintained in ITSM/SIEM.
ndc.takeaway: Incident ticket = ID + timestamps + severity + affected systems + timeline of actions + resolution

ndc.q: What should a security incident executive report contain?
opts: Full technical details of every packet captured

High-level summary: what happened (non-technical), business impact (financial, reputational, regulatory), number of affected users/systems, current status, immediate actions taken, and recommendations — without technical jargon
Only financial impact numbers
Only technical remediation steps
ndc.expl: Executive security reports translate technical incidents into business terms: what happened (simple description), business impact (# customers affected, data exposed, downtime cost), regulatory implications (GDPR notification required?), current status (contained/resolved), and strategic recommendations (investment needed).
ndc.takeaway: Executive IR report = Non-technical: business impact + affected users + regulatory + actions + recommendations

ndc.q: What is the legal requirement for breach notification under GDPR?
opts: No notification required

Organizations must notify the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach; affected individuals must be notified if there is high risk to their rights and freedoms
Notification must happen within 30 days
Notification is only required for healthcare data
ndc.expl: GDPR Article 33: data processors must notify the controller immediately; controllers must notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware. Article 34: individuals must be notified if the breach is likely to result in high risk to their rights and freedoms. Failure = up to €10M or 2% global turnover.
ndc.takeaway: GDPR breach notification: Supervisory authority within 72 hours; individuals if high risk; max €10M fine

ndc.q: What is IOC (Indicator of Compromise) sharing and why is it important?
opts: A financial indicator for security investments

Sharing threat intelligence (malicious IPs, domains, file hashes, Snort rules) between organizations via STIX/TAXII, ISACs, and platforms like MISP so defenders industry-wide benefit from one organization's discovery
Sharing vulnerability disclosures
Sharing audit findings between auditors
ndc.expl: IOC sharing allows organizations to benefit from each other's threat discoveries. When one organization detects a new malware sample (file hash, C2 IP), sharing it via STIX/TAXII feeds, ISACs (Information Sharing and Analysis Centers), or MISP enables others to pre-emptively block the same threat.
ndc.takeaway: IOC sharing = Share malicious IPs/hashes/domains via STIX/TAXII/MISP; industry-wide threat defense

ndc.q: What is a security metrics program and what should it measure?
opts: Measuring network bandwidth utilization

Quantitative measurement of security program effectiveness: mean time to detect (MTTD), mean time to respond (MTTR), vulnerability remediation SLAs, patch compliance rate, phishing simulation click rate, security awareness training completion
Measuring hardware performance
Measuring compliance audit scores only
ndc.expl: Security metrics quantify program effectiveness and enable improvement. Key metrics: MTTD (how long to detect threats), MTTR (how long to respond/contain), vulnerability age (time from disclosure to patch), patch compliance rate, phishing click rate (awareness training effectiveness), and SOC alert false positive rate.
ndc.takeaway: Security metrics: MTTD, MTTR, vuln age, patch compliance, phishing click rate, false positive rate

ndc.q: What is the purpose of the traceroute (tracert) command?
opts: To test DNS resolution
To discover the path packets take through the network and measure latency at each hop using TTL manipulation and ICMP Time Exceeded messages
To test web server connectivity
To scan open ports
ndc.expl: Traceroute sends packets with incrementing TTL (1, 2, 3...). Each router decrements TTL; when TTL reaches 0, the router discards the packet and sends ICMP Time Exceeded back to source. Source records each router's IP and RTT. Reveals network path and identifies slow/failing hops.
ndc.takeaway: Traceroute = TTL manipulation reveals network path and per-hop latency to destination

ndc.q: What is the significance of the 127.0.0.1 IP address?
opts: The default gateway address
The loopback address representing the local host; used for testing the local network stack without sending packets to the network; also accessible as 'localhost'
A broadcast address
A private IP range address
ndc.expl: 127.0.0.1 (loopback) refers to the local machine itself. Traffic to 127.0.0.1 never leaves the host's network stack. Used for testing (is the local TCP/IP stack working?), local service communication, and development. The entire 127.0.0.0/8 range is reserved for loopback.
ndc.takeaway: 127.0.0.1 = Loopback address (localhost); never leaves host; tests local TCP/IP stack

ndc.q: What is the purpose of a network firewall's stateful inspection for ICMP?
opts: ICMP is always allowed through firewalls
Stateful firewalls track ICMP echo request/reply pairs; when internal host sends ping (echo request), the firewall creates state allowing the echo reply; prevents unsolicited ICMP from external hosts
ICMP does not go through firewalls
Stateful inspection only applies to TCP
ndc.expl: ICMP has no port numbers or connection state like TCP. Stateful firewalls handle ICMP by tracking request/reply pairs: outbound Echo Request creates state allowing inbound Echo Reply from same IP. Prevents unsolicited inbound ICMP (ping floods, ping scanning) without blocking legitimate ping responses.
ndc.takeaway: Stateful ICMP = Track echo request, allow only matching echo reply; block unsolicited inbound ICMP

ndc.q: What is BGP and what is its role in internet routing?
opts: A routing protocol for internal networks

BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the routing protocol that connects autonomous systems (ISPs, large organizations) on the internet; it exchanges reachability information and selects paths based on policies, not just shortest path
A DNS routing protocol
A load balancing protocol
ndc.expl: BGP is the internet's inter-domain routing protocol. It exchanges network reachability information between autonomous systems (ASes). Unlike OSPF (shortest path), BGP selects routes based on policy attributes (AS path length, local preference, MED, community). 800,000+ routes in the global routing table.
ndc.takeaway: BGP = Internet inter-AS routing; policy-based (AS path, local pref) not just shortest path

ndc.q: What is a network intrusion and how does it differ from an attempt?
opts: They are identical events
An intrusion is successful unauthorized access that compromised a system; an intrusion attempt (attack) is a failed try to gain unauthorized access — IDS alerts on both, but response priority differs
An attempt is more serious than an intrusion
Intrusion only refers to physical access
ndc.expl: An intrusion means the attacker successfully gained unauthorized access to a system or data. An intrusion attempt (or attack) means the attacker tried but failed (blocked by firewall, IDS, or authentication). Both should be investigated, but confirmed intrusions require incident response escalation.
ndc.takeaway: Intrusion = Successful unauthorized access; Intrusion attempt = Failed attack; both need investigation

ndc.q: What is defense-in-depth and name its layers?
opts: A single strong security control

Multiple overlapping security layers: Physical security → Network perimeter (firewall, IPS) → Network interior (segmentation, VLAN) → Host (endpoint protection, HIPS) → Application (WAF, code review) → Data (encryption, DLP)
Three security layers maximum
Network perimeter security only
ndc.expl: Defense-in-depth layers: 1) Physical (guards, locks, cameras), 2) Perimeter (FW, IPS, DMZ), 3) Network interior (VLANs, internal FW), 4) Host (antivirus, HIPS, patching), 5) Application (WAF, SDLC, code review), 6) Data (encryption, DLP, access control), 7) Policies/People (training, procedures).
ndc.takeaway: DiD layers: Physical → Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data → Policies/People

ndc.q: What are the three types of authentication factors?
opts: Username, password, email
Something you know (password/PIN), something you have (token/smart card), something you are (biometric/fingerprint); MFA combines two or more factors
Password, security question, backup code
ID card, password, supervisor approval
ndc.expl: Authentication factors: 1) Knowledge factor: something you know (password, PIN, security question). 2) Possession factor: something you have (hardware token, smart card, authenticator app, OTP). 3) Inherence factor: something you are (fingerprint, retina, face, voice). MFA = 2+ different factor types.
ndc.takeaway: Auth factors: Know (password) + Have (token/OTP) + Are (biometric); MFA = 2+ different types

ndc.q: What is the difference between authorization and access control?
opts: They are identical concepts
Authorization is the process of determining what a user is permitted to do; access control is the mechanism enforcing authorization decisions (ACLs, RBAC, ABAC)
Authorization is for networks; access control is for systems
Access control is stronger than authorization
ndc.expl: Authorization determines what actions/resources an authenticated user is permitted (policy decision). Access control is the technical mechanism enforcing those decisions: ACLs (network), file permissions, RBAC (role-based), ABAC (attribute-based). Authorization is the policy; access control is the enforcement.
ndc.takeaway: Authorization = What you can do (policy decision); Access control = Mechanism enforcing it (ACL, RBAC)

ndc.q: What is a honeypot vs a honeytoken?
opts: They are the same thing

A honeypot is a decoy system attracting attackers; a honeytoken is a fake data item (credential, document, API key) placed in systems that when accessed, alerts on compromise — more lightweight than a full honeypot
Honeytokens are physical devices
Honeypots are software-only solutions
ndc.expl: Honeypots are full decoy systems (servers, services) attracting attackers. Honeytokens are lightweight decoy data: fake credentials that trigger alerts when used, canary documents that phone home when opened, fake API keys in source code that alert when called, or canary email addresses seeded in data breaches.
ndc.takeaway: Honeypot = Decoy system; Honeytoken = Decoy data (fake credential, canary document, canary API key)

ndc.q: What is the purpose of logging in security?
opts: Logging slows systems so is avoided

Logging records system events, user actions, and security events; enables incident investigation, forensic analysis, compliance demonstration, anomaly detection, and accountability (who did what when)
Logging only matters for compliance
Logging is only for web servers
ndc.expl: Security logging serves multiple purposes: incident response (what happened?), forensic investigation (who did what?), compliance (GDPR, PCI-DSS require audit trails), anomaly detection (SIEM correlation), and accountability (non-repudiation). Log all: authentication, authorization decisions, admin actions, and security events.
ndc.takeaway: Logging = Investigation + forensics + compliance + anomaly detection + accountability (who, what, when)

ndc.q: What is a packet flood attack and what are common types?
opts: A single large packet attack
Sending massive volumes of packets to overwhelm target network/host resources; types: UDP flood, ICMP flood (ping flood), SYN flood, HTTP flood (Layer 7)
A targeted malware attack
A social engineering attack
ndc.expl: Packet flood attacks overwhelm targets with high packet rates: UDP flood (random UDP to random ports, exhausting resources handling port unreachable), ICMP flood (ping flood), SYN flood (half-open connections), HTTP flood (Layer 7 resource exhaustion). Mitigated by rate limiting, BCP38, and scrubbing.
ndc.takeaway: Packet floods: UDP flood, ICMP flood, SYN flood, HTTP flood; mitigated by rate limiting + scrubbing

ndc.q: What is network reconnaissance and what tools/techniques are used?
opts: Mapping physical network infrastructure
Intelligence gathering about targets before an attack: port scanning (Nmap), banner grabbing, OSINT (WHOIS, Shodan, LinkedIn), DNS enumeration (dig, dnsenum), and traceroute for network mapping
Physical security assessment
Network performance measurement
ndc.expl: Reconnaissance (Kill Chain phase 1): passive (OSINT: WHOIS, Shodan, LinkedIn, certificate transparency) and active (port scanning with Nmap, banner grabbing, DNS zone transfer attempts, traceroute, vulnerability scanning). Detected by IDS signatures for scanning patterns and threat intelligence.
ndc.takeaway: Recon: Passive (OSINT: WHOIS/Shodan/LinkedIn) + Active (Nmap, banner grab, DNS enum) = attack phase 1

ndc.q: What is a brute force attack and what defenses exist?
opts: A physical attack on network hardware

Systematically trying all possible password combinations; defended by: account lockout policies, rate limiting login attempts, strong password complexity, MFA (renders brute force useless even with correct password), and CAPTCHA
A type of DoS attack
A social engineering technique
ndc.expl: Brute force tries all password combinations (dictionary attack tries common words). Defenses: account lockout (lock after N failures), progressive delays, CAPTCHA, MFA (even correct password needs second factor), strong passwords (long+complex = exponentially more combinations), and alerting on failed login patterns.
ndc.takeaway: Brute force defense: Account lockout + rate limiting + CAPTCHA + MFA + strong passwords

ndc.q: What is session hijacking and how is it prevented?
opts: Taking over an administrator's physical session

An attacker stealing or forging a session token after authentication to impersonate an authenticated user without needing credentials; prevented by: HTTPS, short session timeouts, secure+httponly cookie flags, token rotation
A type of SQL injection
A physical access attack
ndc.expl: Session hijacking steals an authenticated session token (via XSS, sniffing, MitM, predictable tokens). Prevention: HTTPS only (prevents sniffing), Secure cookie flag (HTTPS-only transmission), HttpOnly flag (blocks JS access/XSS theft), session timeout, token rotation on privilege change.
ndc.takeaway: Session hijacking = Steal session token; prevent with HTTPS + Secure/HttpOnly cookies + short timeout

ndc.q: What is a watering hole attack?
opts: An attack targeting water treatment facilities
An attacker compromising websites frequently visited by the target organization's employees; victims are infected when they visit the compromised (but legitimate) site — targeted and stealthy
A supply chain attack
A direct server attack
ndc.expl: Watering hole attacks compromise websites the target community frequently visits (industry forums, news sites, vendor portals). When victims browse the compromised site, malware is delivered. Difficult to detect because the site itself is legitimate. Targets specific industries/organizations.
ndc.takeaway: Watering hole = Compromise site victims regularly visit; malware delivered to targeted community

ndc.q: What is network segmentation and micro-segmentation?
opts: Segmentation is physical; micro-segmentation is virtual

Network segmentation divides the network into broad zones (DMZ, internal, OT); micro-segmentation creates fine-grained policy between individual workloads (even same VLAN), implemented via software-defined networking or NGFW
Micro-segmentation is less secure
They are identical techniques
ndc.expl: Network segmentation creates macro-level zones. Micro-segmentation goes further: defines policy between individual VMs or containers within the same zone, preventing lateral movement even within a segment. Implemented via NSX (VMware), Illumio, or NGFW per-VM policy. Defeats east-west attack movement.
ndc.takeaway: Segmentation = Broad zones; Micro-segmentation = Per-workload policy within zones; defeats east-west movement

ndc.q: What is the OSI model and what are its seven layers?
opts: A 5-layer networking model
Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application (Layer 1-7); each layer provides services to the layer above using protocols specific to that layer
A 4-layer model like TCP/IP
A hardware architecture model
ndc.expl: OSI 7 layers (bottom to top): 1-Physical (bits, cable, NIC), 2-Data Link (MAC, Ethernet, frames), 3-Network (IP, routing), 4-Transport (TCP/UDP, ports), 5-Session (session management), 6-Presentation (encryption, encoding, SSL), 7-Application (HTTP, DNS, SMTP, application protocols).
ndc.takeaway: OSI: Physical→DataLink→Network→Transport→Session→Presentation→Application (Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away)

ndc.q: What is a man-in-the-middle attack on HTTPS and how does HSTS prevent it?
opts: MitM on HTTPS is impossible

Attacker downgrades HTTPS to HTTP (SSL stripping) or presents a forged certificate; HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) prevents SSL stripping by telling browsers to always use HTTPS for a domain, rejecting any HTTP connections
HSTS encrypts HTTP traffic
HSTS is a certificate type
ndc.expl: SSL stripping MitM: attacker between client and server converts HTTPS to HTTP (client sees HTTP, attacker communicates HTTPS with server). HSTS header tells browsers to only ever connect via HTTPS to a domain (with max-age). HSTS preload lists bake this into browsers. Prevents SSL stripping.
ndc.takeaway: HSTS = Browser always uses HTTPS for domain; prevents SSL stripping MitM; HSTS preload baked in browser

ndc.q: What is a proxy server and how is a forward proxy different from a reverse proxy?
opts: They are identical

Forward proxy: sits between internal clients and internet — clients send requests to proxy which forwards to servers (used for content filtering, anonymity, caching); Reverse proxy: sits in front of servers — clients connect to proxy which forwards to backend servers (used for load balancing, WAF, SSL termination)
Forward proxy is for incoming traffic
Reverse proxy is used by clients for anonymity
ndc.expl: Forward proxy: clients use it to access the internet (explicit or transparent). Benefits: web filtering, caching, anonymity, access control. Reverse proxy: external clients connect to it (unaware it's a proxy) to reach backend servers. Benefits: load balancing, SSL termination, WAF, DDoS protection.
ndc.takeaway: Forward proxy = Client→Proxy→Internet (filtering, caching); Reverse proxy = Client→Proxy→Backend (LB, WAF)

ndc.q: What is the difference between vulnerability, exploit, and payload in the context of cyberattacks?
opts: They are synonymous terms

Vulnerability = weakness in software/system; Exploit = code/technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability to gain access; Payload = the malicious action executed after exploitation (malware installation, data theft, ransomware)
Exploit and payload are the same
Vulnerability refers to network weakness only
ndc.expl: Attack chain: Vulnerability (flaw in Apache log4j) → Exploit (Log4Shell attack code that triggers the vulnerability) → Payload (malicious code downloaded and executed, e.g., Cobalt Strike beacon, cryptominer). Metasploit separates exploits and payloads as independent components.
ndc.takeaway: Vulnerability = Weakness; Exploit = Weapon triggering it; Payload = Malicious action after entry

ndc.q: What is a rainbow table attack and how is salting a defense?
opts: A visual attack on network infrastructure

A precomputed table of password hashes used to reverse hash-to-password lookups; salting prevents it by adding a unique random value (salt) to each password before hashing, making precomputed tables useless
A brute force attack variant
A network packet timing attack
ndc.expl: Rainbow tables precompute hash-to-plaintext mappings for common passwords. Attacker looks up a stolen hash and finds the password instantly. Salting adds a unique random value to each password before hashing (store: salt+hash). Even identical passwords produce different hashes, making precomputed tables useless.
ndc.takeaway: Rainbow table = Precomputed hash lookup; Salt = Random value per password preventing precomputed attacks

ndc.q: What is port knocking and how is it used as a security control?
opts: Knocking on physical server ports
A firewall technique where a specific sequence of closed port connection attempts (knock sequence) opens a firewall rule; hides services from port scanners and unauthorized access
A type of port scanning
A DDoS protection technique
ndc.expl: Port knocking: a client sends connection attempts to a pre-agreed sequence of closed ports (e.g., 1001, 2002, 3003). The firewall detects the sequence and temporarily opens the real service port (e.g., SSH). The service is invisible to normal port scanners, reducing attack surface.
ndc.takeaway: Port knocking = Secret knock sequence on closed ports temporarily opens real service; hides from scanners

ndc.q: What is the difference between a stateless and stateful packet filter in handling FTP?
opts: Both handle FTP identically

Stateless filters require static rules for all FTP data ports (active mode port 20, passive mode random ports); stateful filters with FTP ALG track the PORT/PASV command to dynamically allow data connections without static rules
FTP does not pass through firewalls
Stateless filters are better for FTP
ndc.expl: FTP uses port 21 (control) + dynamic data port (active: port 20; passive: random high port). Stateless firewall must open all high ports for passive FTP (security risk). Stateful FW with FTP ALG inspects PORT/PASV command, learns the negotiated data port, and creates a dynamic state entry only for that connection.
ndc.takeaway: FTP stateful ALG = Reads PORT/PASV to dynamically allow only the negotiated data port; no static high-port rules

ndc.q: What is an IDS/IPS bypass using packet fragmentation?
opts: Breaking the network with large packets

Splitting attack payload across multiple IP fragments so each fragment looks benign; if IDS doesn't reassemble fragments before inspection, it misses the attack; modern IDS reassembles fragments before matching signatures
A DDoS technique using fragments
Fragmenting legitimate traffic for performance
ndc.expl: Fragmentation evasion: attacker splits malicious payload (e.g., Snort rule content match target) across IP fragments. If IDS inspects each fragment independently without reassembly, no single fragment matches the signature. Modern IDS/IPS perform fragment reassembly before signature matching to defeat this.
ndc.takeaway: Fragmentation evasion = Split attack payload across fragments; modern IDS reassembles before matching

ndc.q: What is a DMZ with a screened subnet using two firewalls?
opts: A DMZ configuration with a single firewall

An architecture with an outer firewall between internet and DMZ, and an inner firewall between DMZ and internal network; provides two layers of defense: compromise of outer firewall still requires breaching inner firewall to reach internal network
A cloud-based DMZ architecture
A wireless DMZ configuration
ndc.expl: Dual-firewall DMZ (screened subnet): outer firewall allows internet traffic to DMZ servers only. Inner firewall allows only specific DMZ-to-internal traffic (e.g., DB queries). If attacker compromises a DMZ server, the inner firewall still blocks access to internal network — two independent layers must be breached.
ndc.takeaway: Dual-firewall DMZ: Outer FW (internet→DMZ) + Inner FW (DMZ→internal); two independent breach requirements

ndc.q: What is network behavior analysis (NBA) in IDS/IPS?
opts: A statistical analysis of network equipment performance

Analyzing network flow data (NetFlow, IPFIX, sFlow) to detect anomalies in traffic patterns that indicate attacks (DDoS, scanning, data exfiltration, botnet C2) without signature matching; effective for zero-days
A type of signature-based detection
A wireless network analysis technique
ndc.expl: Network Behavior Analysis (NBA) analyzes flow metadata (source/dest IPs, ports, bytes, timing, frequency) at scale without inspecting packet payloads. Detects: DDoS (sudden traffic surge), port scans (one source to many ports), data exfiltration (large outbound volumes), and botnet beaconing (periodic intervals).
ndc.takeaway: NBA = Flow data analysis for anomalies; detects DDoS, scanning, exfiltration, beaconing without payloads

ndc.q: What is the MITRE ATT&CK technique for credential dumping?
opts: T1110 - Brute Force
T1003 - OS Credential Dumping (accessing LSASS memory, SAM database, or /etc/shadow to extract hashed/plaintext credentials)
T1059 - Command and Scripting Interpreter
T1566 - Phishing
ndc.expl: MITRE ATT&CK T1003 (OS Credential Dumping) covers techniques for extracting credentials from operating systems: LSASS memory dump (Mimikatz), SAM database (Windows local accounts), NTDS.dit (AD database), /etc/shadow (Linux), and credential stores. Used for privilege escalation and lateral movement.
ndc.takeaway: MITRE T1003 = OS Credential Dumping (Mimikatz LSASS, SAM, NTDS.dit, /etc/shadow)

ndc.q: What is a network access control list (ACL) and how is it different from a firewall?
opts: They are identical

Network ACLs on routers/switches are stateless (inspect each packet independently, no connection tracking) and process at line rate; firewalls are typically stateful (track connections) and apply more complex rules; ACLs are first line of defense, firewalls provide deeper inspection
ACLs are more powerful than firewalls
Firewalls cannot perform ACL functions
ndc.expl: Router/switch ACLs are stateless — each packet inspected independently against ordered permit/deny rules at wire speed. Firewalls are typically stateful — track TCP/UDP connection state for more intelligent decisions. ACLs are used for basic coarse filtering; firewalls provide deep inspection and stateful tracking.
ndc.takeaway: Router ACL = Stateless, line-rate coarse filtering; Firewall = Stateful, deep inspection, connection tracking

ndc.q: What is a content delivery network (CDN) and how does it help with DDoS mitigation?
opts: A type of VPN service

A distributed network of servers globally caching and serving content close to users; DDoS mitigation: distributes attack traffic across many PoPs via anycast, absorbs volumetric attacks with massive capacity, and filters at edge before origin sees traffic
A network monitoring service
A backup internet connection service
ndc.expl: CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly) use anycast routing to direct users to nearest PoP. DDoS mitigation: 1) Anycast distributes attack traffic across global PoPs (no single point overwhelmed), 2) Massive capacity (Cloudflare has 100+ Tbps capacity), 3) Edge filtering removes attack traffic before reaching origin servers.
ndc.takeaway: CDN DDoS mitigation: Anycast distribution + massive capacity + edge filtering before origin

ndc.q: What is a network tap and how does it differ from a SPAN port for IDS deployment?
opts: Network taps are software; SPAN is hardware

A network tap is a passive hardware device providing lossless copies of all traffic in both directions; a SPAN port is a switch feature that mirrors traffic but may drop mirrored packets under load; taps are preferred for forensic-grade capture
SPAN ports are more accurate than taps
Taps can only capture one direction
ndc.expl: Physical taps (optical or copper) are passive in-line devices that create an exact copy of all traffic without any possibility of missing packets. SPAN ports are convenient (software-configured, no hardware change) but may drop mirrored traffic when the switch is processing heavily. Forensic analysis requires tap-level accuracy.
ndc.takeaway: Network tap = Passive hardware, lossless, exact copy; SPAN = Switch feature, may drop packets under load

ndc.q: What is the difference between a DMZ and a virtual host in web hosting?
opts: They are the same concept

DMZ is a network architecture concept (subnet between internet and internal network for public services); virtual host is a web server configuration allowing multiple websites on one server using different domain names or IP addresses
Virtual host is a security concept
DMZ requires virtual hosting
ndc.expl: DMZ is a network security zone where public-facing servers are placed, isolated from the internal network by firewalls. Virtual hosting is a web server configuration (nginx/Apache VirtualHost) allowing one server to serve multiple websites. A single physical server in the DMZ can use virtual hosting for multiple domains.
ndc.takeaway: DMZ = Network security zone; Virtual host = Multiple websites on one server (different domains/IPs)

ndc.q: What is a reverse proxy's role in protecting web application servers?
opts: Reverse proxy replaces all security controls

Reverse proxy hides backend server IPs, performs SSL termination, applies WAF rules, enables rate limiting, caches responses, performs load balancing, and provides DDoS protection — all before requests reach backend application servers
Reverse proxy only speeds up websites
Reverse proxy is a type of VPN
ndc.expl: Reverse proxy security functions: hides backend architecture (attackers see only proxy IP), WAF integration (inspect/block malicious requests), rate limiting (prevent brute force and DDoS), SSL offloading (centralized certificate management), load balancing (distribute and absorb load), and caching (reduce backend exposure).
ndc.takeaway: Reverse proxy: Hides backend + WAF + rate limiting + SSL offload + load balance + DDoS protection

ndc.q: What is the purpose of Snort's preprocessors?
opts: To pre-process network hardware configuration

Snort preprocessors normalize and decode protocols before rule matching: HTTP preprocessor (URL normalization, encoding detection), fragmentation preprocessor (reassembly), stream preprocessor (TCP session reassembly, state tracking)
To pre-process Snort rules
To manage Snort performance settings
ndc.expl: Snort preprocessors prepare traffic for signature matching: Stream5 (TCP/UDP session tracking and reassembly), HTTP_Inspect (HTTP normalization, detect encodings), FRAG3 (IP defragmentation), SMTP/FTP/SSH preprocessors (protocol validation). Prevent evasion via encoding, fragmentation, and session manipulation.
ndc.takeaway: Snort preprocessors: Stream5 (TCP reassembly) + FRAG3 (defrag) + HTTP_Inspect (normalize) = prevent evasion

ndc.q: What is security orchestration, automation, and response (SOAR)?
opts: A manual security process tool

A platform that integrates security tools, automates repetitive tasks (alert triage, IOC lookup, response actions), and orchestrates workflows; reduces MTTR by automating playbooks for common incidents
A type of SIEM
A network monitoring platform
ndc.expl: SOAR integrates security tools (SIEM, threat intel, endpoint, firewall) and automates incident response playbooks. Example: SIEM alert triggers SOAR playbook: auto-enrich IOC with threat intel, query endpoint for process info, if confirmed malicious → auto-isolate endpoint, block IP on firewall, create ticket. Reduces MTTR from hours to minutes.
ndc.takeaway: SOAR = Integrate security tools + automate playbooks; reduces MTTR (hours→minutes) for common incidents

ndc.q: What is a threat intelligence platform (TIP)?
opts: A news feed about cybersecurity

A platform aggregating, normalizing, and enriching threat intelligence from multiple sources (OSINT, ISACs, commercial feeds, internal data) to provide actionable intelligence for detection and response
A SIEM dashboard
A vulnerability scanner
ndc.expl: Threat Intelligence Platforms (TIP — e.g., MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali) aggregate IOCs and TTPs from multiple sources, normalize them into common formats (STIX/TAXII), enrich with context (who uses this IOC, what campaigns, severity), and distribute to security controls (firewalls, IDS, SIEM) for automated blocking and detection.
ndc.takeaway: TIP = Aggregate + normalize + enrich threat intel; distribute IOCs to controls (firewall, IDS, SIEM)

ndc.q: What is an egress-only firewall rule and why is it important?
opts: A rule only blocking inbound traffic

A rule controlling what traffic is allowed to LEAVE the network; important for preventing compromised internal systems from communicating with C2 servers, exfiltrating data, or participating in DDoS; often overlooked compared to ingress filtering
A rule that only logs outbound traffic
A rule specific to IPv6 egress
ndc.expl: Egress filtering controls outbound traffic. Without it, compromised internal hosts can freely contact C2 servers, exfiltrate data, and attack external targets. Best practice: egress rules should only permit traffic on specific ports needed for business (80/443/53), from specific IPs, to specific destinations.
ndc.takeaway: Egress filtering = Control outbound traffic; prevents C2 communication, data exfil, and using compromised hosts for DDoS

ndc.q: What is a jump host (bastion server) and how does it improve security?
opts: A server that processes high-volume traffic

A hardened, dedicated server that provides controlled access to critical systems in a secure or isolated network; administrators connect to the jump host first, then from there to target systems; reduces attack surface of critical systems
A type of proxy server
A server for testing security tools
ndc.expl: A jump host (jump server, bastion host) is a hardened server in a restricted network zone. Administrators SSH/RDP to the jump host from the management network, then connect to target systems from there. All admin access is logged, multi-factor authenticated, and audited. Critical systems never directly reachable from user networks.
ndc.takeaway: Jump host = Hardened gateway to admin target systems; all access audited + MFA; reduces direct exposure

ndc.q: What is data loss prevention (DLP) and how does it protect against insider threats?
opts: A backup solution for data

A system identifying, monitoring, and blocking unauthorized transmission of sensitive data (PII, credit cards, IP) via email, USB, web uploads, and printing; detects insider data theft and accidental exposure
A firewall for database protection
An encryption tool
ndc.expl: DLP identifies sensitive data patterns (credit card numbers, SSNs, confidential documents) and enforces policies: alert when emailed externally, block USB upload of sensitive files, prevent web upload to non-business sites. Endpoint DLP monitors local actions; Network DLP inspects traffic. Addresses insider threat and accidental exposure.
ndc.takeaway: DLP = Identifies sensitive data + enforces transmission controls; prevents insider theft and accidental exposure

ndc.q: What is a security operations center (SOC) tier model?
opts: A geographic distribution of SOC analysts

Tier 1 (triage analysts): process initial alerts, categorize, escalate; Tier 2 (incident responders): investigate escalated incidents, contain threats; Tier 3 (threat hunters/senior analysts): proactive hunting, forensics, complex investigations; Tier 4 (management/architects): strategy and tool management
A system for managing SOC shifts
A performance evaluation model
ndc.expl: SOC tiers: T1 analysts monitor SIEM dashboards, triage alerts, close false positives, escalate true positives. T2 analysts investigate confirmed incidents, perform containment and eradication. T3 analysts proactively hunt, perform forensics, develop new detections. T4 (SOC manager/architect) manages the program, team, and technology.
ndc.takeaway: SOC Tiers: T1=Triage+alert, T2=Investigate+contain, T3=Threat hunt+forensics, T4=Management+strategy

ndc.q: What is the principle of zero trust and how does it apply to firewall design?
opts: Firewalls are unnecessary in zero trust

Zero trust eliminates implicit trust based on network location; firewall design shifts from perimeter-only to micro-segmentation, identity-verified access, and continuous monitoring; all traffic (east-west and north-south) is inspected regardless of source network
Zero trust means no firewall rules
Zero trust only applies to cloud environments
ndc.expl: Zero trust firewall design: 1) No implicit trust for internal traffic (east-west inspection required). 2) Identity verification before any resource access (integration with IAM). 3) Least-privilege access policies. 4) Micro-segmentation between workloads. 5) Continuous monitoring and re-verification. Traditional perimeter firewalls alone are insufficient.
ndc.takeaway: Zero trust firewall: No implicit internal trust + east-west inspection + identity-verified + micro-segmentation
